heading: Welcome!
text: Welcome “Scientometrics R: Bibliometrics, Network Science, Research Evaluation”.working guide scientometrics R researchers, librarians, research administrators, data scientists want study scholarly communication quantitatively. walks full pipeline — pulling records OpenAlex, Crossref, PubMed, computing standard indicators (h-index, MNCS, journal metrics), building co-authorship co-citation networks, applying text topic models scientific corpora, packaging results reproducible reports dashboards.book assumes working knowledge R tidyverse level basic statistics. prior scientometric experience needed — early chapters lay conceptual ethical foundations, later chapter starts concrete research question ends callout responsible use grounded Leiden Manifesto DORA.examples run free, open data; Web Science Scopus subscription required. Small sample datasets ship companion R package scientometricsInR; everything else fetched live public APIs. book cover proprietary platforms (InCites, SciVal), manual screening workflows systematic reviews, qualitative sociology science.

heading: 2.3 Conceptual background
text: Scientometrics quantitative study science — growth, structure, productivity, impact. scientists disciplines study natural social world, scientometricians turn lens inward: study system produces scientific knowledge.term sits within family overlapping labels. Bibliometrics, oldest sibling, focuses statistical analysis publications — books, journal articles, metadata authors, citations, keywords. word coined Alan Pritchard 1969 replace earlier “statistical bibliography”. Informetrics broadest umbrella, encompassing quantitative study information transfer, including web analytics, patent analysis, library usage statistics. practice, three terms often used interchangeably, helps remember bibliometrics emphasises document, scientometrics emphasises science, informetrics emphasises information.

heading: 2.3.1 A brief history
text: intellectual roots field trace back least 1955, Eugene Garfield (1955) proposed idea citation index science. Garfield argued citations create “association ideas” — traceable web intellectual influence — indexing links transform scholars discover literature. vision realised Science Citation Index (SCI), launched 1964 Institute Scientific Information (ISI). first time, researchers systematically trace cited across entire scientific literature.years later, Derek de Solla Price gave field quantitative backbone. Little Science, Big Science (Solla Price 1963), Price marshalled data show science growing exponentially — number journals, articles, scientists — growth followed remarkably regular statistical patterns. estimated volume scientific literature doubles approximately every 15 years, regularity striking came known “Price’s Law.” Price’s work established science studied empirical rigour applies phenomena.foundations reinforced earlier statistical regularities. Alfred Lotka (1926) already shown distribution scientific productivity follows power law: small number authors produce majority papers. Samuel Bradford (1934) demonstrated similar pattern journal coverage: core set journals field accounts disproportionate share relevant literature. laws, explore detail next chapter, remain cornerstones scientometric theory.beginnings, scientometrics expanded rapidly latter half twentieth century. h-index (Hirsch 2005), journal impact factors, co-citation networks, science maps became standard tools research evaluation science policy. Landmark declarations Leiden Manifesto (Hicks et al. 2015) DORA (American Society Cell Biology 2012) later established principles responsible use tools, warning mechanical reliance single indicator.

heading: 2.3.2 The modern era
text: Today field undergoing another transformation. launch OpenAlex (Priem et al. 2022) — fully open index 250 million scholarly works, built Crossref, PubMed, institutional repositories, open sources — democratised access raw data powers scientometric research. Researchers longer need expensive subscriptions Web Science Scopus conduct large-scale analyses. book takes advantage openness: every example uses data reader can reproduce free.

heading: 2.3.3 Scope of this book
text: book teaches scientometrics R. covers:Data acquisition — querying OpenAlex, Crossref, PubMed programmatically.Core bibliometrics — computing productivity, impact, citation indicators.Network analysis — building analysing co-authorship, co-citation, keyword co-occurrence networks.Text topic analysis — extracting themes trends titles, abstracts, full texts.Advanced topics — altmetrics, open science, gender equity, retraction analysis, .Production — building reproducible pipelines, dashboards, reports.chapter follows pattern: conceptual background, fully reproducible worked example, diagnostics, limitations, exercises.

heading: 2.4.1 Fetching a sample from OpenAlex
text: search works whose title abstract mentions “scientometrics”, published 2014 2023, draw random sample 500 records.row scholarly work. Key columns include display_name (title), publication_date, cited_by_count, doi, source_display_name (source journal name).
code:
works <- oa_fetch(
  entity = "works",
  search = "scientometrics",
  from_publication_date = "2014-01-01",
  to_publication_date = "2023-12-31",
  options = list(sample = 500, seed = 42)
)

cat(glue("Fetched {nrow(works)} works\n"))#> Fetched 500 works
glimpse(works)#> Rows: 500

heading: 2.4.2 Basic descriptive statistics
text: many works retrieve, citation distribution look like?large gap mean median hallmark citation distributions: papers receive citations small number accumulate many. skewness one robust empirical regularities scientometrics, return theoretical basis 3.3.
code:
works_summary <- works |>
  summarise(
    n_works       = n(),
    doi_coverage  = scales::percent(mean(!is.na(doi))),
    median_cites  = median(cited_by_count, na.rm = TRUE),
    mean_cites    = round(mean(cited_by_count, na.rm = TRUE), 1),
    max_cites     = max(cited_by_count, na.rm = TRUE),
    zero_cites    = sum(cited_by_count == 0)
  )

works_summary#> # A tibble: 1 × 6
#>   n_works doi_coverage median_cites mean_cites max_cites zero_cites
#>     <int> <chr>               <dbl>      <dbl>     <int>      <int>
#> 1     500 89%                     3       15.2       471        152

heading: 2.4.4 Publication trend over time
text:
Figure 2.1: Annual publication counts sampled works related scientometrics (2014–2023).
trend shows growing volume scientometrics-related publications past decade — consistent broader exponential growth science documented Solla Price (1963).
code:
works |>
  mutate(year = year(publication_date)) |>
  count(year) |>
  ggplot(aes(x = year, y = n)) +
  geom_col(fill = palette_sci(1)) +
  labs(
    x = "Publication year",
    y = "Number of works (sampled)"
  ) +
  theme_sci()

heading: 2.4.6 Citation distribution
text:
Figure 2.3: Citation distribution sampled scientometrics works (log-scaled x-axis).
right-skewed citation distribution universal feature scholarly communication. Chapter 2, see connects deeper theoretical regularities: Lotka’s Law, Bradford’s Law, Matthew Effect.
code:
works |>
  filter(cited_by_count > 0) |>
  ggplot(aes(x = cited_by_count)) +
  geom_histogram(bins = 40, fill = palette_sci(1), colour = "white") +
  scale_x_log10() +
  labs(
    x = "Citation count (log scale)",
    y = "Number of works"
  ) +
  theme_sci()

heading: 2.5 Diagnostics and interpretation
text: Even simple descriptive analysis, diagnostic checks essential:Sample vs. population. drew random sample 500 works. publication trend reflects underlying population sampling truly random within date window. OpenAlex’s sample parameter , always verify date range returned results.Source coverage. OpenAlex strongest English-language, peer-reviewed journal articles. Conference proceedings, book chapters, grey literature may underrepresented. “top journals” list reflects OpenAlex indexes, full universe scientometrics publishing.Citation counts snapshots. cited_by_count field reflects current citation total time API query. Older papers time accumulate citations, direct comparisons across years misleading without normalization.
code:
cat(glue("Date range: {min(works$publication_date)} to {max(works$publication_date)}\n"))#> Date range: 2014-01-01 to 2023-12-31
cat(glue("Works with zero citations: {sum(works$cited_by_count == 0)}\n"))#> Works with zero citations: 152
cat(glue("Unique journals: {n_distinct(works$source_display_name, na.rm = TRUE)}\n"))#> Unique journals: 365

heading: 2.7 Limitations and responsible use
text: Descriptive statistics evaluations. Counting publications citations describes activity, quality. high publication count may reflect salami slicing; high citation count may reflect controversy rather excellence.Data source bias. OpenAlex, like databases, systematic coverage gaps. English-language STEM journals overrepresented; social sciences, humanities, non-English scholarship underrepresented. Always report data source known limitations.Ecological fallacy. Aggregate statistics field apply individual researchers. growing field mean every researcher productive.Leiden Manifesto (Hicks et al. 2015) DORA (American Society Cell Biology 2012) provide essential guardrails. Quantitative data support, never replace, expert qualitative judgement.

heading: 2.9 Common pitfalls
text: Confusing scientometrics research evaluation. Scientometrics provides tools studying science; provide verdicts value individual researchers papers.Treating OpenAlex counts ground truth. OpenAlex counts may differ Web Science Scopus due different coverage, deduplication, metadata parsing. Always triangulate precision matters.Ignoring citation window. Comparing raw citation counts across publication years misleading. 2023 paper 5 citations may performing better 2015 paper 20. Field- time-normalised indicators (covered Chapter 10) address .Overgeneralising sample. random sample 500 works useful learning prototyping. Drawing conclusions entire field requires comprehensive corpus.Forgetting set seed. Random samples reproducible set seed R (set.seed()) API call (seed parameter). Without , every render produces different results.

heading: 3.2 Setup
code:
library(tidyverse)
library(openalexR)
library(tidytext)
library(glue)

heading: 3.3 Conceptual background
text: Scientometrics rests small number empirical regularities discovered across decades studying scholarly communication:Lotka’s Law (Lotka 1926) observes number authors producing n papers approximately proportional 1/n². prolific authors generate large share output, researchers publish one two papers.Bradford’s Law (Bradford 1934) describes scattering articles across journals. given topic, small core journals contains roughly one-third relevant articles; larger zone contains next third; much larger periphery final third. successive zone contains roughly number articles geometrically journals.Zipf’s Law states frequency word inversely proportional rank. scientific text, handful terms dominate vast majority appear rarely.Price’s Law (Solla Price 1963) generalises observations: cumulative output science grows exponentially, doubling roughly every 10–15 years. Price also noted square root contributors produce half contributions.Matthew Effect (Merton 1968) — named Gospel Matthew (“, shall given”) — describes cumulative advantage science. Well-known researchers attract disproportionate credit resources, amplifying initial differences visibility. mechanism helps explain skewed distributions observed Lotka Price.

text: use corpus works journal Scientometrics demonstrate law.

heading: 3.4.1 Fetching the data
code:
works <- oa_fetch(
  entity = "works",
  primary_location.source.id = "S148561398",
  from_publication_date = "2015-01-01",
  to_publication_date = "2023-12-31",
  options = list(sample = 500, seed = 42)
)

heading: 3.4.2 Lotka’s Law
text:
unnest authors count number papers per author sample.
Figure 3.1: Author productivity distribution. dashed line shows inverse-square law predicted Lotka.
data approximate inverse-square pattern, though real distributions often deviate exact exponent 2.
code:
author_prod <- works |>
  select(id, authorships) |>
  unnest(authorships, names_sep = "_") |>
  count(authorships_display_name, name = "n_papers") |>
  count(n_papers, name = "n_authors") |>
  arrange(n_papers)

author_prod#> # A tibble: 8 × 2
#>   n_papers n_authors
#>      <int>     <int>
#> 1        1      1107
#> 2        2       100
#> 3        3        23
#> 4        4         8
#> 5        5         2
#> 6        6         2
#> 7        9         1
#> 8       11         1
lotka_c <- author_prod$n_authors[1]

author_prod |>
  ggplot(aes(x = n_papers, y = n_authors)) +
  geom_point(colour = palette_sci(1), size = 2) +
  geom_line(
    aes(y = lotka_c / n_papers^2),
    linetype = "dashed", colour = "grey40"
  ) +
  scale_x_log10() +
  scale_y_log10() +
  labs(x = "Papers per author", y = "Number of authors") +
  theme_sci()

heading: 3.4.3 Bradford’s Law
text:
count many articles journal source contributes (across broader query).
Figure 3.2: Bradford plot: cumulative articles vs. log journal rank.
code:
broad_works <- oa_fetch(
  entity = "works",
  search = "bibliometrics",
  from_publication_date = "2020-01-01",
  to_publication_date = "2023-12-31",
  options = list(sample = 1000, seed = 42)
)

journal_counts <- broad_works |>
  filter(!is.na(source_display_name)) |>
  count(source_display_name, name = "articles", sort = TRUE) |>
  mutate(
    cum_articles = cumsum(articles),
    rank = row_number()
  )
journal_counts |>
  ggplot(aes(x = rank, y = cum_articles)) +
  geom_line(colour = palette_sci(1), linewidth = 1) +
  scale_x_log10() +
  labs(x = "Journal rank (log scale)", y = "Cumulative articles") +
  theme_sci()

heading: 3.4.4 Zipf’s Law
text:
extract title words examine frequency distribution.
Figure 3.3: Word frequency vs. rank article titles, approximating Zipf’s law.
code:
word_freq <- works |>
  select(display_name) |>
  unnest_tokens(word, display_name, token = "words") |>
  anti_join(tidytext::get_stopwords(), by = "word") |>
  count(word, sort = TRUE) |>
  mutate(rank = row_number())
word_freq |>
  head(200) |>
  ggplot(aes(x = rank, y = n)) +
  geom_point(colour = palette_sci(1), size = 1, alpha = 0.7) +
  scale_x_log10() +
  scale_y_log10() +
  labs(x = "Rank", y = "Frequency") +
  theme_sci()

heading: 3.4.5 Price’s growth law

heading: 3.5 Diagnostics and interpretation
text: laws empirical regularities, physical laws. describe approximate patterns:Lotka’s exponent often exactly 2; values 1.5 3.5 common depending field time window.Bradford zones sensitive define subject scope.Zipf’s law holds best common words; tail deviates.Price’s exponential growth eventually saturates — science grow faster economy indefinitely.value laws predictive precision insight skewed distributions norm, exception, scholarly communication.

heading: 3.7 Limitations and responsible use
text: laws describe aggregate patterns, individual behaviour. researcher publishing one paper “less productive” evaluative sense — Lotka’s law statistical observation, performance standard.Matthew Effect (Merton 1968) mechanism amplifies inequality. Recognising prompt caution using cumulative metrics (like h-index) inherit reinforce bias.Power-law fitting notoriously difficult. Many distributions “look” power-law log-log plot better described lognormal stretched exponential models. Rigorous fitting requires maximum-likelihood methods goodness--fit tests (Hicks et al. 2015).

heading: 3.9 Common pitfalls
text: Eyeballing log-log plots. straight line log-log plot prove power law. Use formal statistical tests.Confusing description prescription. “authors publish papers” mean publish .Ignoring field differences. exponent Lotka’s law scattering pattern Bradford’s law vary substantially across disciplines.Assuming stationarity. patterns can shift time publication norms change (e.g., rise mega-journals).

heading: 4.3 Conceptual background
text: Metrics shape behaviour. number used judge researchers, departments, journals, people adjust actions improve number — regardless whether number actually captures meant measure. insight, often called Goodhart’s Law, intellectual foundation responsible metrics movement: “measure becomes target, ceases good measure” (Goodhart 1984).

heading: 4.3.1 The Leiden Manifesto
text: 2015, Hicks et al. (2015) published Leiden Manifesto Research Metrics — ten principles distilled decades experience bibliometric practice. principles :Quantitative evaluation support qualitative, expert assessment. Metrics inform human judgement, never replace .Measure performance research missions institution, group, researcher. everyone pursues goals; evaluation criteria must reflect .Protect excellence locally relevant research. Research published regional languages addressing local problems must penalised lower citation rates.Keep data collection analytical processes open, transparent, simple. Black-box indicators erode trust prevent scrutiny.Allow evaluated verify data analysis. Researchers able inspect challenge data used judge .Account variation field publication citation practices. Citation rates mathematics molecular biology differ order magnitude; raw counts compared across fields.Base assessment individual researchers qualitative judgement portfolio. single number (h-index) capture breadth researcher’s contribution.Avoid misplaced concreteness false precision. Three decimal places make flawed indicator meaningful.Recognize systemic effects assessment indicators. Indicators reshape system measure. reward publication volume, incentivise salami slicing.Scrutinize indicators regularly update . research landscape evolves; indicators must evolve .

heading: 4.3.2 The San Francisco Declaration (DORA)
text: San Francisco Declaration Research Assessment (American Society Cell Biology 2012) emerged 2012 Annual Meeting American Society Cell Biology. central demand straightforward: use journal-level metrics — especially Journal Impact Factor — proxy quality individual research articles. DORA’s key commitments include:funders institutions: evaluate research merits, journal published. Use article-level metrics qualitative indicators.publishers: reduce emphasis Journal Impact Factor promotional tool. Make article-level metrics (downloads, citations, altmetrics) available.researchers: challenge evaluation practices rely journal-based metrics. serving hiring promotion committees, assess content papers, appeared.2026, DORA signed 3,000 organisations 20,000 individuals. Many major funders — including European Commission, Wellcome Trust, Dutch Research Council (NWO) — formally adopted DORA principles.

heading: 4.3.3 The Metric Tide
text: UK’s independent review metrics research assessment, Metric Tide (Wilsdon et al. 2015), reinforced principles systematic evidence base. report concluded metric can substitute expert peer review, recommended UK research system adopt framework “responsible metrics” built five dimensions: robustness, humility, transparency, diversity, reflexivity.

heading: 4.3.4 Goodhart’s Law in practice
text: metrics attached career consequences, incentive game becomes powerful (Goodhart 1984). Common forms metric manipulation include:Self-citation inflation: authors systematically cite prior work boost citation counts, even citations intellectually necessary.Citation cartels: informal agreements among groups authors journals cite ’s work, inflating citation counts reciprocally.Citation stacking: journals encourage require authors add citations recently published articles journal, artificially increasing Impact Factor.Salami slicing: splitting single study maximum number “least publishable units” inflate publication counts.practices merely hypothetical. Clarivate repeatedly suspended journals Journal Citation Reports citation stacking, several high-profile cases self-citation manipulation led editorial sanctions.

heading: 4.3.5 Publish or perish
text: systemic root metric gaming “publish perish” culture dominates academic career structures worldwide. hiring, promotion, tenure decisions driven publication volume citation counts, researchers face relentless pressure produce papers, chase high-impact journals, avoid risky long-term projects. consequences include:Reduced risk-taking: researchers avoid novel interdisciplinary questions might produce quick, high-citation publications.Replication crisis: pressure publish positive results contributes publication bias irreproducible findings.Mental health harms: relentless pressure produce linked elevated rates anxiety, burnout, attrition academia.Erosion research quality: volume rewarded substance, incentive structure favours quantity expense depth rigour.dynamics make responsible use metrics merely technical concern ethical imperative.

heading: 4.4.2 Fetching works and computing self-citations
text: author, retrieve sample recent works examine many citations come author’s papers. OpenAlex provides cited_by_count work level allows us query author’s works directly.
code:
# For a manageable demonstration, pick 10 authors
authors_sample <- authors |>
  slice_sample(n = min(10, nrow(authors)))

# Function to estimate self-citation rate for one author
estimate_self_citation <- function(author_id, author_name) {
  # Fetch up to 50 recent works by this author
  works <- tryCatch(
    oa_fetch(
      entity = "works",
      author.id = author_id,
      from_publication_date = "2018-01-01",
      options = list(sample = 50, seed = 42)
    ),
    error = function(e) tibble()
  )

  if (is.null(works) || nrow(works) == 0) {
    return(tibble(
      author_id = author_id,
      author_name = author_name,
      n_works = 0L,
      total_citations = NA_integer_,
      self_citations = NA_integer_,
      self_cite_rate = NA_real_
    ))
  }

  # Count references that point to the same author's works
  # We look at the referenced_works and check which are by the same author
  total_cites <- sum(works$cited_by_count, na.rm = TRUE)

  # For self-citation, we check which of the works' referenced_works

  # are also in the author's own works list
  own_work_ids <- works$id

  self_cite_count <- works |>
    pull(referenced_works) |>
    map_int(~ sum(.x %in% own_work_ids, na.rm = TRUE))

  tibble(
    author_id = author_id,
    author_name = author_name,
    n_works = nrow(works),
    total_references = sum(map_int(works$referenced_works, length)),
    self_references = sum(self_cite_count),
    self_cite_rate = if_else(
      sum(map_int(works$referenced_works, length)) > 0,
      sum(self_cite_count) / sum(map_int(works$referenced_works, length)),
      NA_real_
    )
  )
}

self_cite_results <- map2_dfr(
  authors_sample$id,
  authors_sample$display_name,
  estimate_self_citation
)

heading: 4.4.3 Summarizing results
code:
self_cite_results |>
  filter(!is.na(self_cite_rate)) |>
  arrange(desc(self_cite_rate)) |>
  mutate(self_cite_rate = scales::percent(self_cite_rate, accuracy = 0.1)) |>
  gt() |>
  tab_header(
    title = "Self-citation rates among sampled scientometrics authors",
    subtitle = "Based on references in up to 50 recent works per author"
  ) |>
  cols_label(
    author_name = "Author",
    n_works = "Works sampled",
    total_references = "Total references",
    self_references = "Self-references",
    self_cite_rate = "Self-citation rate"
  ) |>
  cols_hide(author_id)

heading: 4.4.4 Visualization
text: Figure 4.1: Distribution self-citation rates across sampled scientometrics authors. dashed line marks sample median.
code:
self_cite_clean <- self_cite_results |>
  filter(!is.na(self_cite_rate))

if (nrow(self_cite_clean) > 0) {
  ggplot(self_cite_clean, aes(x = self_cite_rate)) +
    geom_histogram(
      binwidth = 0.02,
      fill = palette_sci(1),
      colour = "white"
    ) +
    geom_vline(
      xintercept = median(self_cite_clean$self_cite_rate, na.rm = TRUE),
      linetype = "dashed", linewidth = 0.8
    ) +
    scale_x_continuous(labels = scales::percent_format()) +
    labs(
      x = "Self-citation rate (proportion of references to own works)",
      y = "Number of authors"
    ) +
    theme_sci()
}

heading: 4.5 Diagnostics and interpretation
text: Self-citation rates vary enormously across disciplines, career stages, research specialities. interpreting results, keep following mind:Baseline rates differ field. small, specialised fields, self-citation often necessary researchers work topic. Self-citation rates 15–25% common niche subfields , , indicate manipulation.Career stage matters. Senior researchers large publication portfolios naturally accumulate self-citations simply prior work build .estimate lower bound. count self-references works appear author’s sampled works OpenAlex. Works outside sample window (2018) works indexed OpenAlex missed, true self-citation rate likely higher.Context essential. self-citation rate 30% might perfectly reasonable researcher building coherent long-term programme, rate 10% still problematic citations strategically placed inflate particular metric.Never use self-citation rates alone accuse individuals misconduct. Leiden Manifesto insists, quantitative indicators must support — replace — qualitative judgement (Hicks et al. 2015).

heading: 4.7 Limitations and responsible use
text: Self-citation analysis misconduct detection. High self-citation rates many legitimate explanations. Presenting self-citation analysis evidence gaming without qualitative context form metric misuse.Coverage gaps matter. OpenAlex index scholarly works. Authors publish venues well covered OpenAlex incomplete reference lists, biasing self-citation estimates.Author disambiguation imperfect. OpenAlex uses algorithmic author disambiguation. Common names may conflated (merging two people one profile) split (one person appearing multiple profiles), distorting individual-level statistics.Snapshot bias. analysis uses sample works particular time window. Conclusions generalised beyond window without analysis.Leiden Manifesto applies . Every principle 4.3 applies analysis. must transparent methods, allow evaluated verify data, avoid false precision (Hicks et al. 2015).

heading: 4.9 Common pitfalls
text: Equating self-citation misconduct. Self-citation normal part scientific writing. excessive, strategically motivated self-citation problematic — even , boundary context-dependent.Using Journal Impact Factor judge individual articles. precisely DORA warns . journal’s average citation rate says nothing single paper journal (American Society Cell Biology 2012).Ignoring field differences comparing metrics. citation count 20 exceptional mathematics unremarkable biomedical research. cross-field comparison requires field normalisation (Waltman 2016).Treating indicators objective truth. bibliometric indicators constructed measures specific assumptions, coverage limitations, known biases. Present approximations, facts.Forgetting Goodhart’s Law. propose new indicator evaluation purposes, consider rational actors respond measured . Every metric creates incentives, incentives may align goals good research (Goodhart 1984).

heading: 5.1 Learning objectives
text: completing chapter, able :Describe coverage, access model, key strengths twelve major bibliographic data sourcesSelect appropriate data source (combination sources) given research questionCompare metadata completeness across sources set publicationsEvaluate trade-offs proprietary open data sources reproducible researchRetrieve compare metadata OpenAlex Crossref using R

heading: 5.2 Setup
code:
library(tidyverse)
library(openalexR)
library(rcrossref)
library(glue)
library(gt)

set.seed(20260509)

source(here::here("R", "api_helpers.R"))
source(here::here("R", "utils.R"))
source(here::here("R", "sci_palette.R"))

heading: 5.3 Conceptual background
text: Every bibliometric analysis fundamentally constrained data source feeds . database choose determines publications visible, citations counted, metadata fields available. Visser et al. (2021) demonstrated large-scale comparison, major bibliographic databases differ substantially coverage, differences affecting research outcomes measurable ways. Similarly, Singh et al. (2021) showed journal coverage varies considerably across Web Science, Scopus, Dimensions, database exhibiting distinct disciplinary strengths.choice data source merely technical decision — methodological one direct consequences validity results. citation analysis conducted Web Science produce different numbers analysis Scopus, Dimensions, OpenAlex, database indexes different set publications computes citations different corpus. Researchers must therefore understand differences report transparently, emphasised Leiden Manifesto’s fourth principle: keep data collection analytical processes open, transparent, simple (Hicks et al. 2015).landscape bibliographic data sources shifted dramatically recent years. traditional duopoly Web Science Scopus challenged open alternatives — notably OpenAlex (Priem et al. 2022), provides free, unrestricted access 250 million scholarly works. Crossref serves backbone registration agency DOIs provides rich metadata 150 million records. Discipline-specific databases like PubMed, ADS, arXiv serve targeted communities deep, curated coverage.book, adopt OpenAlex primary data source fully open, freely accessible via API without authentication, provides coverage comparable proprietary alternatives. However, responsible bibliometric practice often requires consulting multiple sources cross-validate findings fill coverage gaps.

heading: 5.3.1 Twelve data sources at a glance
text: summarise twelve major sources. subsequent sections examine detail, comparison table provides structured overview.Web Science (WoS). Maintained Clarivate, WoS oldest established citation index, originating Eugene Garfield’s Science Citation Index (Garfield 1955). covers approximately 90 million records 21,000 journals, citation data back 1900. Access requires institutional subscription. strengths curated journal selection, long historical coverage, Journal Citation Reports (JCR). main limitations cost, restricted API access, lower coverage non-English literature, social sciences, humanities.Scopus. Elsevier’s Scopus indexes 94 million records approximately 27,000 journals, broader coverage non-English titles conference proceedings WoS. provides citation metrics (CiteScore, SJR, SNIP) well-documented API. Like WoS, requires institutional subscription.Dimensions. Operated Digital Science, Dimensions indexes 140 million publications including journal articles, books, book chapters, preprints, clinical trials, patents, policy documents. offers free tier limited functionality paid tier full API access. distinctive strength linking publications grants, patents, clinical trials, policy documents.OpenAlex. successor discontinued Microsoft Academic Graph, OpenAlex fully open, freely accessible index 250 million scholarly works (Priem et al. 2022). aggregates data Crossref, PubMed, institutional repositories, open sources. API requires authentication (though polite-pool email recommended). OpenAlex provides author disambiguation, institutional affiliation data, concept tagging. primary limitations metadata quality (algorithmically parsed, manually curated) less granular subject classification curated databases.Lens. Operated Cambia, Lens provides free access 300 million scholarly works 160 million patent records. aggregates data Crossref, PubMed, Microsoft Academic, patent offices. unique strength integrated patent-paper linkage, making valuable technology-transfer innovation studies. API access available via free institutional plan.Crossref. Crossref DOI registration agency scholarly publications, covering 150 million records. citation index per se — provides metadata deposited publishers, including titles, authors, abstracts, reference lists, licensing information. REST API freely accessible. Crossref’s strengths comprehensive DOI coverage detailed reference metadata; limitation metadata quality depends entirely publishers deposit.PubMed/MEDLINE. Maintained US National Library Medicine, PubMed indexes 36 million records biomedical life sciences. key strengths curated MeSH (Medical Subject Headings) indexing, structured abstracts, deep coverage clinical biomedical literature. PubMed provide citation counts can paired citation data sources.Google Scholar. Google Scholar provides broadest coverage scholarly search engine, indexing journal articles, conference papers, theses, books, patents, grey literature. However, provides API, bulk data export, structured metadata. useful discovery “Cited ” feature suitable systematic bibliometric analysis.Semantic Scholar. Operated Allen Institute AI, Semantic Scholar indexes 200 million papers provides free API rich metadata including algorithmically extracted entities, TLDR summaries, citation context. particularly strong computer science, biomedical, neuroscience literature.NASA/ADS (Astrophysics Data System). ADS curated database 16 million records astronomy, astrophysics, physics. provides excellent citation tracking, full-text search, well-documented API. astronomical research, ADS gold standard.arXiv. original preprint server, arXiv hosts 2.5 million preprints physics, mathematics, computer science, quantitative biology, quantitative finance, statistics, electrical engineering. provide citation data directly records indexed databases. API provides metadata access.bioRxiv/medRxiv. preprint servers biology health sciences, hosting 300,000 preprints. provide API access metadata full text indexed OpenAlex, Dimensions, aggregators.

text: now compare OpenAlex Crossref return set DOIs. practical illustration metadata completeness varies across sources.

heading: 5.4.1 Selecting DOIs
text: start small set well-known scientometrics papers whose DOIs know.
code:
dois <- c(
  "10.1038/520429a",            # Hicks et al. 2015, Leiden Manifesto
  "10.1073/pnas.0507655102",    # Hirsch 2005, h-index
  "10.1007/s11192-006-0144-7",  # Egghe 2006, g-index
  "10.1016/j.joi.2017.08.007",  # Aria & Cuccurullo 2017, bibliometrix
  "10.1126/science.122.3159.108", # Garfield 1955, citation indexes
  "10.48550/arXiv.2205.01833",  # Priem et al. 2022, OpenAlex
  "10.1162/qss_a_00112",        # Visser et al. 2021, database comparison
  "10.1007/s11192-021-03948-5"  # Singh et al. 2021, journal coverage
)

heading: 5.4.2 Fetching from OpenAlex
code:
oa_results <- oa_fetch(
  entity = "works",
  doi = dois
)

oa_meta <- oa_results |>
  transmute(
    doi = doi,
    source = "OpenAlex",
    title = display_name,
    year = publication_year,
    cited_by = cited_by_count,
    has_abstract = !is.na(abstract),
    type = type
  )

glimpse(oa_meta)#> Rows: 9
#> Columns: 7

heading: 5.4.3 Fetching from Crossref
code:
cr_results <- cr_works(dois = dois)

cr_meta <- cr_results$data |>
  transmute(
    doi = doi,
    source = "Crossref",
    title = title,
    year = as.integer(
      coalesce(
        substr(issued, 1, 4),
        substr(deposited, 1, 4)
      )
    ),
    cited_by = as.integer(is.referenced.by.count),
    has_abstract = !is.na(abstract),
    type = type
  )

glimpse(cr_meta)#> Rows: 7
#> Columns: 7
#> $ doi          <chr> "10.1038/520429a", "10.1073/pnas.0507655102", "10.1007/s1…
#> $ source       <chr> "Crossref", "Crossref", "Crossref", "Crossref", "Crossref…
#> $ title        <chr> "Bibliometrics: The Leiden Manifesto for research metrics…
#> $ year         <int> 2015, 2005, 2006, 2017, 1955, 2021, 2021
#> $ cited_by     <int> 1929, 8485, 1811, 11622, 1749, 650, 1558

#> $ type         <chr> "journal-article", "journal-article", "journal-article", …

heading: 5.4.4 Comparing metadata completeness
code:
comparison <- bind_rows(oa_meta, cr_meta) |>
  mutate(short_doi = str_extract(doi, "[^/]+/[^/]+$"))

comparison_wide <- comparison |>
  select(short_doi, source, year, cited_by, has_abstract, type) |>
  pivot_wider(
    names_from = source,
    values_from = c(year, cited_by, has_abstract, type),
    names_glue = "{source}_{.value}"
  )

comparison_wide |>
  gt() |>
  tab_header(
    title = "Metadata comparison: OpenAlex vs. Crossref",
    subtitle = "Same DOIs queried from both sources"
  ) |>
  cols_label(
    short_doi = "DOI (short)",
    OpenAlex_year = "OA Year",
    Crossref_year = "CR Year",
    OpenAlex_cited_by = "OA Citations",
    Crossref_cited_by = "CR Citations",
    OpenAlex_has_abstract = "OA Abstract?",
    Crossref_has_abstract = "CR Abstract?",
    OpenAlex_type = "OA Type",
    Crossref_type = "CR Type"
  )

heading: 5.4.5 Visualization: citation count comparison
text: Figure 5.1: Citation counts reported OpenAlex vs. Crossref set DOIs. Points diagonal indicate higher counts OpenAlex.
code:
cite_compare <- comparison |>
  select(short_doi, source, cited_by) |>
  pivot_wider(names_from = source, values_from = cited_by, values_fn = first)

if (nrow(cite_compare) > 0 && all(c("OpenAlex", "Crossref") %in% names(cite_compare))) {
  max_cite <- max(c(cite_compare$OpenAlex, cite_compare$Crossref), na.rm = TRUE)

  ggplot(cite_compare, aes(x = Crossref, y = OpenAlex)) +
    geom_abline(slope = 1, intercept = 0, linetype = "dashed", colour = "grey50") +
    geom_point(size = 3, colour = palette_sci(1)) +
    geom_text(
      aes(label = short_doi),
      size = 2.5, nudge_y = max_cite * 0.03, check_overlap = TRUE
    ) +
    scale_x_continuous(labels = scales::comma_format()) +
    scale_y_continuous(labels = scales::comma_format()) +
    coord_equal(xlim = c(0, max_cite * 1.1), ylim = c(0, max_cite * 1.1)) +
    labs(x = "Crossref citation count", y = "OpenAlex citation count") +
    theme_sci()
}

heading: 5.5 Diagnostics and interpretation
text: comparing metadata across sources, consider following diagnostic checks:DOI match rate. DOIs may resolve every database. Check many queried DOIs returned results source. DOI missing one source, reveals coverage gap.Citation count discrepancies. Citation counts almost always differ sources database counts citations different corpus. OpenAlex typically reports higher counts Crossref OpenAlex tracks citations across full 250M-record corpus, whereas Crossref counts “-referenced-” links deposited publishers.Abstract availability. Crossref abstracts depend publisher deposits often absent, especially older publications. OpenAlex aggregates abstracts multiple sources generally better coverage.Publication year. Minor discrepancies (e.g., online-first vs. print dates) common. Check whether analysis sensitive exact year assigned.Type classification. Crossref OpenAlex use different type taxonomies. record classified “journal-article” Crossref might appear “article” OpenAlex. Harmonise types analysis.key takeaway single source complete definitive. Cross-validation — fetching records multiple sources — hallmark rigorous bibliometric practice.

heading: 5.7 Limitations and responsible use
text: database complete. Every source coverage gaps — language, discipline, publication type, geography. Analyses based single source systematically miss literature. Always report source used known biases (Hicks et al. 2015).Coverage equal quality. larger database necessarily better. Google Scholar broadest coverage also highest noise level. Curated databases like WoS PubMed trade coverage precision.Proprietary sources limit reproducibility. Analyses based Web Science Scopus independently reproduced researchers without institutional access. fully reproducible work, prefer open sources like OpenAlex, Crossref, PubMed (Priem et al. 2022).Metadata quality variable. Even within single source, metadata quality varies publisher, discipline, publication date. Older records often sparser metadata. Always inspect clean data analysis.Citation counts comparable across sources. database counts citations different corpus, citation counts OpenAlex, Scopus, WoS interchangeable. Never mix citation counts different sources single analysis without explicit justification documentation (Visser et al. 2021).

heading: 5.9 Common pitfalls
text: Assuming one source enough. Researchers often default single database (typically WoS Scopus) without evaluating whether covers field adequately. Social sciences, humanities, non-English literatures systematically underrepresented WoS (Singh et al. 2021).Comparing citation counts across databases. Stating paper “500 citations” meaningless without specifying source. Different databases report different numbers paper.Ignoring access restrictions reproducibility claims. analysis uses Scopus data, researchers need Scopus access reproduce . State explicitly consider providing OpenAlex-based alternatives.Treating Google Scholar systematic source. Google Scholar API, structured export, quality control indexes. useful discovery systematic bibliometric analysis.Conflating preprints peer-reviewed articles. Sources like arXiv bioRxiv index preprints may later published journals. corpus includes preprints published versions without deduplication, double-count works.

library(tidyverse)

heading: 6.3 Conceptual background
text: Bibliometric research begins data. quality completeness bibliographic corpus determines validity every downstream analysis — citation counts network maps topic models.Historically, Web Science Scopus dominated data sources scientometric research. require institutional subscriptions impose restrictions bulk data use. launch OpenAlex 2022 changed landscape fundamentally (Priem et al. 2022). OpenAlex fully open index 250 million scholarly works, built Crossref, PubMed, institutional repositories, open sources. provides free REST API authentication required, making ideal backbone reproducible bibliometric research.OpenAlex organises data around five entity types: works (publications), authors, sources (journals, repositories), institutions, topics (hierarchical subject tags). entity unique OpenAlex ID can queried via filters metadata fields publication date, citation count, open-access status, institutional affiliation.openalexR package provides tidy R interface OpenAlex API. handles pagination, rate limiting, result parsing, returning clean tibbles ready analysis. metadata covered OpenAlex — reference lists funding information — rcrossref provides access Crossref’s extensive metadata registry, pubmedR covers biomedical literature indexed PubMed/MEDLINE.

heading: 6.4 Worked example
text: build small corpus recent scientometrics research, demonstrating full acquisition--clean-data pipeline.

heading: 6.4.1 Querying works from OpenAlex
text: core function oa_fetch(). search works published journal Scientometrics 2020 2023, sampling 200 records manageable demonstration.result tibble row work columns include id, display_name, publication_date, cited_by_count, doi, nested list-columns authors concepts.
code:
works <- oa_fetch(
  entity = "works",
  primary_location.source.id = "S148561398",
  from_publication_date = "2020-01-01",
  to_publication_date = "2023-12-31",
  options = list(sample = 200, seed = 42)
)

heading: 6.4.4 Deduplication by DOI
text: Real-world corpora often contain duplicates merging results multiple queries sources. dedupe_by_doi() function companion package handles cleanly.
code:
works_with_dupes <- bind_rows(works, works[1:10, ])
cat(glue("Before dedup: {nrow(works_with_dupes)} rows\n\n"))#> Before dedup: 210 rows
works_clean <- dedupe_by_doi(works_with_dupes)
cat(glue("After dedup:  {nrow(works_clean)} rows\n"))#> After dedup:  200 rows

heading: 6.4.5 Caching for reproducible builds
text: fetch_openalex() wrapper R/api_helpers.R automatically caches API responses disk. subsequent calls arguments, reads cache instead hitting API.
code:
works_cached <- fetch_openalex(
  entity = "works",
  primary_location.source.id = "S148561398",
  from_publication_date = "2023-01-01",
  to_publication_date = "2023-06-30",
  options = list(sample = 50, seed = 42),
  cache_dir = "_freeze/openalex_cache",
  cache_days = 30
)

nrow(works_cached)#> [1] 50

heading: 6.4.6 Complementary sources: Crossref
text: detailed metadata (reference lists, funders, licenses), Crossref invaluable.
code:
# Requires network access — cached output used in book builds
cr_work <- rcrossref::cr_works(doi = "10.1007/s11192-017-2300-7")
cr_work$data |>
  select(title, container.title, deposited, is.referenced.by.count)

heading: 6.4.7 Visualization
text: Figure 6.1: Annual publication counts sampled Scientometrics articles (2020–2023).
code:
works |>
  mutate(year = year(publication_date)) |>
  count(year) |>
  ggplot(aes(x = year, y = n)) +
  geom_col(fill = palette_sci(1)) +
  labs(x = "Publication year", y = "Number of works") +
  theme_sci()

heading: 6.5 Diagnostics and interpretation
text: working API data, always verify:Completeness: Compare result count API’s reported meta$count confirm received expected records. sampling, count reflects sample, full set.Coverage dates: Check min(works$publication_date) max(works$publication_date) confirm date filters applied correctly.DOI presence: works DOIs. Check mean(!.na(works$doi)) understand DOI coverage rate corpus.Nested columns: Author concept columns list-columns. Use tidyr::unnest() carefully inspect NULL entries unnesting.
code:
cat(glue("Total works: {nrow(works)}\n"))#> Total works: 200
cat(glue("DOI coverage: {scales::percent(mean(!is.na(works$doi)))}\n"))#> DOI coverage: 100%
cat(glue("Date range: {min(works$publication_date)} to {max(works$publication_date)}\n"))#> Date range: 2020-01-02 to 2023-12-16

heading: 6.7 Limitations and responsible use
text: Coverage bias: OpenAlex strongest English-language, peer-reviewed journal articles. Conference proceedings, books, non-English literature may underrepresented. Always report data source known coverage limitations.Metadata quality: Author names affiliations parsed algorithmically may contain errors, especially names diacritics non-Latin scripts.Temporal lag: OpenAlex updates continuously may lag behind primary databases days weeks. recent publications may missing.API rate limits: OpenAlex allows 10 requests per second unauthenticated users (100/s polite pool email). Respect limits avoid throttled.Reproducibility: API results can change OpenAlex updates data. Always cache results document date data collection reproducibility (Hicks et al. 2015).

heading: 6.9 Common pitfalls
text: caching: Running oa_fetch() directly code chunks without caching means every quarto render hits API, producing different results time risking rate limits.Ignoring pagination: oa_fetch() handles pagination automatically, large queries (>10,000 works) can time . Use filters narrow query use cursor-based pagination.Confusing sampling filtering: options = list(sample = 200) returns random sample full result set, first 200 records. useful demonstrations exhaustive analyses.Treating OpenAlex IDs permanent: generally stable, OpenAlex IDs can change entities merged. Use DOIs primary identifier works.Merging sources without deduplication: combining results OpenAlex Crossref, records may overlap. Always deduplicate DOI analysis.

heading: 7.1 Learning objectives
text: completing chapter, able :Parse BibTeX files using bib2df RefManageRImport Web Science plain-text Scopus CSV exports via bibliometrixStandardize field names across formats unified schemaDiagnose common parsing failures missing fields

heading: 7.2 Setup
code:
library(tidyverse)
library(bib2df)
library(RefManageR)
library(bibliometrix)
library(glue)

set.seed(20260509)

source(here::here("R", "sci_palette.R"))

heading: 7.3 Conceptual background
text: bibliometric data comes API. Researchers frequently begin files exported database search interfaces: BibTeX (.bib) Google Scholar Zotero, RIS (.ris) Scopus PubMed, plain text Web Science, CSV Scopus. format encodes information — authors, title, year, journal, DOI — different structures.bibliometrix package (Aria Cuccurullo 2017) provides convert2df(), versatile function reads multiple export formats returns standardized data frame. BibTeX specifically, bib2df RefManageR offer complementary parsing capabilities. key challenge parsing per se standardization: ensuring author names, journal titles, identifiers represented consistently regardless source format.

heading: 7.4.1 Parsing a BibTeX string
text: construct small BibTeX string inline demonstrate parsing without external files.
code:
bib_text <- '
@article{smith2023,
  author = {Smith, John A. and Doe, Jane B.},
  title = {Advances in Bibliometric Methods},
  journal = {Journal of Informetrics},
  year = {2023},
  volume = {17},
  pages = {101--115},
  doi = {10.1234/example.2023}
}
@article{chen2022,
  author = {Chen, Wei and Li, Xiao},
  title = {Citation Networks in Computer Science},
  journal = {Scientometrics},
  year = {2022},
  volume = {127},
  pages = {3201--3220},
  doi = {10.1234/example.2022}
}
'

tmp_bib <- tempfile(fileext = ".bib")
writeLines(bib_text, tmp_bib)

bib_df <- bib2df(tmp_bib)
glimpse(bib_df)#> Rows: 2
#> Columns: 27
#> $ CATEGORY     <chr> "ARTICLE", "ARTICLE"
#> $ BIBTEXKEY    <chr> "smith2023", "chen2022"

#> $ HOWPUBLISHED <chr> NA, NA
#> $ INSTITUTION  <chr> NA, NA

heading: 7.4.4 Standardizing field names
code:
standardize_bib <- function(df) {
  df |>
    transmute(
      title = TITLE,
      authors = AUTHOR,
      year = as.integer(YEAR),
      journal = JOURNAL,
      doi = DOI
    )
}

bib_df |> standardize_bib()#> # A tibble: 2 × 5
#>   title                                 authors    year journal            doi  
#>   <chr>                                 <list>    <int> <chr>              <chr>
#> 1 Advances in Bibliometric Methods      <chr [2]>  2023 Journal of Inform… 10.1…
#> 2 Citation Networks in Computer Science <chr [2]>  2022 Scientometrics     10.1…

heading: 7.4.5 Visualization
text: Figure 7.1: Field completeness parsed BibTeX records.
code:
completeness <- bib_df |>
  summarise(across(everything(), ~ mean(!is.na(.)))) |>
  pivot_longer(everything(), names_to = "field", values_to = "present") |>
  filter(present > 0) |>
  mutate(field = fct_reorder(field, present))

ggplot(completeness, aes(x = present, y = field)) +
  geom_col(fill = palette_sci(1)) +
  scale_x_continuous(labels = scales::percent) +
  labs(x = "Completeness", y = NULL) +
  theme_sci()

heading: 7.5 Diagnostics and interpretation
text: parsing, always check:Row count: number records match export? Missing entries may indicate parsing errors.Field completeness: fields populated? Abstracts keywords often missing BibTeX exports.Author format: Names may appear “Last, First” “First Last” depending source. Standardize analysis.Encoding: Non-ASCII characters (diacritics, CJK) can corrupt parsing. Ensure UTF-8 encoding.

heading: 7.7 Limitations and responsible use
text: Export files snapshots: reflect database state export time become stale immediately.Field mapping formats imperfect. formats lack fields (RIS standard abstract tag across databases).Parsing errors silent — always validate record counts spot-check metadata source (Hicks et al. 2015).

heading: 7.9 Common pitfalls
text: Encoding mismatches: Exporting Latin-1 reading UTF-8 (vice versa) corrupts names titles.Truncated exports: Many databases cap exports 500 1,000 records per file. Large corpora require multiple export batches.Inconsistent author delimiters: BibTeX uses “”, RIS uses newlines, CSV uses semicolons. Parsing must handle .Assuming format extension: .txt file may WoS plain text something else entirely. Check first lines.

heading: 8.1 Learning objectives
text: completing chapter, able :Deduplicate bibliographic records DOI approximate title matchingResolve identifiers across databases (OpenAlex ID, DOI, PMID)Recognize author name disambiguation challenges apply basic heuristicsStandardize institutional affiliations using ROR identifiersSave clean corpus efficient format (Parquet) downstream analysis

heading: 8.2 Setup
code:
library(tidyverse)
library(openalexR)
library(glue)
library(arrow)

set.seed(20260509)

source(here::here("R", "api_helpers.R"))
source(here::here("R", "utils.R"))
source(here::here("R", "sci_palette.R"))

heading: 8.3 Conceptual background
text: Raw bibliographic data messy. Records different sources may describe paper different metadata, author different name variants, institution dozens spelling variations. Building clean, reproducible corpus requires systematic attention three problems:Deduplication. merging data multiple sources, publication may appear multiple times. DOI-based deduplication gold standard, records DOIs. Fuzzy title matching provides fallback error-prone short generic titles.Author disambiguation. “J. Smith” dozens different people. OpenAlex uses machine-learning-based author clustering assign persistent author IDs, errors persist — especially common names authors change institutions (Priem et al. 2022).Affiliation standardization. Institutional names appear countless variants (“MIT”, “Massachusetts Institute Technology”, “Mass. Inst. Tech.”). Research Organization Registry (ROR) provides curated set persistent identifiers research organizations. OpenAlex maps many affiliations ROR IDs, coverage incomplete.

heading: 8.4.1 Fetching raw data
code:
works <- oa_fetch(
  entity = "works",
  search = "bibliometrics",
  from_publication_date = "2021-01-01",
  to_publication_date = "2023-12-31",
  options = list(sample = 300, seed = 42)
)

cat(glue("Raw records: {nrow(works)}\n"))#> Raw records: 300

heading: 8.4.2 DOI-based deduplication
code:
works_deduped <- dedupe_by_doi(works)
cat(glue("After DOI dedup: {nrow(works_deduped)}\n"))#> After DOI dedup: 300
cat(glue("Removed: {nrow(works) - nrow(works_deduped)} duplicates\n"))#> Removed: 0 duplicates

heading: 8.4.3 Fuzzy title deduplication
text: records without DOIs, use approximate string matching.

heading: 8.4.4 Affiliation extraction and ROR mapping
code:
affiliations <- works_deduped |>
  select(id, authorships) |>
  unnest(authorships, names_sep = "_") |>
  unnest(authorships_affiliations, names_sep = "_") |>
  select(work_id = id,
         author_name = authorships_display_name,
         institution = authorships_affiliations_display_name) |>
  filter(!is.na(institution))

top_institutions <- affiliations |>
  count(institution, sort = TRUE) |>
  head(15)

top_institutions#> # A tibble: 15 × 2
#>    institution                                                n
#>    <chr>                                                  <int>
#>  1 Anhui Medical University                                  19
#>  2 Binus University                                          18

heading: 8.4.5 Saving as Parquet
code:
corpus_clean <- works_deduped |>
  select(id, display_name, publication_date, cited_by_count, doi, source_display_name, abstract)

out_path <- here::here("data", "corpus_sample.parquet")
write_parquet(corpus_clean, out_path)
cat(glue("Saved {nrow(corpus_clean)} records to {out_path}\n"))#> Saved 300 records to /home/runner/work/scientometrics-in-r/scientometrics-in-r/data/corpus_sample.parquet

heading: 8.4.6 Visualization
text: Figure 8.1: Top 15 institutions publication count sample corpus.
code:
top_institutions |>
  mutate(institution = fct_reorder(institution, n)) |>
  ggplot(aes(x = n, y = institution)) +
  geom_col(fill = palette_sci(1)) +
  labs(x = "Publications", y = NULL) +
  theme_sci()

heading: 8.5 Diagnostics and interpretation
text: cleaning:Deduplication rate: rate 5% suggests either multi-source merge query returns overlapping result pages.DOI coverage: proportion records DOIs determines effective DOI-based deduplication can .Affiliation coverage: Check fraction author records institutional affiliations. Low coverage limits institutional analysis.Parquet file size: Parquet columnar compressed. 10,000-record corpus typically compresses 1 MB.

heading: 8.7 Limitations and responsible use
text: Fuzzy matching imperfect. Jaro-Winkler similarity can produce false positives (different papers similar titles) false negatives (paper different title versions). Always spot-check.Author disambiguation remains unsolved. automated method achieves perfect accuracy. Report disambiguation method used known error rate.Affiliation data noisy. Even ROR mapping, temporary affiliations, joint appointments, name changes create ambiguity. Never use affiliation data ground truth without validation (Hicks et al. 2015).

heading: 8.9 Common pitfalls
text: Deduplicating merging. Always merge first, deduplicate. Deduplicating source independently misses cross-source duplicates.Trusting DOIs blindly. records incorrect DOIs (typos, test DOIs, placeholder values). Validate sample.Ignoring records without DOIs. Discarding biases corpus toward recent, well-indexed publications.documenting cleaning steps. Every deduplication name-standardization step logged reproducibility.

heading: 8.10 Exercises
text: DOI coverage year. Compute DOI coverage rate publication year sample. trend?DOI coverage year. Compute DOI coverage rate publication year sample. trend?Name variants. Find authors sample appear multiple name spellings. heuristics use merge ?Name variants. Find authors sample appear multiple name spellings. heuristics use merge ?ROR lookup. Use ROR API resolve top 5 institutions corpus official ROR identifiers.ROR lookup. Use ROR API resolve top 5 institutions corpus official ROR identifiers.

heading: 8.11 Solutions
text: Solutions provided 2.11.

heading: 8.12 Further reading
text: Priem et al. (2022) — OpenAlex author disambiguation institutional mapping.Aria Cuccurullo (2017) — bibliometrix data cleaning utilities.Hicks et al. (2015) — Leiden Manifesto; principles 4 5 emphasise data transparency verifiability.

heading: 9.1 Learning objectives
text: completing chapter, able :Extract text PDF files using pdftoolsApply OCR scanned documents tesseractPerform basic text preprocessing (tokenization, stopword removal)Assess full-text analysis adds value metadata-approachesNavigate legal landscape text data mining

heading: 9.2 Setup
code:
library(tidyverse)
library(pdftools)
library(quanteda)
library(glue)

set.seed(20260509)

source(here::here("R", "sci_palette.R"))

heading: 9.3 Conceptual background
text: bibliometric analyses operate metadata: titles, abstracts, keywords, citation links. metadata captures fraction paper’s content. Full-text analysis opens door richer methods: section-level citation context, method extraction, data-availability statements, fine-grained topic modeling.technical pipeline involves three stages: (1) acquisition — obtaining PDF files, subject access rights TDM (text data mining) provisions; (2) extraction — converting PDF content machine-readable text; (3) preprocessing — tokenization, normalization, feature extraction.pdftools wraps Poppler library extract text born-digital PDFs. scanned documents, tesseract provides OCR (optical character recognition). tabulizer (based Tabula) specializes extracting tables PDFs. text preprocessing, quanteda tm offer comprehensive toolkits.Legal frameworks vary jurisdiction. EU Copyright Directive (2019/790) provides TDM exception research. US, TDM generally considered fair use research contexts, publisher terms service may impose additional restrictions.

heading: 9.4.1 Extracting text from a PDF
text: demonstrate programmatically created PDF keep example self-contained.
code:
demo_text <- paste(
  "Title: A Review of Bibliometric Methods",
  "",
  "Abstract: This paper reviews current methods in bibliometrics.",
  "We focus on citation analysis, co-authorship networks, and topic modeling.",
  "",
  "1. Introduction",
  "Bibliometrics has grown rapidly since the work of Garfield (1955).",
  "Modern tools enable large-scale analysis of scholarly communication.",
  "",
  "2. Methods",
  "We surveyed 500 papers published between 2020 and 2023.",
  "Data were obtained from OpenAlex.",
  "",
  "3. Conclusions",
  "The field continues to evolve with open data and new computational methods.",
  sep = "\n"
)

tmp_pdf <- tempfile(fileext = ".pdf")
pdf(tmp_pdf, width = 8.5, height = 11)
plot.new()
text(0.05, 0.95, demo_text, adj = c(0, 1), family = "mono", cex = 0.8)
dev.off()#> png 
#>   2
extracted <- pdf_text(tmp_pdf)
cat(substr(extracted[1], 1, 500))#> Title: A Review of Bibliometric Methods
#> 
#> Abstract: This paper reviews current methods in bibliometrics.
#> We focus on citation analysis, co−authorship networks, and topic modeling.
#> 
#> 1. Introduction
#> Bibliometrics has grown rapidly since the work of Garfield (1955).
#> Modern tools enable large−scale analysis of scholarly communication.
#> 
#> 2. Methods
#> We surveyed 500 papers published between 2020 and 2023.
#> Data were obtained from OpenAlex.
#> 
#> 3. Conclusions
#> The field continues to evolve with open data and n

heading: 9.4.2 Basic text preprocessing
code:
corp <- corpus(extracted)
toks <- tokens(corp, remove_punct = TRUE, remove_numbers = TRUE) |>
  tokens_tolower() |>
  tokens_remove(stopwords("en"))

dfmat <- dfm(toks)
topfeatures(dfmat, 15)#>       methods bibliometrics      analysis             −          data 
#>             4             2             2             2             2 
#>         title        review  bibliometric      abstract         paper 
#>             1             1             1             1             1 
#>       reviews       current         focus      citation            co 
#>             1             1             1             1             1

heading: 9.4.3 Word frequency visualization
text: Figure 9.1: Top 15 words extracted demo PDF preprocessing.
code:
top_words <- topfeatures(dfmat, 15) |>
  enframe(name = "word", value = "count") |>
  mutate(word = fct_reorder(word, count))

ggplot(top_words, aes(x = count, y = word)) +
  geom_col(fill = palette_sci(1)) +
  labs(x = "Frequency", y = NULL) +
  theme_sci()

heading: 9.4.4 OCR with tesseract
code:
# Requires tesseract system library and a scanned PDF
# library(tesseract)
# ocr_text <- ocr(scanned_pdf_path, engine = tesseract("eng"))
# cat(substr(ocr_text, 1, 500))

heading: 9.5 Diagnostics and interpretation
text: Full-text extraction quality varies dramatically:Born-digital PDFs: pdftools produces clean text, column layouts may interleave columns.Scanned PDFs: OCR accuracy depends scan quality, resolution, language. Expect 90–98% character accuracy clean English scans.Figures equations: lost text extraction. expect mathematical notation chart data.Headers footers: Page numbers, journal headers, running titles pollute extracted text. Consider filtering position.

heading: 9.7 Limitations and responsible use
text: Access rights: PDFs legally available TDM. Respect publisher terms licensing. Open-access articles CC licenses generally safe.Extraction errors: Two-column layouts, ligatures, non-standard fonts cause parsing artifacts. Always spot-check extracted text.Scalability: Full-text analysis orders magnitude expensive metadata analysis. Consider whether additional information justifies cost.Bias: Full text available subset publications (typically open access). Analyses based full text may generalise broader literature (Hicks et al. 2015).

heading: 9.9 Common pitfalls
text: Assuming clean extraction. Even pdftools produces artifacts. Always inspect sample extracted text bulk analysis.Skipping preprocessing. Raw extracted text contains headers, page numbers, hyphenated line breaks must cleaned.Mixing OCR digital extraction. PDFs contain digital text scanned images. Detect pages need OCR processing.Ignoring encoding issues. PDFs may encode characters non-standard ways. Check garbled text, especially references sections.

heading: 9.10 Exercises
text: Extract compare. Download open-access PDF. Extract text pdftools. Compare extracted abstract abstract OpenAlex. accurate extraction?Extract compare. Download open-access PDF. Extract text pdftools. Compare extracted abstract abstract OpenAlex. accurate extraction?Section detection. Write function splits extracted PDF text sections based numbered headings (“1. Introduction”, “2. Methods”, etc.).Section detection. Write function splits extracted PDF text sections based numbered headings (“1. Introduction”, “2. Methods”, etc.).Table extraction. tabulizer installed, extract table PDF convert tibble. cleaning steps needed?Table extraction. tabulizer installed, extract table PDF convert tibble. cleaning steps needed?

heading: 9.11 Solutions
text: Solutions provided 2.11.

heading: 9.12 Further reading
text: Aria Cuccurullo (2017) — bibliometrix supports full-text features alongside metadata analysis.Priem et al. (2022) — OpenAlex provides abstracts many works, often sufficient without full-text extraction.

heading: 10.1 Learning objectives
text: completing chapter, able :Import bibliographic data bibliometrix using convert2dfProduce corpus-level summary biblioAnalysis summaryIdentify productive authors, sources, countries datasetConstruct interpret three-field plot (Sankey diagram)Explain use interactive biblioshiny app versus scripted workflowRecognise descriptive statistics precede advanced analysis

heading: 10.2 Setup
code:
library(tidyverse)
library(openalexR)
library(bibliometrix)
library(glue)

set.seed(20260509)

source(here::here("R", "api_helpers.R"))
source(here::here("R", "utils.R"))
source(here::here("R", "sci_palette.R"))

heading: 10.3 Conceptual background
text: computing advanced indicator, responsible bibliometric study begins descriptive analysis: understanding size, growth, composition corpus. many documents per year? journals dominate? countries contribute? questions set stage every subsequent method.bibliometrix package (Aria Cuccurullo 2017) provides integrated R workflow science mapping. companion web app, biblioshiny, wraps functions point--click interface — useful exploration reproducible reporting. Quarto pipeline, prefer scripted calls every number traceable code.Bibliometrix accepts data Scopus, Web Science, Dimensions, PubMed, OpenAlex. convert2df() function normalises heterogeneous export formats common data frame. biblioAnalysis() function computes battery descriptive statistics one call: annual production, author productivity, source rankings, country output, keyword frequencies.Three classical bibliometric laws underpin descriptive stage. Lotka’s law (Lotka 1926) describes inverse-square relationship number authors publication counts. Bradford’s law (Bradford 1934) models articles subject scatter across journals, identifying core, mid-range, peripheral zones. Price’s law (Solla Price 1963) states half contributions come square root total number contributors. empirical regularities provide benchmarks corpus can compared.Descriptive bibliometrics also serves data-quality role. Anomalous spikes annual output may signal duplicate records; suspiciously dominant country may indicate search query biased toward specific language. Inspecting descriptive statistics first line defence analytical errors.

heading: 10.4.1 Acquiring the data
text: fetch sample works Scientometrics journal via OpenAlex convert result bibliometrix-compatible data frame.
code:
works <- oa_fetch(
  entity = "works",
  primary_location.source.id = "S148561398",
  from_publication_date = "2018-01-01",
  to_publication_date = "2023-12-31",
  options = list(sample = 500, seed = 42)
)

nrow(works)#> [1] 500

heading: 10.4.2 Converting to bibliometrix format
code:
bib_df <- oa2bibliometrix(works)

cat(glue("Records: {nrow(bib_df)}\n"))#> Records: 500
cat(glue("Fields: {ncol(bib_df)}\n"))#> Fields: 61

heading: 10.4.3 Running biblioAnalysis
10.4.4 Annual scientific production
text: Figure 10.1: Annual scientific production sampled corpus (2018-2023).
code:
annual <- works |>
  mutate(year = year(publication_date)) |>
  count(year)

ggplot(annual, aes(x = year, y = n)) +
  geom_col(fill = palette_sci(1), width = 0.7) +
  geom_text(aes(label = n), vjust = -0.5, size = 3.5) +
  labs(x = "Publication year", y = "Number of articles") +
  theme_sci()

heading: 10.4.5 Most productive authors
text: Figure 10.2: Top 15 productive authors sample.
code:
author_prod <- works |>
  select(id, authorships) |>
  unnest(authorships, names_sep = "_") |>
  count(authorships_display_name, sort = TRUE) |>
  head(15)

ggplot(author_prod, aes(x = n, y = reorder(authorships_display_name, n))) +
  geom_col(fill = palette_sci(1)) +
  labs(x = "Articles", y = NULL) +
  theme_sci()

heading: 10.4.6 Most productive sources
text: sampled works come single journal example, instead show frequent cited sources references.

top_sources <- works |>

  unnest(referenced_works) |>

cat(glue("Top 10 most-cited reference work IDs:\n"))#> Top 10 most-cited reference work IDs:

heading: 10.4.7 Three-field plot
text: three-field plot (Sankey diagram) links authors, keywords, sources. signature visualisation bibliometrix.
code:
tryCatch(
  threeFieldsPlot(bib_df, fields = c("AU", "DE", "SO_SO"), n = c(8, 8, 8)),
  error = function(e) cat("Three-field plot skipped due to data format incompatibility.\n")
)#> Three-field plot skipped due to data format incompatibility.

heading: 10.5 Diagnostics and interpretation
text: reviewing descriptive output, check following:Completeness: gaps yearly coverage indicate missing data rather genuine drops?Author name variants: person appearing different name forms inflates author counts. OpenAlex’s entity resolution helps, manual inspection remains essential.Lotka’s law fit: author productivity distribution deviates markedly inverse-square law, corpus may small specialised.Bradford zones: multi-journal corpus, check whether small core journals accounts roughly one-third articles, Bradford’s law predicts.

heading: 10.7 Limitations and responsible use
text: Descriptive statistics evaluative. productive author necessarily impactful. Publication counts say nothing quality (Hicks et al. 2015).Sample bias. random sample OpenAlex may represent full population. Always report sample drawn size relative universe.Language database coverage. OpenAlex broader coverage WoS Scopus, still underrepresents non-English-language research certain disciplines (Visser et al. 2021).Name disambiguation. Despite algorithmic clustering, author institutional names can still conflated split. Treat productivity rankings approximate.

heading: 10.9 Common pitfalls
text: Skipping descriptive analysis. Jumping straight network topic analysis without understanding corpus structure often leads misinterpretation.Treating biblioshiny output final. interactive app excellent exploration, figures reproducible. Always script final analysis.Ignoring duplicate records. Bibliographic exports often contain duplicates. Use dedupe_by_doi() similar checks computing statistics.Comparing raw counts across time windows different length. Counting publications 6-year window comparable 3-year window without normalisation.

Modify three-field plot use author countries instead author names left field (use "AU_CO" available). new patterns emerge?Annual growth rate. Compute compound annual growth rate (CAGR) publications sample. field accelerating?Annual growth rate. Compute compound annual growth rate (CAGR) publications sample. field accelerating?

heading: 11.1 Learning objectives
text: completing chapter, able :Compute h-index, g-index, m-quotient vector citation countsExplain difference size-dependent size-independent indicatorsCalculate field-normalized indicators (MNCS, PP(top 10%)) explain normalization mattersArticulate limitations indicator risks misuse, citing Leiden Manifesto

heading: 11.2 Setup
code:
library(tidyverse)
library(openalexR)
library(glue)
library(gt)

set.seed(20260509)

source(here::here("R", "api_helpers.R"))
source(here::here("R", "utils.R"))
source(here::here("R", "sci_palette.R"))

heading: 11.3 Conceptual background
text: Quantitative indicators central research evaluation — among contested tools science policy. h-index, proposed Hirsch (2005), counts number h author’s papers received least h citations. simplicity made wildly popular, also obscures important nuances: conflates productivity impact, insensitive highly cited papers, can increase career (Waltman 2016).g-index (Egghe 2006) addresses insensitivity top-cited papers requiring top g papers together received least g² citations. m-quotient divides h-index career length, offering rough age correction.raw indicators. Comparing across fields meaningless without field normalization. citation count 50 exceptional mathematics ordinary biomedical research. Mean Normalized Citation Score (MNCS) divides paper’s citations world average field publication year (Waltman et al. 2011). PP(top 10%) — proportion entity’s papers top 10% cited field — percentile-based indicator recommended Leiden Ranking.Leiden Manifesto (Hicks et al. 2015) San Francisco Declaration Research Assessment (American Society Cell Biology 2012) caution mechanical use single indicator. Indicators support, replace, qualitative judgement.

heading: 11.4.1 Acquiring sample data
text: fetch sample works citation counts OpenAlex.
code:
works <- oa_fetch(
  entity = "works",
  primary_location.source.id = "S148561398",
  from_publication_date = "2018-01-01",
  to_publication_date = "2023-12-31",
  options = list(sample = 500, seed = 42)
)

works_slim <- works |>
  select(id, display_name, publication_date, cited_by_count, doi) |>
  mutate(year = year(publication_date))

summary(works_slim$cited_by_count)#>    Min. 1st Qu.  Median    Mean 3rd Qu.    Max. 
#>       0       6      13      23      26     669

heading: 11.4.2 Computing the h-index
text: h-index tells us {h} papers sample cited least {h} times.
code:
h <- compute_h_index(works_slim$cited_by_count)
cat(glue("h-index for this corpus: {h}\n"))#> h-index for this corpus: 47

heading: 11.4.3 Computing the g-index
11.4.4 The m-quotient
text: m-quotient normalizes h-index career length (, publication span).
code:
career_years <- as.numeric(
  difftime(max(works_slim$publication_date),
           min(works_slim$publication_date),
           units = "days")
) / 365.25

m_quotient <- h / career_years
cat(glue("m-quotient: {round(m_quotient, 3)} (over {round(career_years, 1)} years)\n"))#> m-quotient: 7.857 (over 6 years)

heading: 11.4.5 Field-normalized indicators: MNCS
text: compute MNCS, need field averages. simulate field means ; real analysis come OpenAlex snapshot reference dataset.MNCS 1.0 means entity performs exactly world average. Values 1.0 indicate -average citation impact.
code:
field_means_by_year <- works_slim |>
  group_by(year) |>
  summarise(field_mean = mean(cited_by_count), .groups = "drop")

works_normalized <- works_slim |>
  left_join(field_means_by_year, by = "year") |>
  mutate(ncs = field_normalize(cited_by_count, field_mean))

mncs <- mean(works_normalized$ncs, na.rm = TRUE)
cat(glue("MNCS: {round(mncs, 3)}\n"))#> MNCS: 1

heading: 11.4.6 PP(top 10%)
code:
works_with_pct <- works_normalized |>
  group_by(year) |>
  mutate(
    pct_rank = percent_rank(cited_by_count),
    is_top10 = pct_rank >= 0.90
  ) |>
  ungroup()

pp_top10 <- mean(works_with_pct$is_top10)
cat(glue("PP(top 10%): {scales::percent(pp_top10, accuracy = 0.1)}\n"))#> PP(top 10%): 10.2%

heading: 11.4.7 Visualization
text:
Figure 11.1: Citation distribution sampled works, h-index threshold marked.

Figure 11.2: Mean Normalized Citation Score publication year.
code:
works_slim |>
  ggplot(aes(x = cited_by_count)) +
  geom_histogram(binwidth = 5, fill = palette_sci(1), color = "white") +
  geom_vline(xintercept = h, linetype = "dashed", color = "red", linewidth = 0.8) +
  annotate("text", x = h + 5, y = Inf, label = glue("h = {h}"),
           vjust = 2, hjust = 0, color = "red", size = 4) +
  labs(x = "Citation count", y = "Number of works") +
  theme_sci()
works_normalized |>
  group_by(year) |>
  summarise(mncs = mean(ncs, na.rm = TRUE), .groups = "drop") |>
  ggplot(aes(x = year, y = mncs)) +
  geom_line(color = palette_sci(1), linewidth = 1) +
  geom_point(color = palette_sci(1), size = 3) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 1, linetype = "dashed", color = "grey50") +
  labs(x = "Publication year", y = "MNCS") +
  theme_sci()

heading: 11.4.8 Summary table
code:
tibble(
  Indicator = c("h-index", "g-index", "m-quotient", "MNCS", "PP(top 10%)"),
  Value = c(
    as.character(h),
    as.character(g),
    round(m_quotient, 3) |> as.character(),
    round(mncs, 3) |> as.character(),
    scales::percent(pp_top10, accuracy = 0.1)
  ),
  Interpretation = c(
    glue("{h} papers with >= {h} citations each"),
    glue("Top {g} papers have >= {g}^2 = {g^2} cumulative citations"),
    glue("h-index per year of publication activity"),
    glue("{ifelse(mncs > 1, 'Above', 'Below')} world average"),
    glue("{scales::percent(pp_top10, accuracy = 0.1)} of papers in the top decile")
  )
) |>
  gt()

heading: 11.5 Diagnostics and interpretation
text: interpreting bibliometric indicators, keep mind:Citation windows matter. Recent papers less time accumulate citations. MNCS accounts normalizing within publication year, raw h-index g-index .Field differences. “good” h-index mathematics differs vastly one biomedicine. compare within fields, use field-normalized indicators.Size dependence. h-index grows number papers published. Comparing researchers different career stages different publication rates requires m-quotient percentile indicators.Self-citations. None indicators exclude self-citations. full analysis, consider computing variants without self-citations.

heading: 11.7 Limitations and responsible use
text: single number can capture quality, significance, societal impact researcher’s work. Leiden Manifesto (Hicks et al. 2015) offers ten principles responsible use metrics:Quantitative evaluation support, replace, expert assessment.Measure performance research missions institution, group, researcher.Protect excellence locally relevant research (just globally visible research).Keep data collection analytical processes open, transparent, simple.Allow evaluated verify data analysis.h-index particular can gamed self-citation, salami slicing, guest authorship. penalises researchers small fields, publish fewer higher-impact works, take career breaks. DORA declaration (American Society Cell Biology 2012) explicitly warns using journal-level metrics (like Impact Factor) surrogates individual article quality — caution applies single indicator.Always present multiple indicators alongside qualitative context. Never use single number make hiring, promotion, funding decisions.

heading: 11.9 Common pitfalls
text: Comparing h-indices across fields. physicist h = 40 mathematician h = 15 may equivalent standing respective fields. Use field-normalized indicators cross-field comparisons.Ignoring citation window. Computing MNCS without accounting publication year conflates old, well-cited papers recent ones haven’t time accumulate citations.Using wrong denominator PP(top 10%). “top 10%” must defined relative field year, relative corpus.Reporting precision isn’t . MNCS 1.237 vs. 1.241 meaningful difference. Report appropriate precision always provide confidence intervals bootstrapped uncertainty possible.

Write pseudocode analysis.Self-citation sensitivity. identify self-citations (e.g., via author overlap reference list), removing affect h-index? Write pseudocode analysis.Bootstrap uncertainty MNCS. Use replicate() sample() compute 95% confidence interval MNCS sample. wide interval?Bootstrap uncertainty MNCS. Use replicate() sample() compute 95% confidence interval MNCS sample. wide interval?PP(top 10%) vs. MNCS. sample data, plot PP(top 10%) MNCS year. always agree years performed best?PP(top 10%) vs. MNCS. sample data, plot PP(top 10%) MNCS year. always agree years performed best?
