heading: 12.1 Learning objectives
text: completing chapter, able :Describe Journal Impact Factor, CiteScore, SNIP, SJR, Eigenfactor computedExplain network-based journal metrics differ simple citation ratiosCompute JIF-like SNIP-like proxies OpenAlex dataArticulate proxy values differ official proprietary metricsEvaluate journals using multiple indicators rather single number

heading: 12.2 Setup
code:
library(tidyverse)
library(openalexR)
library(glue)
library(gt)

set.seed(20260509)

source(here::here("R", "api_helpers.R"))
source(here::here("R", "utils.R"))
source(here::here("R", "sci_palette.R"))

heading: 12.3 Conceptual background
text: Journal-level metrics aim summarise journal’s citation performance. famous Journal Impact Factor (JIF), introduced Garfield (1955) commercialised Clarivate. two-year JIF year t equals citations received year t articles published years t-1 t-2, divided number “citable items” published two years. simplicity appeal weakness: numerator includes citations document types, denominator often excludes editorials letters, inflating ratio (Hicks et al. 2015).CiteScore (Elsevier) uses four-year window counts document types numerator denominator, making transparent. SNIP (Source-Normalized Impact per Paper) normalises citation potential — average number references citing articles’ reference lists — journals fields longer reference lists automatically advantaged (Waltman 2016). SJR (SCImago Journal Rank) Eigenfactor use iterative algorithms inspired PageRank: citation prestigious journal carries weight one low-impact source. makes network-based indicators, conceptually similar eigenvector centrality graph theory.official metrics tied proprietary databases (Web Science Scopus). OpenAlex (Priem et al. 2022), can compute proxy values fully open data. Proxies match official figures exactly — differences coverage, document-type classification, citation linking guarantee divergence (Visser et al. 2021) — transparent, reproducible, free.DORA (American Society Cell Biology 2012) explicitly warns using JIF proxy individual article quality. Leiden Manifesto (Hicks et al. 2015) echoes : journal metrics describe average journal performance say nothing specific paper.

heading: 12.4.1 Selecting journals
text: analyse five well-known journals information science scientometrics.
code:
journals <- tribble(
  ~short_name,        ~source_id,
  "Scientometrics",   "S148561398",
  "J Informetrics",   "S205038879",
  "Quant Sci Stud",   "S4210178031",
  "J Am Soc Inf Sci", "S145342767",
  "Res Evaluation",   "S100532675"
)

heading: 12.4.2 Fetching citation data
text: fetch works published 2021-2022 count citations received 2022-2023 construct two-year JIF-like proxy.

heading: 12.4.3 Computing JIF-like proxies
code:
jif_proxy <- journal_works |>
  mutate(
    n_articles = map_int(data, nrow),
    total_cites = map_dbl(data, \(d) sum(d$cited_by_count, na.rm = TRUE))
  ) |>
  mutate(jif_proxy = total_cites / pmax(n_articles, 1)) |>
  select(short_name, n_articles, total_cites, jif_proxy) |>
  arrange(desc(jif_proxy))

jif_proxy |>
  gt() |>
  fmt_number(columns = jif_proxy, decimals = 2) |>
  cols_label(
    short_name = "Journal",
    n_articles = "Articles (2021-22)",
    total_cites = "Total citations",
    jif_proxy = "JIF proxy"
  )

heading: 12.4.4 Computing a SNIP-like proxy
text: SNIP normalises citation potential. approximate dividing journal’s mean citations per paper median reference-list length citing works.
code:
snip_proxy <- journal_works |>
  mutate(
    mean_cites = map_dbl(data, \(d) if (nrow(d) == 0) NA_real_ else mean(d$cited_by_count, na.rm = TRUE)),
    median_refs = map_dbl(data, \(d) {
      if (nrow(d) == 0) return(NA_real_)
      median(d$referenced_works_count, na.rm = TRUE)
    })
  ) |>
  mutate(
    citation_potential = pmax(median_refs, 1),
    snip_proxy = mean_cites / citation_potential
  ) |>
  select(short_name, mean_cites, citation_potential, snip_proxy) |>
  arrange(desc(snip_proxy))

snip_proxy |>
  gt() |>
  fmt_number(columns = c(mean_cites, snip_proxy), decimals = 2)

heading: 12.4.5 Visualization
text: Figure 12.1: Comparison JIF proxy SNIP proxy across five journals.
code:
combined <- jif_proxy |>
  select(short_name, jif_proxy) |>
  left_join(snip_proxy |> select(short_name, snip_proxy), by = "short_name") |>
  pivot_longer(cols = c(jif_proxy, snip_proxy), names_to = "metric", values_to = "value") |>
  mutate(metric = recode(metric, jif_proxy = "JIF proxy", snip_proxy = "SNIP proxy"))

ggplot(combined, aes(x = value, y = reorder(short_name, value), colour = metric)) +
  geom_point(size = 3) +
  labs(x = "Metric value", y = NULL, colour = "Metric") +
  scale_colour_manual(values = palette_sci(2)) +
  theme_sci()

heading: 12.5 Diagnostics and interpretation
text: evaluating journal metric proxies:Coverage gaps: OpenAlex may miss document types citations present Scopus/WoS. Compare article counts known totals.Document-type classification: JIF denominator famously excludes editorials letters. proxy counts articles; may lower ratio compared official JIF values.Temporal alignment: Ensure citation windows match intended metric definition exactly (two years JIF, four CiteScore).Rank correlation: Even absolute values differ official metrics, rank order journals often agrees, sufficient many analyses.

heading: 12.7 Limitations and responsible use
text: Journal metrics article metrics. distribution citations within journal highly skewed; mean says little individual paper (American Society Cell Biology 2012).Goodhart’s law applies. metric becomes target, ceases good metric (Goodhart 1984). Editors may game JIF citation stacking, coercive citation, manipulating denominator.Proxy ≠ official. OpenAlex-based proxies transparent reproducible, differ Clarivate JIF Elsevier CiteScore. Never present proxies official values.Field dependence persists. Even SNIP fully eliminate field effects. Comparing SNIP values across distant disciplines requires caution (Waltman 2016).

heading: 12.9 Common pitfalls
text: Treating JIF measure article quality. JIF describes journal, paper. paper high-JIF journal may zero citations.Ignoring citation window. Computing “JIF” using lifetime citations instead two-year window produces different non-comparable number.Conflating numerator denominator document types. Mixing reviews (inflate numerator) denominator excludes inflates ratio.Ranking journals single metric. Always use multiple indicators consider disciplinary context.

heading: 13.1 Learning objectives
text: completing chapter, able :Explain author disambiguation problem impact bibliometric analysisRetrieve interpret OpenAlex author entities, including name variantsIntegrate ORCID identifiers validate author identityCompute author-level productivity, citation, topical statistics works dataBuild reproducible author profile report OpenAlex

heading: 13.2 Setup
code:
library(tidyverse)
library(openalexR)
library(glue)
library(gt)

set.seed(20260509)

source(here::here("R", "api_helpers.R"))
source(here::here("R", "utils.R"))
source(here::here("R", "sci_palette.R"))

heading: 13.3 Conceptual background
text: Correctly attributing publications individual researchers prerequisite author-level analysis, yet remains one hardest problems bibliometrics. Author disambiguation — determining whether two name strings refer person — complicated name variants (initials vs. full names, transliterations, name changes marriage), homonymy (different people sharing name), inconsistent metadata across databases.OpenAlex (Priem et al. 2022) addresses algorithmic author clustering groups works likely belonging person. cluster receives unique author ID (e.g., A5023888391). algorithm considers name similarity, co-author overlap, institutional affiliation, topic continuity, citation patterns. imperfect, clustering transparent continuously improved.ORCID (Open Researcher Contributor ID) provides complementary solution: persistent digital identifier researchers claim curate . ORCID linked OpenAlex author entity, provides strong confirmation identity. However, ORCID adoption uneven across disciplines geographies, serve sole disambiguation strategy.h-index (Hirsch 2005) related indicators (11.3) commonly used summarise author-level impact, meaningful underlying publication set correctly attributed. split author (one person assigned two IDs) deflated h-index; merged author (two people sharing one ID) inflated one.Author-level analysis also raises ethical concerns. Leiden Manifesto (Hicks et al. 2015) warns reducing researcher’s contribution single number. Career breaks, discipline-specific norms, Matthew effect (Merton 1968) — established researchers accumulate citations disproportionately — must considered interpreting author-level metrics.

heading: 13.4.1 Retrieving an author entity
text: retrieve OpenAlex profile well-known scientometrician.
code:
author_entity <- oa_fetch(
  entity = "authors",
  search = "Ludo Waltman",
  options = list(sort = "cited_by_count:desc")
)

author_info <- author_entity |> slice(1)
cat(glue("Name: {author_info$display_name}\n"))#> Name: Ludo Waltman
cat(glue("OpenAlex ID: {author_info$id}\n"))#> OpenAlex ID: https://openalex.org/A5027467000
cat(glue("ORCID: {author_info$orcid}\n"))#> ORCID: https://orcid.org/0000-0001-8249-1752
cat(glue("Works count: {author_info$works_count}\n"))#> Works count: 391
cat(glue("Cited by count: {author_info$cited_by_count}\n"))#> Cited by count: 45005

heading: 13.4.2 Fetching the author’s works
code:
author_works <- oa_fetch(
  entity = "works",
  author.id = author_info$id,
  from_publication_date = "2010-01-01",
  to_publication_date = "2023-12-31"
)

works_slim <- author_works |>
  select(id, display_name, publication_date, cited_by_count, type, source_display_name) |>
  mutate(year = year(publication_date))

cat(glue("Works retrieved: {nrow(works_slim)}\n"))#> Works retrieved: 300

heading: 13.4.3 Author-level statistics
code:
h_idx <- compute_h_index(works_slim$cited_by_count)

stats <- tibble(
  Metric = c("Total works", "Total citations", "h-index",
             "Mean citations/paper", "Median citations/paper",
             "First publication year", "Most recent year"),
  Value = c(
    nrow(works_slim),
    sum(works_slim$cited_by_count),
    h_idx,
    round(mean(works_slim$cited_by_count), 1),
    median(works_slim$cited_by_count),
    min(works_slim$year),
    max(works_slim$year)
  )
)

stats |> gt()

heading: 13.4.4 Publication timeline
text: Figure 13.1: Annual publication output cumulative citation count.
code:
annual <- works_slim |>
  group_by(year) |>
  summarise(n_pubs = n(), cites = sum(cited_by_count), .groups = "drop") |>
  mutate(cum_cites = cumsum(cites))

ggplot(annual, aes(x = year)) +
  geom_col(aes(y = n_pubs), fill = palette_sci(1), width = 0.7) +
  geom_line(aes(y = cum_cites / max(cum_cites) * max(n_pubs)),
            colour = palette_sci(2)[2], linewidth = 1) +
  scale_y_continuous(
    name = "Publications per year",
    sec.axis = sec_axis(~ . / max(annual$n_pubs) * max(annual$cum_cites),
                        name = "Cumulative citations")
  ) +
  labs(x = "Year") +
  theme_sci()

heading: 13.4.5 Top venues
text: Figure 13.2: Top 10 venues number publications author.
code:
venue_counts <- works_slim |>
  filter(!is.na(source_display_name)) |>
  count(source_display_name, sort = TRUE) |>
  head(10)

ggplot(venue_counts, aes(x = n, y = reorder(source_display_name, n))) +
  geom_col(fill = palette_sci(1)) +
  labs(x = "Number of articles", y = NULL) +
  theme_sci()

heading: 13.4.6 Checking for name variants
13.5 Diagnostics and interpretation
text: building author profiles, verify following:Works count plausibility: number retrieved works match author’s known output? Large discrepancies suggest split merged entity.Topical coherence: Scan titles venues. works span wildly unrelated fields, entity may conflate two researchers similar names.ORCID match: author ORCID, cross-check OpenAlex entity links .Co-author consistency: author’s frequent co-authors recognisable collaborators, strangers unrelated field.Citation distribution: extreme outlier (e.g., one paper 10,000 citations others fewer 50) may indicate attribution error.

heading: 13.7 Limitations and responsible use
text: Disambiguation imperfect. OpenAlex’s algorithm sometimes split one person multiple entities merge distinct individuals. Always validate profiles manually high-stakes analyses.ORCID coverage uneven. Researchers Global South, early-career scholars, humanities adopt ORCID lower rates. Filtering ORCID-linked authors introduces bias.Matthew effect. Established researchers accumulate citations disproportionately (Merton 1968). Author-level metrics favour seniority visibility, necessarily quality originality.Career breaks part-time research. Indicators like h-index penalise researchers take parental leave, work part-time, switch careers. m-quotient rough correction (Hirsch 2005).Never evaluate individuals numbers alone (Hicks et al. 2015; American Society Cell Biology 2012). Author profiles supplement, replace, reading actual work.

heading: 13.9 Common pitfalls
text: Trusting single database. OpenAlex may lack works indexed domain-specific databases (e.g., SSRN, arXiv preprints yet linked). Cross-check ORCID profiles.Ignoring co-author contributions. Raw publication counts treat single-author 50-author papers equally. Consider fractional counting productivity analysis.Comparing across career stages. early-career researcher h = 8 5 years may productive senior researcher h = 25 30 years. Use m-quotient age-normalised indicators.Conflating author-level paper-level metrics. author’s h-index says nothing papers excellent .

heading: 13.10 Exercises
text: Profile comparison. Fetch profiles two researchers field. Compare h-index, publication count, mean citations per paper. metric reveal others ?Profile comparison. Fetch profiles two researchers field. Compare h-index, publication count, mean citations per paper. metric reveal others ?ORCID validation. author ORCID, fetch works via OpenAlex author ID ORCID filtering. two sets agree? might explain discrepancies?ORCID validation. author ORCID, fetch works via OpenAlex author ID ORCID filtering. two sets agree? might explain discrepancies?Name variant detection. Search OpenAlex common name (e.g., “Wang Wei”). many author entities returned? heuristics use identify correct one?Name variant detection. Search OpenAlex common name (e.g., “Wang Wei”). many author entities returned? heuristics use identify correct one?Fractional productivity. Recompute annual publication counts using fractional counting (1/k credit per paper k authors). change author’s productivity curve?Fractional productivity. Recompute annual publication counts using fractional counting (1/k credit per paper k authors). change author’s productivity curve?Self-citation ratio. author’s works, estimate fraction citations come author’s papers. (Hint: check cited works share author ID.)Self-citation ratio. author’s works, estimate fraction citations come author’s papers. (Hint: check cited works share author ID.)

text: Solutions provided 2.11.

(2022) — OpenAlex’s author disambiguation entity resolution approach.Hirsch (2005) — h-index, widely used author-level indicator.Merton (1968) — Matthew effect: cumulative advantage scientific recognition.Hicks et al. (2015) — Leiden Manifesto: principles responsible author evaluation.American Society Cell Biology (2012) — DORA: recommendations reducing researchers single numbers.Egghe (2006) — g-index, complement h-index author evaluation.

heading: 14.1 Learning objectives
text: completing chapter, able :Aggregate bibliometric data institutional country levels using OpenAlexExplain difference full counting fractional counting appropriateUse ROR (Research Organization Registry) identifiers reliable institutional matchingCompute size-independent indicators (MNCS, PP(top 10%)) institutional levelInterpret institutional rankings critically, accounting size, field mix, counting method

heading: 14.2 Setup
code:
library(tidyverse)
library(openalexR)
library(glue)
library(gt)

set.seed(20260509)

source(here::here("R", "api_helpers.R"))
source(here::here("R", "utils.R"))
source(here::here("R", "sci_palette.R"))

heading: 14.3 Conceptual background
text: Comparing research performance across institutions countries one common — contentious — applications bibliometrics. University rankings, national R&D assessments, funding allocation decisions rely aggregate bibliometric statistics. Getting aggregation right matters enormously.fundamental choice full counting fractional counting. full counting, institution listed paper receives one full credit paper. paper authors five institutions thus generates five institutional publications. fractional counting, credit divided: institution receives 1/5 publication count. Full counting inflates output institutions frequently collaborate, fractional counting better reflects entity’s proportional contribution (Glänzel Schubert 2004; Waltman et al. 2011).ROR (Research Organization Registry) identifiers provide stable, open system identifying institutions, analogous DOIs publications ORCID researchers. OpenAlex maps institutional affiliations ROR IDs, though coverage varies discipline geography. Using ROR avoids pitfalls string matching institution names, notoriously inconsistent (e.g., “MIT”, “Massachusetts Institute Technology”, “Mass. Inst. Tech.”).Size-independent indicators essential fair comparison. Raw publication counts favour large universities. Mean Normalised Citation Score (MNCS) (Waltman et al. 2011) PP(top 10%) — proportion papers top 10% cited field year — allow meaningful comparison small specialised institute large comprehensive university. Leiden Ranking uses indicators fractional counting default, offering model responsible institutional benchmarking.Country-level analysis faces choices. Additionally, linguistic bias databases means countries English primary research language may appear less productive truly (Visser et al. 2021).

heading: 14.4.1 Selecting institutions
text: compare five research-intensive universities.
code:
institutions <- tribble(
  ~short_name,  ~ror_id,
  "MIT",        "I63966007",
  "Oxford",     "I40120149",
  "ETH Zurich", "I202697423",
  "U Tokyo",    "I39804265",
  "U Cape Town", "I173603587"
)

heading: 14.4.2 Fetching institutional publications
code:
fetch_inst_works <- function(inst_id) {
  res <- tryCatch(
    oa_fetch(
      entity = "works",
      authorships.institutions.id = inst_id,
      from_publication_date = "2020-01-01",
      to_publication_date = "2022-12-31",
      type = "article",
      options = list(sample = 400, seed = 42)
    ),
    error = function(e) NULL
  )
  if (is.null(res)) tibble() else res
}

inst_data <- institutions |>
  mutate(works = map(ror_id, fetch_inst_works)) |>
  filter(map_int(works, nrow) > 0)

heading: 14.4.3 Full counting vs. fractional counting
code:
compute_counts <- function(works_df, inst_id) {
  if (is.null(works_df) || nrow(works_df) == 0) {
    return(tibble(full_count = 0L, frac_count = 0))
  }
  # Full count: each paper counts as 1
  full_count <- nrow(works_df)

  # Fractional count: 1 / number of distinct institutions per paper
  frac <- works_df |>
    select(id, authorships) |>
    unnest(authorships, names_sep = "_") |>
    unnest(authorships_affiliations, names_sep = "_") |>
    group_by(id) |>
    summarise(n_inst = n_distinct(authorships_affiliations_id), .groups = "drop") |>
    mutate(frac_credit = 1 / pmax(n_inst, 1))

  frac_count <- sum(frac$frac_credit)

  tibble(full_count = full_count, frac_count = round(frac_count, 1))
}

counting_comparison <- inst_data |>
  mutate(counts = map2(works, ror_id, compute_counts)) |>
  unnest(counts) |>
  select(short_name, full_count, frac_count) |>
  mutate(ratio = round(frac_count / full_count, 2))

counting_comparison |>
  gt() |>
  cols_label(
    short_name = "Institution",
    full_count = "Full count",
    frac_count = "Fractional count",
    ratio = "Frac / Full"
  )

heading: 14.4.4 Normalised citation impact
code:
inst_impact <- inst_data |>
  mutate(
    mean_cites = map_dbl(works, \(w) mean(w$cited_by_count, na.rm = TRUE)),
    median_cites = map_dbl(works, \(w) median(w$cited_by_count, na.rm = TRUE)),
    mncs_approx = map_dbl(works, \(w) {
      w |>
        mutate(year = year(publication_date)) |>
        group_by(year) |>
        mutate(field_mean = mean(cited_by_count)) |>
        ungroup() |>
        mutate(ncs = cited_by_count / pmax(field_mean, 0.01)) |>
        pull(ncs) |>
        mean(na.rm = TRUE)
    })
  ) |>
  select(short_name, mean_cites, median_cites, mncs_approx) |>
  arrange(desc(mncs_approx))

inst_impact |>
  gt() |>
  fmt_number(columns = c(mean_cites, mncs_approx), decimals = 2)

heading: 14.4.5 Visualization
text:
Figure 14.1: Full count vs. fractional count publication output five institutions.

Figure 14.2: Approximate MNCS institution (within-sample normalisation).
code:
counting_long <- counting_comparison |>
  pivot_longer(cols = c(full_count, frac_count),
               names_to = "method", values_to = "count") |>
  mutate(method = recode(method,
                         full_count = "Full counting",
                         frac_count = "Fractional counting"))

ggplot(counting_long, aes(x = count, y = reorder(short_name, count),
                          fill = method)) +
  geom_col(position = "dodge", width = 0.7) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = palette_sci(2)) +
  labs(x = "Publication count", y = NULL, fill = "Method") +
  theme_sci()
ggplot(inst_impact, aes(x = mncs_approx, y = reorder(short_name, mncs_approx))) +
  geom_col(fill = palette_sci(1)) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = 1, linetype = "dashed", colour = "grey50") +
  labs(x = "MNCS (approx.)", y = NULL) +
  theme_sci()

heading: 14.5 Diagnostics and interpretation
text: Size vs. impact: large university may rank highly total output poorly per-paper impact. Always report size-dependent size-independent indicators.Field mix: medical university appear productive (highly cited) social-science institute due field-level citation norms. True normalisation requires field-level data.Sample vs. population: sample 400 works per institution illustrative. real benchmarking, use full population report confidence intervals.Fractional/full ratio: ratio near 1.0 indicates collaborations beyond institution; ratio well 1.0 indicates extensive inter-institutional collaboration.

heading: 14.7 Limitations and responsible use
text: Rankings oversimplify. Institutional country rankings compress multidimensional reality single number. Leiden Manifesto (Hicks et al. 2015) warns using rankings mechanically.Affiliation data quality. papers complete correct institutional affiliations. OpenAlex coverage depends publisher metadata quality.Counting method changes conclusions. data can yield different institutional rankings depending whether full fractional counting used. Always report method.Language geographic bias. Databases undercount research published non-English languages, disadvantaging countries Latin America, Asia, Africa (Visser et al. 2021; Singh et al. 2021).compare across fields without normalisation. biomedical research university always outperform humanities-focused institution raw citation indicators.

heading: 14.9 Common pitfalls
text: Using full counting fractional appropriate. Full counting double-credits collaborative work, inflating output institutions many international partnerships.Ignoring field normalisation. Comparing raw MNCS across institutions different disciplinary profiles misleading.String-matching institution names. Never match institutions name strings alone. Use ROR OpenAlex institution IDs.Treating samples populations. sampled 400 works per institution, resulting metrics uncertainty. Report .Forgetting subsidiary institutions. Large universities may hospitals, research institutes, campuses separate ROR IDs. Decide unit analysis upfront.

heading: 15.1 Learning objectives
text: completing chapter, able :Define apply citation windows different lengthsCompute interpret citation half-life journal corpusPlot citation aging curves showing citations accumulate timeCalculate Price index (proportion references less five years old)Analyse temporal trends publication output citation ratesExplain aging patterns differ across disciplines

heading: 15.2 Setup
code:
library(tidyverse)
library(openalexR)
library(glue)
library(gt)

set.seed(20260509)

source(here::here("R", "api_helpers.R"))
source(here::here("R", "utils.R"))
source(here::here("R", "sci_palette.R"))

heading: 15.3 Conceptual background
text: Citations evenly distributed time. papers receive bulk citations within years publication, gradually fade. phenomenon — citation aging obsolescence — first studied systematically Solla Price (1963), observed scientific literature characteristic “half-life”: time half citations given year’s output received.citation half-life varies dramatically across disciplines. fast-moving fields like molecular biology, half-lives may three four years; mathematics humanities, can exceed ten years. direct consequences metrics: two-year Journal Impact Factor systematically undervalues journals slow-aging fields (Waltman 2016).citation window fixed period publication citations counted. JIF uses two-year window; CiteScore uses four years. Choosing window involves trade-: short windows favour fast-aging fields miss delayed-recognition papers; long windows inclusive dilute signal recent trends.Price index — proportion references paper’s bibliography less five years old — measures rapidly field consumes recent literature. high Price index indicates fast-moving frontier field; low Price index suggests reliance older foundational works. Solla Price (1963) introduced “immediacy” measure.Sleeping beauties — papers receive citations years suddenly attract attention (Kleinberg 2003) — violate standard aging model. existence reminder citation half-lives describe averages, individual papers. Garfield (1955) already noted ideas take decades recognised.Understanding citation temporality essential fair evaluation. Comparing citation counts without accounting publication year, citation window, field-specific aging rates produces meaningless results.

heading: 15.4.1 Acquiring the data
text: fetch works two journals different expected aging profiles.
code:
# Scientometrics (social science, moderate aging)
works_sciento <- oa_fetch(
  entity = "works",
  primary_location.source.id = "S148561398",
  from_publication_date = "2013-01-01",
  to_publication_date = "2018-12-31",
  type = "article",
  options = list(sample = 400, seed = 42)
)

# PLOS ONE (multidisciplinary, faster aging)
works_plos <- oa_fetch(
  entity = "works",
  primary_location.source.id = "S202381698",
  from_publication_date = "2013-01-01",
  to_publication_date = "2018-12-31",
  type = "article",
  options = list(sample = 400, seed = 42)
)

works_all <- bind_rows(
  works_sciento |> mutate(journal = "Scientometrics"),
  works_plos |> mutate(journal = "PLOS ONE")
) |>
  mutate(year = year(publication_date),
         age = 2024 - year)

heading: 15.4.2 Citation aging curves
text:
compute mean citations age (years since publication) journal.
Figure 15.1: Citation aging curves Scientometrics PLOS ONE articles published 2013-2018.
code:
aging <- works_all |>
  group_by(journal, age) |>
  summarise(
    mean_cites = mean(cited_by_count, na.rm = TRUE),
    median_cites = median(cited_by_count, na.rm = TRUE),
    n = n(),
    .groups = "drop"
  ) |>
  arrange(journal, age)
ggplot(aging, aes(x = age, y = mean_cites, colour = journal)) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 1) +
  geom_point(size = 2) +
  scale_colour_manual(values = palette_sci(2)) +
  labs(x = "Years since publication", y = "Mean citations",
       colour = "Journal") +
  theme_sci()

heading: 15.4.3 Estimating citation half-life
text: estimate half-life age cumulative citations reach 50% total.
code:
half_life <- works_all |>
  group_by(journal) |>
  arrange(age) |>
  mutate(
    cum_cites = cumsum(cited_by_count),
    total_cites = sum(cited_by_count),
    cum_pct = cum_cites / total_cites
  ) |>
  summarise(
    total_cites = first(total_cites),
    half_life_age = min(age[cum_pct >= 0.50]),
    .groups = "drop"
  )

half_life |>
  gt() |>
  cols_label(
    journal = "Journal",
    total_cites = "Total citations",
    half_life_age = "Half-life (years)"
  )

heading: 15.4.4 Price index
text: Price index fraction references less five years old. full computation requires publication years reference; show reference-list lengths starting point.
code:
works_all |>
  filter(!is.na(referenced_works_count), referenced_works_count > 0) |>
  group_by(journal) |>
  summarise(mean_refs = round(mean(referenced_works_count), 1), .groups = "drop")#> # A tibble: 2 × 2
#>   journal        mean_refs
#>   <chr>              <dbl>
#> 1 PLOS ONE            49.7
#> 2 Scientometrics      42.7

heading: 15.4.5 Cumulative citation trajectories
text: Figure 15.2: Cumulative citation percentage years since publication.
code:
cum_aging <- works_all |>
  group_by(journal, age) |>
  summarise(total = sum(cited_by_count), .groups = "drop") |>
  group_by(journal) |>
  arrange(age) |>
  mutate(
    cum_total = cumsum(total),
    cum_pct = cum_total / sum(total)
  )

ggplot(cum_aging, aes(x = age, y = cum_pct, colour = journal)) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 1) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0.5, linetype = "dashed", colour = "grey50") +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = scales::percent) +
  scale_colour_manual(values = palette_sci(2)) +
  labs(x = "Years since publication", y = "Cumulative % of citations",
       colour = "Journal") +
  annotate("text", x = 9, y = 0.52, label = "50% threshold",
           colour = "grey40", size = 3.5) +
  theme_sci()

heading: 15.5 Diagnostics and interpretation
text: Sample size per age bin: Ensure year enough papers stable estimates. Small cohorts produce noisy aging curves.Truncation: Papers published recent year time accumulate citations. Exclude flag incomplete citation windows.Disciplinary benchmarks: Compare half-life estimates known benchmarks. Social sciences typically half-lives 5-8 years; biomedical fields 3-5 years; mathematics 8-12 years.Outliers: extremely highly cited papers can distort mean-based aging curves. Compare mean median trajectories.

heading: 15.7 Limitations and responsible use
text: Half-life average. Individual papers deviate wildly average aging pattern. Sleeping beauties flash---pan papers exist.Database lag. Citation counts OpenAlex may lag behind reality weeks months. recent citation data unreliable.Survivor bias. Retracted papers papers removed databases disappear aging analyses, potentially biasing half-life estimates.Citation inflation. total volume citations grown time. paper published 2020 accumulate citations faster identical paper published 2000, simply papers published (Solla Price 1963).penalise slow-aging fields. Using short citation windows systematically undervalues mathematics, humanities, theoretical disciplines (Hicks et al. 2015).

heading: 15.9 Common pitfalls
text: Mixing citation windows. Comparing 2-year JIF one journal lifetime citations another produces meaningless rankings.Ignoring publication-year effects. corpus spanning 2000-2023 includes papers 23 years citation history alongside papers 1 year. Normalise publication year.Confusing obsolescence irrelevance. declining citation curve mean knowledge wrong useless; may mean ideas absorbed textbooks standard practice.Forgetting denominator. number publications field growing, absolute citation count grows even per-paper impact constant.

heading: 16.1 Learning objectives
text: completing chapter, able :Construct co-authorship network bibliographic data using igraphCompute interpret node-level metrics: degree, betweenness centrality, clustering coefficientApply Louvain Leiden community detection algorithms compare resultsProduce deterministic network layouts render identically HTML PDFIdentify central authors structural holes collaboration network

heading: 16.2 Setup
code:
library(tidyverse)
library(openalexR)
library(igraph)
library(tidygraph)
library(ggraph)
library(glue)

set.seed(20260509)

source(here::here("R", "api_helpers.R"))
source(here::here("R", "utils.R"))
source(here::here("R", "sci_palette.R"))

heading: 16.3 Conceptual background
text: Co-authorship one visible well-studied forms scientific collaboration. two researchers appear together publication, form tie co-authorship network. Studying networks reveals social structure science: collaborates , groups isolated, individuals serve bridges communities (Newman 2001).Co-authorship networks typically modelled undirected, weighted graphs. Nodes represent authors, edge connects two authors co-authored least one paper. Edge weights reflect number co-authored papers (, alternatively, fractional counts inversely proportional number authors per paper). networks tend exhibit properties common social networks: heavy-tailed degree distribution (authors collaborators, many), high clustering (collaborators collaborators tend collaborate), small-world structure (Barabási et al. 2002).Community detection identifies groups authors collaborate densely among rest network. Louvain algorithm (Blondel et al. 2008) fast widely used; Leiden algorithm (Traag et al. 2019) provides theoretical guarantees communities well-connected, avoiding “resolution limit” problem can afflict Louvain.Network analysis co-authorship data widely applied map research fields (Glänzel Schubert 2004), identify key opinion leaders, assess interdisciplinary collaboration, study evolution research communities time. Fortunato (2010) provides comprehensive review community detection methods applicable networks.

heading: 16.4.1 Acquiring co-authorship data
text: fetch works journal extract author pairs.
code:
works <- oa_fetch(
  entity = "works",
  primary_location.source.id = "S148561398",
  from_publication_date = "2021-01-01",
  to_publication_date = "2023-12-31",
  options = list(sample = 300, seed = 42)
)

nrow(works)#> [1] 300

heading: 16.4.2 Extracting author pairs
text: work list-column authors. unnest build pairwise co-author edges.
code:
author_data <- works |>
  select(id, authorships) |>
  unnest(authorships, names_sep = "_") |>
  select(work_id = id, author_id = authorships_id, author_name = authorships_display_name) |>
  filter(!is.na(author_id))

edges <- author_data |>
  inner_join(author_data, by = "work_id", suffix = c("_1", "_2"),
             relationship = "many-to-many") |>
  filter(author_id_1 < author_id_2) |>
  count(author_id_1, author_id_2,
        name_1 = author_name_1, name_2 = author_name_2,
        name = "weight")

cat(glue("Authors: {n_distinct(c(edges$author_id_1, edges$author_id_2))}\n"))#> Authors: 784
cat(glue("Edges: {nrow(edges)}\n"))#> Edges: 1813

heading: 16.4.3 Building the network
code:
g <- graph_from_data_frame(
  edges |> select(author_id_1, author_id_2, weight),
  directed = FALSE
)

g <- simplify(g, edge.attr.comb = list(weight = "sum"))

cat(glue("Nodes: {vcount(g)}, Edges: {ecount(g)}\n"))#> Nodes: 784, Edges: 1813
cat(glue("Components: {components(g)$no}\n"))#> Components: 188
cat(glue("Density: {round(graph.density(g), 4)}\n"))#> Density: 0.0059

heading: 16.4.4 Network metrics
code:
V(g)$degree <- degree(g)
V(g)$betweenness <- betweenness(g, normalized = TRUE)
V(g)$clustering <- transitivity(g, type = "local")

author_lookup <- author_data |>
  distinct(author_id, author_name)

metrics <- tibble(
  author_id = V(g)$name,
  degree = V(g)$degree,
  betweenness = V(g)$betweenness,
  clustering = V(g)$clustering
) |>
  left_join(author_lookup, by = "author_id") |>
  arrange(desc(degree))

metrics |>
  head(10) |>
  select(author_name, degree, betweenness, clustering)#> # A tibble: 10 × 4
#>    author_name                degree betweenness clustering
#>    <chr>                       <dbl>       <dbl>      <dbl>
#>  1 Vincent Larivière              31 0.00149          0.708
#>  2 Rodrigo Costas                 30 0.00233          0.749
#>  3 Loet Leydesdorff               30 0.00781          0.752
#>  4 Ludo Waltman                   29 0.000902         0.808
#>  5 Isabella Peters                29 0.000902         0.808
#>  6 Mike Thelwall                  28 0.000608         0.862
#>  7 Isidro F. Aguillo              26 0.000000126      1    
#>  8 Zaida Chinchilla‐Rodríguez     26 0.000000126      1    
#>  9 Ciriaco Andrea D’Angelo        26 0                1    
#> 10 Juan Miguel Campanario         26 0.000000126      1

heading: 16.4.5 Community detection
text: apply Louvain Leiden largest connected component.
code:
comp <- components(g)
giant <- induced_subgraph(g, which(comp$membership == which.max(comp$csize)))

louvain <- cluster_louvain(giant)
V(giant)$community_louvain <- membership(louvain)

cat(glue("Louvain communities: {length(unique(membership(louvain)))}\n"))#> Louvain communities: 11
cat(glue("Louvain modularity: {round(modularity(louvain), 3)}\n"))#> Louvain modularity: 0.427
leiden_result <- cluster_leiden(giant, resolution_parameter = 1.0,
                                objective_function = "modularity")
V(giant)$community_leiden <- membership(leiden_result)

cat(glue("Leiden communities: {length(unique(membership(leiden_result)))}\n"))#> Leiden communities: 11
cat(glue("Leiden modularity: {round(modularity(giant, membership(leiden_result)), 3)}\n"))#> Leiden modularity: 0.427

heading: 16.4.6 Comparing community assignments
code:
comparison <- tibble(
  louvain = V(giant)$community_louvain,
  leiden = V(giant)$community_leiden
)

nmi <- igraph::compare(
  V(giant)$community_louvain,
  V(giant)$community_leiden,
  method = "nmi"
)
cat(glue("Normalized Mutual Information (Louvain vs Leiden): {round(nmi, 3)}\n"))#> Normalized Mutual Information (Louvain vs Leiden): 0.94

heading: 16.4.7 Visualization
text:
use Fruchterman-Reingold layout fixed seed deterministic output.
Figure 16.1: Co-authorship network sampled Scientometrics articles (2021–2023), coloured Leiden community.

Figure 16.2: Degree distribution co-authorship network.
code:
giant_tidy <- as_tbl_graph(giant) |>
  mutate(
    community = as.factor(community_leiden),
    label = ifelse(degree > quantile(degree, 0.95),
                   author_lookup$author_name[match(name, author_lookup$author_id)],
                   NA_character_)
  )

set.seed(42)
layout <- create_layout(giant_tidy, layout = "fr")

ggraph(layout) +
  geom_edge_link(alpha = 0.15, colour = "grey60") +
  geom_node_point(aes(size = degree, colour = community), alpha = 0.8) +
  geom_node_text(aes(label = label), repel = TRUE, size = 2.5,
                 max.overlaps = 15, na.rm = TRUE) +
  scale_size_continuous(range = c(1, 6), guide = "none") +
  scale_colour_manual(values = palette_sci(
    n_distinct(giant_tidy |> pull(community), na.rm = TRUE)
  )) +
  labs(colour = "Community") +
  theme_void(base_family = "sans", base_size = 11) +
  theme(legend.position = "bottom")
tibble(degree = degree(giant)) |>
  ggplot(aes(x = degree)) +
  geom_histogram(binwidth = 1, fill = palette_sci(1), colour = "white") +
  labs(x = "Degree (number of co-authors)", y = "Count") +
  theme_sci()

heading: 16.5 Diagnostics and interpretation
text: Key diagnostics co-authorship networks:Giant component ratio: fraction nodes largest connected component? fragmented network may indicate niche field sparse sample.Degree distribution: heavy-tailed distribution expected. network nodes similar degree may indicate problem data (e.g., single-author papers excluded).Modularity: Values 0.3 typically indicate meaningful community structure. Modularity near zero suggests network clear communities.Comparison algorithms: Louvain Leiden produce different community counts, investigate resolution parameter. High NMI (>0.8) indicates strong agreement.
code:
giant_ratio <- vcount(giant) / vcount(g)
cat(glue("Giant component: {vcount(giant)}/{vcount(g)} nodes ({scales::percent(giant_ratio)})\n"))#> Giant component: 96/784 nodes (12%)
cat(glue("Mean degree: {round(mean(degree(giant)), 1)}\n"))#> Mean degree: 10.4
cat(glue("Transitivity (global): {round(transitivity(giant, type = 'global'), 3)}\n"))#> Transitivity (global): 0.925

heading: 16.7 Limitations and responsible use
text: Co-authorship ≠ collaboration. collaborations result co-authored publications, co-authorships reflect genuine intellectual contribution (honorary authorship).Name ambiguity. Author names bibliographic databases unique identifiers. Despite OpenAlex’s entity resolution, errors persist — especially common names transliterated names. Always inspect suspicious high-degree nodes.Fractional vs. full counting. construction counts co-authorship edge equally regardless number authors paper. Fractional counting (weight = 1/(n-1) per edge) gives less weight papers many authors may appropriate analyses (Glänzel Schubert 2004).Temporal dynamics. Static networks aggregate collaborations single snapshot. Consider building time-sliced networks study collaboration patterns evolve.Causality. Network position (e.g., high betweenness) imply leadership influence. structural observation, evaluative judgement (Hicks et al. 2015).

heading: 16.9 Common pitfalls
text: Non-deterministic layouts. Force-directed layouts (Fruchterman-Reingold, Kamada-Kawai) depend random seed. Always set.seed() computing layout, figure change every render.Including full network. large networks unreadable. Filter giant component, top-N nodes, specific community plotting.Ignoring multi-edges self-loops. igraph::simplify() essential constructing graph edge lists may contain duplicate pairs.Comparing modularity across networks different sizes. Modularity sensitive network size density. Use Normalized Mutual Information (NMI) compare community structures.Overinterpreting small components. component 2–3 authors co-authored one paper “community” — artifact sample.

text: completing chapter, able :Distinguish co-citation analysis bibliographic coupling explain appropriateConstruct co-citation bibliographic coupling networks OpenAlex reference dataCompute normalised similarity measures (cosine, Jaccard) citation-based linkagesIdentify core documents intellectual clusters research fieldInterpret co-citation maps representations intellectual structure

heading: 17.2 Setup
code:
library(tidyverse)
library(openalexR)
library(igraph)
library(tidygraph)
library(ggraph)
library(glue)
library(gt)

set.seed(20260509)

source(here::here("R", "api_helpers.R"))
source(here::here("R", "utils.R"))
source(here::here("R", "sci_palette.R"))

heading: 17.3 Conceptual background
text: co-authorship networks (16.3) reveal social structure, co-citation bibliographic coupling networks reveal intellectual structure — conceptual relationships documents expressed citation behaviour.Co-citation analysis, introduced independently Small (1973) Marshakova-Shaikevich 1973, measures relatedness two documents often cited together papers. documents B frequently appear reference lists, likely address related topics represent complementary contributions field. Co-citation strength changes time: field evolves, new citing papers create new co-citation links strengthen weaken existing ones.Bibliographic coupling, proposed Kessler (1963), takes complementary perspective. Two documents bibliographically coupled share one references. Unlike co-citation, coupling strength fixed publication time — paper published, reference list change. makes bibliographic coupling especially useful analysing recent literature, co-citation links yet time form.two methods reveal different facets intellectual structure. Co-citation maps reception ideas (community groups prior work); bibliographic coupling maps production knowledge (authors draw shared foundations). Waltman et al. (2010) demonstrated combining perspectives yields richer maps science either alone.methods produce similarity matrices can converted networks. Common normalisation approaches include cosine similarity (emphasising relative overlap) Jaccard index (penalising pairs many unshared references). Unnormalised raw counts dominated highly-cited papers; normalisation essential balanced network construction.

heading: 17.4.1 Fetching citing and cited works
text: fetch corpus scientometrics research extract reference lists.
code:
works <- oa_fetch(
  entity = "works",
  primary_location.source.id = "S148561398",
  from_publication_date = "2020-01-01",
  to_publication_date = "2023-12-31",
  options = list(sample = 200, seed = 42)
)

refs <- works |>
  select(citing_id = id, referenced_works) |>
  unnest(referenced_works) |>
  rename(cited_id = referenced_works)

cat(glue("Citing works: {n_distinct(refs$citing_id)}\n"))#> Citing works: 200
cat(glue("Cited works: {n_distinct(refs$cited_id)}\n"))#> Cited works: 8536
cat(glue("Citation links: {nrow(refs)}\n"))#> Citation links: 9988

heading: 17.4.2 Building a co-citation network
text: Two cited documents co-cited appear reference list. count shared citing papers.
code:
cocit_pairs <- refs |>
  inner_join(refs, by = "citing_id", suffix = c("_a", "_b"),
             relationship = "many-to-many") |>
  filter(cited_id_a < cited_id_b) |>
  count(cited_id_a, cited_id_b, name = "cocit_count")

cocit_top <- cocit_pairs |>
  filter(cocit_count >= 3)

cat(glue("Co-citation pairs (count >= 3): {nrow(cocit_top)}\n"))#> Co-citation pairs (count >= 3): 135
g_cocit <- graph_from_data_frame(
  cocit_top |> select(cited_id_a, cited_id_b, weight = cocit_count),
  directed = FALSE
) |>
  simplify(edge.attr.comb = list(weight = "sum"))

cat(glue("Co-citation network: {vcount(g_cocit)} nodes, {ecount(g_cocit)} edges\n"))#> Co-citation network: 110 nodes, 135 edges

heading: 17.4.3 Building a bibliographic coupling network
text: Two citing documents coupled share least one reference.
code:
bibcoup_pairs <- refs |>
  inner_join(refs, by = "cited_id", suffix = c("_a", "_b"),
             relationship = "many-to-many") |>
  filter(citing_id_a < citing_id_b) |>
  count(citing_id_a, citing_id_b, name = "shared_refs")

bibcoup_top <- bibcoup_pairs |>
  filter(shared_refs >= 5)

cat(glue("Bibliographic coupling pairs (shared refs >= 5): {nrow(bibcoup_top)}\n"))#> Bibliographic coupling pairs (shared refs >= 5): 73
g_bibcoup <- graph_from_data_frame(
  bibcoup_top |> select(citing_id_a, citing_id_b, weight = shared_refs),
  directed = FALSE
) |>
  simplify(edge.attr.comb = list(weight = "sum"))

cat(glue("Coupling network: {vcount(g_bibcoup)} nodes, {ecount(g_bibcoup)} edges\n"))#> Coupling network: 80 nodes, 73 edges

heading: 17.4.4 Visualisation
text:
Figure 17.1: Co-citation network frequently co-cited references Scientometrics (2020–2023).

Figure 17.2: Bibliographic coupling network Scientometrics articles (2020–2023).
code:
comp <- components(g_cocit)
giant <- induced_subgraph(g_cocit, which(comp$membership == which.max(comp$csize)))
V(giant)$community <- as.factor(membership(cluster_leiden(
  giant, resolution_parameter = 1.0, objective_function = "modularity"
)))
V(giant)$degree <- degree(giant)

set.seed(42)
layout <- create_layout(as_tbl_graph(giant), layout = "fr")

ggraph(layout) +
  geom_edge_link(alpha = 0.1, colour = "grey60") +
  geom_node_point(aes(size = degree, colour = community), alpha = 0.8) +
  scale_size_continuous(range = c(1, 5), guide = "none") +
  scale_colour_manual(values = palette_sci(
    n_distinct(V(giant)$community)
  )) +
  labs(colour = "Community") +
  theme_void(base_family = "sans", base_size = 11) +
  theme(legend.position = "bottom")
comp_bc <- components(g_bibcoup)
giant_bc <- induced_subgraph(g_bibcoup,
                             which(comp_bc$membership == which.max(comp_bc$csize)))
V(giant_bc)$community <- as.factor(membership(cluster_leiden(
  giant_bc, resolution_parameter = 1.0, objective_function = "modularity"
)))
V(giant_bc)$degree <- degree(giant_bc)

set.seed(42)
layout_bc <- create_layout(as_tbl_graph(giant_bc), layout = "fr")

ggraph(layout_bc) +
  geom_edge_link(alpha = 0.1, colour = "grey60") +
  geom_node_point(aes(size = degree, colour = community), alpha = 0.8) +
  scale_size_continuous(range = c(1, 5), guide = "none") +
  scale_colour_manual(values = palette_sci(
    n_distinct(V(giant_bc)$community)
  )) +
  labs(colour = "Community") +
  theme_void(base_family = "sans", base_size = 11) +
  theme(legend.position = "bottom")

heading: 17.4.5 Comparing the two approaches
code:
comparison <- tibble(
  Method = c("Co-citation", "Bibliographic coupling"),
  Nodes = c(vcount(g_cocit), vcount(g_bibcoup)),
  Edges = c(ecount(g_cocit), ecount(g_bibcoup)),
  Components = c(components(g_cocit)$no, components(g_bibcoup)$no),
  Density = c(round(graph.density(g_cocit), 4),
              round(graph.density(g_bibcoup), 4))
)

comparison |> gt()

heading: 17.5 Diagnostics and interpretation
text: Threshold selection: minimum co-citation count shared-reference count determines network density. low threshold creates hairball; high excludes important links. Experiment multiple thresholds report chosen value.Normalisation: Raw co-citation counts favour highly cited papers. Cosine normalisation produces balanced networks. Always report whether counts raw normalised.Giant component ratio: fragmented co-citation network may indicate corpus spans multiple disconnected subfields, threshold high.Temporal stability: Co-citation networks change new papers cite old ones. Bibliographic coupling networks fixed publication time. need stable snapshot recent literature, coupling preferable.

heading: 17.7 Limitations and responsible use
text: Co-citation reflects citing behaviour, truth. Frequently co-cited papers may co-cited represent dominant paradigm, best relevant works. Minority viewpoints may invisible.Reference list quality. methods depend entirely accuracy completeness reference lists. Missing references, formatting errors, DOI mismatches reduce coverage. OpenAlex reference parsing imperfect (Priem et al. 2022).Size effects. Papers long reference lists contribute disproportionately bibliographic coupling. Consider normalising reference list length.evaluative. Co-citation coupling measure relatedness, quality. highly co-cited paper intellectually central, excellent correct (Hicks et al. 2015).

heading: 17.9 Common pitfalls
text: Confusing two methods. Co-citation links cited documents; bibliographic coupling links citing documents. answer different questions citation data.Skipping normalisation. Raw counts produce networks dominated highly cited papers. Always normalise balanced analysis.Ignoring threshold. Reporting results without specifying minimum count similarity cutoff makes analysis irreproducible.Treating coupling dynamic. Bibliographic coupling strength fixed publication. interpret changes coupling networks time evolving relationships — reflect changes corpus, links.

heading: 18.1 Learning objectives
text: completing chapter, able :Explain principles co-word analysis role science mappingExtract clean keywords OpenAlex data (author keywords concepts)Build keyword co-occurrence matrix convert networkApply community detection identify topical clustersVisualise keyword co-occurrence networks interpretable layouts

heading: 18.2 Setup
code:
library(tidyverse)
library(openalexR)
library(igraph)
library(tidygraph)
library(ggraph)
library(glue)
library(gt)

set.seed(20260509)

source(here::here("R", "api_helpers.R"))
source(here::here("R", "utils.R"))
source(here::here("R", "sci_palette.R"))

heading: 18.3 Conceptual background
text: Co-word analysis maps conceptual structure research field examining terms appear together documents. Introduced Callon et al. (1983), method assumes two keywords repeatedly co-occur across publications, thematically related. resulting co-occurrence network reveals topical landscape field: clusters densely connected keywords represent coherent research themes, bridges clusters indicate interdisciplinary connections.method works several types terms:Author keywords: Terms selected paper’s authors. intentional descriptors suffer inconsistency — authors may use different terms concept (“machine learning” vs. “ML” vs. “statistical learning”).Indexed keywords: Terms assigned database indexers (e.g., MeSH terms PubMed). consistent available databases.OpenAlex concepts: Algorithmically assigned topics multiple levels hierarchical taxonomy. provide broad coverage may miss nuanced distinctions (Priem et al. 2022).Title/abstract words: Extracted via text mining. Comprehensive noisy; requires extensive preprocessing.Normalisation important co-word networks. Raw co-occurrence counts favour high-frequency terms. Common normalisations include association strength (equivalent pointwise mutual information) equivalence index (cosine co-occurrence vector). Waltman et al. (2010) demonstrated association strength produces balanced network structures bibliometric mapping.Co-word analysis complements citation-based methods (17.3). Citation networks reveal intellectual influence; co-word networks reveal thematic content. Combining provides complete picture field’s structure.

heading: 18.4.1 Extracting keywords
text:
extract author keywords sample scientometrics research.
Figure 18.1: Top 20 frequent keywords Scientometrics sample.
code:
works <- oa_fetch(
  entity = "works",
  primary_location.source.id = "S148561398",
  from_publication_date = "2019-01-01",
  to_publication_date = "2023-12-31",
  options = list(sample = 500, seed = 42)
)
keywords <- works |>
  select(id, topics) |>
  unnest(topics, names_sep = "_") |>
  filter(topics_display_name != "") |>
  select(work_id = id, keyword = topics_display_name) |>
  mutate(keyword = str_to_lower(str_trim(keyword))) |>
  filter(!is.na(keyword), nchar(keyword) >= 3)

kw_counts <- keywords |>
  count(keyword, sort = TRUE)

cat(glue("Total keyword occurrences: {nrow(keywords)}\n"))#> Total keyword occurrences: 5224
cat(glue("Unique keywords: {n_distinct(keywords$keyword)}\n"))#> Unique keywords: 413
kw_counts |>
  head(20) |>
  mutate(keyword = fct_reorder(keyword, n)) |>
  ggplot(aes(x = n, y = keyword)) +
  geom_col(fill = palette_sci(1)) +
  labs(x = "Frequency", y = NULL) +
  theme_sci()

heading: 18.4.2 Building the co-occurrence network
text: create edges keywords appear paper.
code:
kw_frequent <- kw_counts |>
  filter(n >= 5) |>
  pull(keyword)

kw_filtered <- keywords |>
  filter(keyword %in% kw_frequent)

coword_pairs <- kw_filtered |>
  inner_join(kw_filtered, by = "work_id", suffix = c("_a", "_b"),
             relationship = "many-to-many") |>
  filter(keyword_a < keyword_b) |>
  count(keyword_a, keyword_b, name = "cooccurrence")

coword_top <- coword_pairs |>
  filter(cooccurrence >= 3)

g_kw <- graph_from_data_frame(
  coword_top |> select(keyword_a, keyword_b, weight = cooccurrence),
  directed = FALSE
) |>
  simplify(edge.attr.comb = list(weight = "sum"))

cat(glue("Keyword network: {vcount(g_kw)} nodes, {ecount(g_kw)} edges\n"))#> Keyword network: 112 nodes, 937 edges

heading: 18.4.3 Community detection for topical clusters
code:
V(g_kw)$degree <- degree(g_kw)
V(g_kw)$strength <- strength(g_kw)

communities <- cluster_leiden(g_kw, resolution_parameter = 0.8,
                              objective_function = "modularity")
V(g_kw)$community <- as.factor(membership(communities))

cat(glue("Communities: {length(unique(membership(communities)))}\n"))#> Communities: 2
cat(glue("Modularity: {round(modularity(g_kw, membership(communities)), 3)}\n"))#> Modularity: 0.214
community_summary <- tibble(
  keyword = V(g_kw)$name,
  community = V(g_kw)$community,
  strength = V(g_kw)$strength
) |>
  group_by(community) |>
  slice_max(strength, n = 5) |>
  summarise(top_keywords = paste(keyword, collapse = ", "),
            n_keywords = n(), .groups = "drop")

community_summary |> gt()

heading: 18.4.4 Visualisation
text: Figure 18.2: Keyword co-occurrence network coloured topical cluster.
code:
set.seed(42)
layout <- create_layout(as_tbl_graph(g_kw), layout = "fr")

ggraph(layout) +
  geom_edge_link(aes(width = weight), alpha = 0.15, colour = "grey60") +
  scale_edge_width_continuous(range = c(0.3, 2), guide = "none") +
  geom_node_point(aes(size = degree, colour = community), alpha = 0.8) +
  geom_node_text(aes(label = ifelse(degree > quantile(degree, 0.85),
                                     name, NA_character_)),
                 repel = TRUE, size = 2.5, max.overlaps = 20, na.rm = TRUE) +
  scale_size_continuous(range = c(1, 6), guide = "none") +
  scale_colour_manual(values = palette_sci(
    n_distinct(V(g_kw)$community)
  )) +
  labs(colour = "Cluster") +
  theme_void(base_family = "sans", base_size = 11) +
  theme(legend.position = "bottom")

heading: 18.5 Diagnostics and interpretation
text: Keyword cleaning: Inconsistent terminology inflates vocabulary creates spurious nodes. Standardise spelling, merge synonyms (e.g., “h-index” “hirsch index”), remove generic terms (“research”, “analysis”) co-occur everything convey topical information.Frequency threshold: Including rare keywords produces large, sparse, unreadable networks. Start keywords appearing least 5 papers adjust based corpus size.Community interpretability: community correspond recognisable research theme. communities uninterpretable, resolution parameter may need adjustment keywords need cleaning.Centrality interpretation: High-degree keywords thematically central (used across many contexts). High-betweenness keywords bridge distinct topics may represent interdisciplinary concepts.

heading: 18.7 Limitations and responsible use
text: Vocabulary inconsistency. Author keywords controlled vocabulary. concept may appear multiple terms, fragmenting single node. Merging synonyms requires domain expertise.Algorithmic concepts. OpenAlex concepts assigned machine learning may contain errors — especially fine-grained levels. Always validate topic assignments reading sample papers (Priem et al. 2022).Static snapshots. co-word network represents time-averaged view. Emerging topics publications may invisible. Consider building temporal slices track field evolution.evaluative. Popular keywords indicate research activity, research quality societal impact. equate topical centrality importance (Hicks et al. 2015).

heading: 18.9 Common pitfalls
text: cleaning keywords. Punctuation variants (“co-authorship” vs. “coauthorship”), case differences, trailing whitespace create duplicate nodes. Clean building network.Including stop-keywords. Generic terms like “research”, “study”, “analysis” co-occur everything dominate network without conveying topical information. Remove .Comparing co-occurrence counts across corpora different sizes. Larger corpora produce higher co-occurrence counts mechanically. Normalise use relative measures.-reading small clusters. cluster two three keywords may reflect single paper, research theme. Report cluster size alongside content.

heading: 19.1 Learning objectives
text: completing chapter, able :Explain dense networks require backbone extraction community detectionApply disparity filter extract statistically significant edgesTune resolution parameter Louvain Leiden community detectionCompare community assignments across methods using Normalised Mutual InformationInterpret hierarchical community structure multiple resolutions

heading: 19.2 Setup
code:
library(tidyverse)
library(openalexR)
library(igraph)
library(tidygraph)
library(ggraph)
library(glue)
library(gt)

set.seed(20260509)

source(here::here("R", "api_helpers.R"))
source(here::here("R", "utils.R"))
source(here::here("R", "sci_palette.R"))

heading: 19.3 Conceptual background
text: Bibliometric networks often dense: co-citation bibliographic coupling network, every pair documents shared citation creates edge. corpus 1,000 papers can easily produce network hundreds thousands edges, represent weak coincidental relationships. Applying community detection directly networks yields poor results — algorithms distinguish signal noise.Backbone extraction addresses pruning edges statistically significant, retaining “skeleton” network. disparity filter (Serrano et al. 2009) tests edge null model based local weight distribution endpoints. edge retained weight unexpectedly high given node’s total weight degree. preserves multi-scale structure: high-weight low-weight nodes can retain important connections.alternative simple threshold filtering (remove edges fixed weight), systematically removes connections low-activity nodes, biasing backbone toward already-prominent actors. disparity filter avoids bias using local significance test.backbone extracted, community detection identifies groups nodes densely connected internally externally. Leiden algorithm (Traag et al. 2019) improves Louvain (Blondel et al. 2008) guaranteeing detected communities internally connected. algorithms accept resolution parameter controls community granularity: lower values produce fewer, larger communities; higher values produce , smaller communities. single “correct” resolution — appropriate value depends research question.Fortunato (2010) provides comprehensive review community detection methods. practice, running algorithm multiple resolutions examining hierarchical structure provides richest understanding network organisation.

heading: 19.4.1 Building a dense network
text: construct bibliographic coupling network scientometrics articles.
code:
works <- oa_fetch(
  entity = "works",
  primary_location.source.id = "S148561398",
  from_publication_date = "2020-01-01",
  to_publication_date = "2023-12-31",
  options = list(sample = 300, seed = 42)
)

refs <- works |>
  select(citing_id = id, referenced_works) |>
  unnest(referenced_works) |>
  rename(cited_id = referenced_works)

bibcoup <- refs |>
  inner_join(refs, by = "cited_id", suffix = c("_a", "_b"),
             relationship = "many-to-many") |>
  filter(citing_id_a < citing_id_b) |>
  count(citing_id_a, citing_id_b, name = "shared_refs")

g_full <- graph_from_data_frame(
  bibcoup |> select(citing_id_a, citing_id_b, weight = shared_refs),
  directed = FALSE
) |>
  simplify(edge.attr.comb = list(weight = "sum"))

cat(glue("Full network: {vcount(g_full)} nodes, {ecount(g_full)} edges\n"))#> Full network: 292 nodes, 3577 edges
cat(glue("Density: {round(graph.density(g_full), 4)}\n"))#> Density: 0.0842

heading: 19.4.2 Backbone extraction: disparity filter
code:
disparity_filter <- function(g, alpha = 0.05) {
  el <- as_data_frame(g, what = "edges")
  node_strength <- strength(g)

  keep <- map2_lgl(seq_len(nrow(el)), el$weight, function(i, w) {
    from_name <- el$from[i]
    to_name <- el$to[i]
    k_from <- degree(g, from_name)
    k_to <- degree(g, to_name)
    s_from <- node_strength[from_name]
    s_to <- node_strength[to_name]

    p_from <- (1 - w / s_from)^(k_from - 1)
    p_to <- (1 - w / s_to)^(k_to - 1)

    min(p_from, p_to) < alpha
  })

  subgraph.edges(g, which(keep), delete.vertices = TRUE)
}

g_backbone <- disparity_filter(g_full, alpha = 0.05)
cat(glue("Backbone: {vcount(g_backbone)} nodes, {ecount(g_backbone)} edges\n"))#> Backbone: 129 nodes, 123 edges
cat(glue("Edge retention: {scales::percent(ecount(g_backbone) / ecount(g_full))}\n"))#> Edge retention: 3%

heading: 19.4.3 Threshold filtering for comparison
code:
threshold <- quantile(E(g_full)$weight, 0.75)
g_threshold <- subgraph.edges(
  g_full,
  which(E(g_full)$weight >= threshold),
  delete.vertices = TRUE
)

cat(glue("Threshold (75th pctile = {threshold}): {vcount(g_threshold)} nodes, {ecount(g_threshold)} edges\n"))#> Threshold (75th pctile = 2): 257 nodes, 980 edges

heading: 19.4.4 Community detection at multiple resolutions
text: Figure 19.1: Number communities modularity function resolution parameter.
code:
resolutions <- c(0.5, 0.8, 1.0, 1.5, 2.0)

sweep_results <- map_dfr(resolutions, function(res) {
  comm <- cluster_leiden(g_backbone, resolution_parameter = res,
                         objective_function = "modularity")
  tibble(
    resolution = res,
    n_communities = length(unique(membership(comm))),
    modularity = round(modularity(g_backbone, membership(comm)), 3),
    max_size = max(table(membership(comm))),
    min_size = min(table(membership(comm)))
  )
})

sweep_results |> gt()
ggplot(sweep_results, aes(x = resolution)) +
  geom_line(aes(y = n_communities), colour = palette_sci(2)[1], linewidth = 1) +
  geom_point(aes(y = n_communities), colour = palette_sci(2)[1], size = 3) +
  geom_line(aes(y = modularity * max(n_communities)),
            colour = palette_sci(2)[2], linewidth = 1, linetype = "dashed") +
  scale_y_continuous(
    name = "Number of communities",
    sec.axis = sec_axis(~ . / max(sweep_results$n_communities),
                        name = "Modularity")
  ) +
  labs(x = "Resolution parameter") +
  theme_sci()

heading: 19.4.5 Comparing backbone vs. full network communities
code:
comm_full <- cluster_leiden(g_full, resolution_parameter = 1.0,
                            objective_function = "modularity")
comm_backbone <- cluster_leiden(g_backbone, resolution_parameter = 1.0,
                                objective_function = "modularity")

shared_nodes <- intersect(V(g_full)$name, V(g_backbone)$name)
mem_full <- membership(comm_full)[shared_nodes]
mem_back <- membership(comm_backbone)[shared_nodes]

nmi <- compare(mem_full, mem_back, method = "nmi")
cat(glue("NMI (full vs backbone, shared nodes): {round(nmi, 3)}\n"))#> NMI (full vs backbone, shared nodes): 0.655
cat(glue("Communities in full network: {length(unique(membership(comm_full)))}\n"))#> Communities in full network: 10
cat(glue("Communities in backbone: {length(unique(membership(comm_backbone)))}\n"))#> Communities in backbone: 31

heading: 19.4.6 Visualisation
text: Figure 19.2: Backbone network Leiden communities (resolution = 1.0).
code:
V(g_backbone)$community <- as.factor(membership(comm_backbone))
V(g_backbone)$degree <- degree(g_backbone)

set.seed(42)
layout <- create_layout(as_tbl_graph(g_backbone), layout = "fr")

ggraph(layout) +
  geom_edge_link(alpha = 0.1, colour = "grey60") +
  geom_node_point(aes(size = degree, colour = community), alpha = 0.8) +
  scale_size_continuous(range = c(1, 5), guide = "none") +
  scale_colour_manual(values = palette_sci(
    n_distinct(V(g_backbone)$community)
  )) +
  labs(colour = "Community") +
  theme_void(base_family = "sans", base_size = 11) +
  theme(legend.position = "bottom")

heading: 19.5 Diagnostics and interpretation
text: Edge retention rate: disparity filter typically retains 10–30% edges. Retention 50% suggests network may dense enough require backbone extraction.Isolated nodes: Backbone extraction removes nodes lose edges. Report many nodes lost whether represent biased subset (e.g., low-cited papers).Resolution plateau: modularity remains stable across range resolutions, community structure robust. Rapid changes suggest sensitivity parameter.Singleton communities: Communities one two nodes usually noise. Consider merging nearest larger community excluding interpretation.

heading: 19.7 Limitations and responsible use
text: disparity filter assumptions. assumes uniform null distribution edge weights across node’s connections. Highly skewed weight distributions may violate assumption.Backbone choice affects conclusions. Different backbone methods (disparity filter, noise-corrected, threshold) retain different edges can produce different community structures. Report method alpha level.Resolution researcher decision. objectively “correct” resolution parameter. choice determines granularity analysis justified research question, optimising modularity alone (Fortunato 2010).Communities ground truth. Detected communities algorithmic constructs. reflect structural patterns citation data, necessarily real-world research groups coherent intellectual traditions (Hicks et al. 2015).

heading: 19.9 Common pitfalls
text: Applying community detection unfiltered dense networks. result usually single giant community plus isolated fragments. Always extract backbone first.Using single resolution without justification. default resolution 1.0 arbitrary. Run sweep show sensitivity analysis.Comparing modularity across networks different sizes. Modularity values comparable different networks. Use NMI compare partitions.Interpreting backbone edges “strong” relationships. disparity filter retains locally significant edges, necessarily highest absolute weight. low-weight edge can retained important relative endpoint’s connections.

heading: 20.1 Learning objectives
text: completing chapter, able :Explain principles science mapping applicationsConstruct journal-level co-citation network OpenAlex dataCreate overlay map positions research profile base mapInterpret overlay maps identify research strengths gapsDiscuss limitations global science maps local evaluation

heading: 20.2 Setup
code:
library(tidyverse)
library(openalexR)
library(igraph)
library(tidygraph)
library(ggraph)
library(glue)
library(gt)

set.seed(20260509)

source(here::here("R", "api_helpers.R"))
source(here::here("R", "utils.R"))
source(here::here("R", "sci_palette.R"))

heading: 20.3 Conceptual background
text: science map spatial representation structure science, proximity indicates relatedness. Just geographic map shows layout countries continents, science map shows layout disciplines, fields, research areas. maps constructed bibliometric data — typically citation links journals document sets — visualised using network layouts dimensionality reduction techniques.common base maps use journal-level citation networks. Two journals linked papers one frequently cite papers . Community detection applied networks produces clusters corresponding broad disciplines (biomedical sciences, physical sciences, social sciences, etc.). Waltman et al. (2010) developed methods constructing maps large-scale citation data, resulting visualisations widely used science policy institutional assessment.overlay map (Rafols et al. 2010) takes global science map fixed background plots specific entity’s output top. example, university’s publications mapped journals appeared; overlay shows areas global map university active , intensely. technique powerful visualising research profiles, identifying strategic gaps, comparing institutions countries without reducing activity single number.construction pipeline involves three steps: (1) build obtain base map journals coordinates cluster assignments; (2) map entity’s publications journals base map; (3) scale node sizes proportionally overlay base map.Science maps provide intuitive, holistic overviews research landscapes. However, projections high-dimensional data two dimensions, like projection, introduce distortion. Interdisciplinary journals may appear unexpected locations, layout algorithm’s random seed affects exact positions (Hicks et al. 2015).

heading: 20.4.1 Building a journal co-citation base map
text: construct base map citation flows journals multidisciplinary sample.
code:
works_multi <- oa_fetch(
  entity = "works",
  from_publication_date = "2022-01-01",
  to_publication_date = "2022-12-31",
  type = "article",
  options = list(sample = 500, seed = 42)
)

journal_data <- works_multi |>
  select(id, source_display_name, referenced_works) |>
  filter(!is.na(source_display_name)) |>
  unnest(referenced_works) |>
  rename(cited_id = referenced_works)

cat(glue("Citing papers with journal info: {n_distinct(journal_data$id)}\n"))#> Citing papers with journal info: 478
cat(glue("Unique source journals: {n_distinct(journal_data$source_display_name)}\n"))#> Unique source journals: 408
journal_cited <- journal_data |>
  select(citing_journal = source_display_name, cited_id)

journal_pairs <- journal_cited |>
  inner_join(journal_cited, by = "cited_id", suffix = c("_a", "_b"),
             relationship = "many-to-many") |>
  filter(citing_journal_a < citing_journal_b) |>
  count(citing_journal_a, citing_journal_b, name = "cocitation")

journal_pairs_top <- journal_pairs |>
  filter(cocitation >= 3)

g_journals <- graph_from_data_frame(
  journal_pairs_top |> select(citing_journal_a, citing_journal_b,
                               weight = cocitation),
  directed = FALSE
) |>
  simplify(edge.attr.comb = list(weight = "sum"))

cat(glue("Journal base map: {vcount(g_journals)} journals, {ecount(g_journals)} edges\n"))#> Journal base map: 139 journals, 1082 edges

heading: 20.4.2 Clustering journals into disciplines
code:
comm <- cluster_leiden(g_journals, resolution_parameter = 0.8,
                       objective_function = "modularity")
V(g_journals)$cluster <- as.factor(membership(comm))
V(g_journals)$degree <- degree(g_journals)

cluster_table <- tibble(
  journal = V(g_journals)$name,
  cluster = V(g_journals)$cluster,
  degree = V(g_journals)$degree
) |>
  group_by(cluster) |>
  summarise(n_journals = n(),
            top_journals = paste(head(journal[order(-degree)], 3),
                                 collapse = "; "),
            .groups = "drop") |>
  arrange(desc(n_journals))

cluster_table |> head(8) |> gt()

heading: 20.4.3 Visualising the base map
text: Figure 20.1: Journal co-citation base map coloured discipline cluster.
code:
set.seed(42)
layout <- create_layout(as_tbl_graph(g_journals), layout = "fr")

ggraph(layout) +
  geom_edge_link(alpha = 0.05, colour = "grey70") +
  geom_node_point(aes(colour = cluster, size = degree), alpha = 0.7) +
  scale_size_continuous(range = c(1, 5), guide = "none") +
  scale_colour_manual(values = palette_sci(
    n_distinct(V(g_journals)$cluster)
  )) +
  labs(colour = "Cluster") +
  theme_void(base_family = "sans", base_size = 11) +
  theme(legend.position = "bottom")

heading: 20.4.4 Creating an overlay map
text:
overlay publication profile institution.
Figure 20.2: Overlay map showing Oxford’s 2022 publication profile journal base map.
code:
inst_works <- oa_fetch(
  entity = "works",
  authorships.institutions.id = "I40120149",
  from_publication_date = "2022-01-01",
  to_publication_date = "2022-12-31",
  type = "article",
  options = list(sample = 300, seed = 42)
)

inst_journals <- inst_works |>
  filter(!is.na(source_display_name)) |>
  count(source_display_name, name = "n_pubs", sort = TRUE)

basemap_journals <- V(g_journals)$name
inst_journals_matched <- inst_journals |>
  filter(source_display_name %in% basemap_journals)

cat(glue("Institution journals matched to base map: {nrow(inst_journals_matched)} / {nrow(inst_journals)}\n"))#> Institution journals matched to base map: 7 / 249
overlay_sizes <- tibble(journal = V(g_journals)$name) |>
  left_join(inst_journals_matched, by = c("journal" = "source_display_name")) |>
  mutate(n_pubs = replace_na(n_pubs, 0))

set.seed(42)
layout_overlay <- create_layout(as_tbl_graph(g_journals), layout = "fr")

ggraph(layout_overlay) +
  geom_edge_link(alpha = 0.03, colour = "grey80") +
  geom_node_point(colour = "grey85", size = 1.5, alpha = 0.5) +
  geom_node_point(
    data = layout_overlay |> left_join(overlay_sizes, by = c("name" = "journal")) |>
      filter(n_pubs > 0),
    aes(size = n_pubs, colour = cluster), alpha = 0.8
  ) +
  scale_size_continuous(range = c(2, 8), name = "Publications") +
  scale_colour_manual(values = palette_sci(
    n_distinct(V(g_journals)$cluster)
  )) +
  labs(colour = "Cluster") +
  theme_void(base_family = "sans", base_size = 11) +
  theme(legend.position = "bottom")

heading: 20.5 Diagnostics and interpretation
text: Journal matching rate: fraction entity’s publications match journals base map determines overlay completeness. Low matching rates (< 50%) indicate base map narrow entity’s profile.Layout stability: Force-directed layouts sensitive initial conditions. Always use set.seed() report seed. Consider consensus layouts multiple seeds important maps.Cluster labelling: Algorithmic clusters need human interpretation. Label clusters inspecting journals contain, relying algorithm.Overlay concentration: overlay concentrated one two clusters, institution highly specialised. Dispersed overlays indicate breadth. Neither inherently better.

heading: 20.7 Limitations and responsible use
text: Projection distortion. Two-dimensional layouts compress high-dimensional citation relationships. Journals boundaries disciplines may misplaced. -interpret exact positions.Journal-level aggregation. Mapping publications journals loses within-journal variation. paper Nature biology, physics, chemistry — overlay distinguish.Sample-dependent base maps. base map built small sample. Production science maps (e.g., CWTS journal map) use comprehensive citation data. Treat example illustrative.evaluative. Science maps show research happens, good . empty region university’s overlay map weakness — may reflect deliberate strategic choice (Hicks et al. 2015).Interdisciplinary research underrepresented. Journals disciplinary boundaries may cluster one discipline, making interdisciplinary work partially invisible (Rafols et al. 2010).

heading: 20.9 Common pitfalls
text: Using small base map. base map fewer 50 journals lacks resolution. Aim least 100–200 journals meaningful discipline separation.Comparing overlays different base maps. Node positions transferable different base maps. compare institutions, overlay base map.Ignoring unmatched publications. Publications journals base map simply disappear overlay. Report matching rate.Treating clusters fixed disciplines. Cluster assignments depend algorithm, resolution parameter, data sample. analytical tools, natural categories.

Build overlays institution two different years. research profile shifted toward new areas remained stable?Temporal overlay. Build overlays institution two different years. research profile shifted toward new areas remained stable?Country-level overlay. Instead institution, overlay country’s publication profile. small specialised country (e.g., Singapore) compare large diversified one (e.g., United States)?Country-level overlay. Instead institution, overlay country’s publication profile. small specialised country (e.g., Singapore) compare large diversified one (e.g., United States)?
