heading: 21.1 Learning objectives
text: completing chapter, able :Choose appropriate layout algorithms different network typesExport igraph networks Gephi (GraphML), VOSviewer, Pajek formatsCreate interactive network visualisations visNetworkApply colour size encoding communicates structure clearlyProduce publication-quality static network figures ggraph

heading: 21.2 Setup
code:
library(tidyverse)
library(openalexR)
library(igraph)
library(tidygraph)
library(ggraph)
library(visNetwork)
library(glue)

set.seed(20260509)

source(here::here("R", "api_helpers.R"))
source(here::here("R", "utils.R"))
source(here::here("R", "sci_palette.R"))

heading: 21.3 Conceptual background
text: Network visualisation analytical tool communication device. well-designed network figure can reveal structure — clusters, bridges, outliers — glance. poorly designed one obscures patterns visual noise.Layout algorithms determine node positions. Force-directed layouts (Fruchterman-Reingold, Kamada-Kawai) simulate physical forces: connected nodes attract, nodes repel. work well small--medium networks (~1,000 nodes) produce aesthetically pleasing results. larger networks, DrL (Distributed Recursive Layout) OpenOrd algorithms scale better. trees hierarchies, Reingold-Tilford Sugiyama layouts appropriate. choice algorithm depends network’s structure visual question asked.Visual encoding maps network properties visual channels: node size (degree, centrality), node colour (community, attribute), edge width (weight), edge colour transparency. Effective encoding follows principle proportional ink: visual weight element correspond data importance. Avoid redundant encoding (mapping variable size colour) limit number distinct colours reader can distinguish (typically 8–12 categories) (Fortunato 2010).Interoperability essential single tool excels everything. R igraph ggraph excellent reproducible analysis publication figures. Gephi (Bastian et al. 2009) provides rich GUI exploration layout refinement. VOSviewer specialises bibliometric network visualisation built-clustering. Pajek handles large networks efficiently. practical workflow often involves constructing analysing network R, exporting specialised tool specific visualisation tasks.Reproducibility requires layouts deterministic. Force-directed algorithms start random positions, set.seed() every layout computation. cross-tool reproducibility, export node coordinates alongside network data.

heading: 21.4.1 Building a sample network
text: reuse co-authorship network Scientometrics.
code:
works <- oa_fetch(
  entity = "works",
  primary_location.source.id = "S148561398",
  from_publication_date = "2021-01-01",
  to_publication_date = "2023-12-31",
  options = list(sample = 200, seed = 42)
)

author_data <- works |>
  select(id, authorships) |>
  unnest(authorships, names_sep = "_") |>
  select(work_id = id, author_id = authorships_id,
         author_name = authorships_display_name) |>
  filter(!is.na(author_id))

edges <- author_data |>
  inner_join(author_data, by = "work_id", suffix = c("_1", "_2"),
             relationship = "many-to-many") |>
  filter(author_id_1 < author_id_2) |>
  count(author_id_1, author_id_2, name = "weight")

g <- graph_from_data_frame(
  edges |> select(author_id_1, author_id_2, weight),
  directed = FALSE
) |>
  simplify(edge.attr.comb = list(weight = "sum"))

comp <- components(g)
giant <- induced_subgraph(g, which(comp$membership == which.max(comp$csize)))

comm <- cluster_leiden(giant, resolution_parameter = 1.0,
                       objective_function = "modularity")
V(giant)$community <- membership(comm)
V(giant)$degree <- degree(giant)

author_lookup <- author_data |> distinct(author_id, author_name)
V(giant)$label <- author_lookup$author_name[
  match(V(giant)$name, author_lookup$author_id)
]

cat(glue("Network: {vcount(giant)} nodes, {ecount(giant)} edges\n"))#> Network: 45 nodes, 382 edges

heading: 21.4.2 Comparing layout algorithms
text: Figure 21.1: network rendered four different layout algorithms.
code:
tg <- as_tbl_graph(giant) |>
  mutate(community = as.factor(community))

layouts <- c("fr", "kk", "stress", "drl")
layout_names <- c("Fruchterman-Reingold", "Kamada-Kawai", "Stress", "DrL")

plots <- map2(layouts, layout_names, function(algo, name) {
  set.seed(42)
  ggraph(tg, layout = algo) +
    geom_edge_link(alpha = 0.1, colour = "grey60") +
    geom_node_point(aes(colour = community, size = degree), alpha = 0.7) +
    scale_size_continuous(range = c(0.5, 4), guide = "none") +
    scale_colour_manual(values = palette_sci(
      n_distinct(V(giant)$community)
    ), guide = "none") +
    labs(title = name) +
    theme_void(base_family = "sans", base_size = 11)
})

patchwork::wrap_plots(plots, ncol = 2)

heading: 21.4.3 Exporting to GraphML (Gephi)
code:
out_dir <- here::here("data")
graphml_path <- file.path(out_dir, "coauth_network.graphml")
write_graph(giant, graphml_path, format = "graphml")
cat(glue("Exported GraphML: {graphml_path}\n"))#> Exported GraphML: /home/runner/work/scientometrics-in-r/scientometrics-in-r/data/coauth_network.graphml
cat(glue("File size: {round(file.size(graphml_path) / 1024, 1)} KB\n"))#> File size: 42 KB

heading: 21.4.4 Exporting to Pajek format
code:
pajek_path <- file.path(out_dir, "coauth_network.net")
write_graph(giant, pajek_path, format = "pajek")
cat(glue("Exported Pajek: {pajek_path}\n"))#> Exported Pajek: /home/runner/work/scientometrics-in-r/scientometrics-in-r/data/coauth_network.net

heading: 21.4.5 Exporting to VOSviewer format
text: VOSviewer reads tab-separated map network files.
code:
set.seed(42)
coords <- layout_with_fr(giant)

vos_map <- tibble(
  id = seq_len(vcount(giant)),
  label = V(giant)$label,
  x = coords[, 1],
  y = coords[, 2],
  cluster = V(giant)$community,
  weight = V(giant)$degree
)

vos_network <- as_data_frame(giant, what = "edges") |>
  mutate(
    from_idx = match(from, V(giant)$name),
    to_idx = match(to, V(giant)$name)
  ) |>
  select(from_idx, to_idx, weight)

map_path <- file.path(out_dir, "vosviewer_map.txt")
net_path <- file.path(out_dir, "vosviewer_network.txt")

write_tsv(vos_map, map_path)
write_tsv(vos_network, net_path)
cat(glue("VOSviewer map: {map_path}\n"))#> VOSviewer map: /home/runner/work/scientometrics-in-r/scientometrics-in-r/data/vosviewer_map.txt
cat(glue("VOSviewer network: {net_path}\n"))#> VOSviewer network: /home/runner/work/scientometrics-in-r/scientometrics-in-r/data/vosviewer_network.txt

heading: 21.4.6 Interactive visualisation with visNetwork
text: Figure 21.2: Interactive co-authorship network (zoom, pan, hover details).
code:
# HTML widget — only rendered for HTML output (PDF/EPUB skip this chunk). vis_nodes <- tibble( id = V(giant)$name, label = ifelse(V(giant)$degree > quantile(V(giant)$degree, 0.9), V(giant)$label, ""), title = paste0(V(giant)$label, "
Degree: ", V(giant)$degree, "
Community: ", V(giant)$community), group = as.character(V(giant)$community), value = V(giant)$degree ) vis_edges <- as_data_frame(giant, what = "edges") |> select(from, to, weight = weight) visNetwork(vis_nodes, vis_edges, width = "100%", height = "500px") |> visOptions(highlightNearest = TRUE, nodesIdSelection = TRUE) |> visPhysics(stabilization = FALSE) |> visLayout(randomSeed = 42)

heading: 21.4.7 Publication-quality figure
text: Figure 21.3: Publication-ready co-authorship network labelled high-degree nodes.
code:
set.seed(42)

ggraph(tg, layout = "fr") +
  geom_edge_link(alpha = 0.08, colour = "grey60") +
  geom_node_point(aes(size = degree, colour = community), alpha = 0.8) +
  geom_node_text(
    aes(label = ifelse(degree > quantile(degree, 0.95), label, NA)),
    repel = TRUE, size = 2.5, max.overlaps = 15, na.rm = TRUE
  ) +
  scale_size_continuous(range = c(1, 6), guide = "none") +
  scale_colour_manual(values = palette_sci(
    n_distinct(V(giant)$community)
  )) +
  labs(colour = "Community") +
  theme_void(base_family = "sans", base_size = 11) +
  theme(legend.position = "bottom")

heading: 21.5 Diagnostics and interpretation
text: Layout quality: good layout separates communities visually, minimises edge crossings, distributes nodes evenly. communities overlap heavily, try different algorithm increase number layout iterations.Readability: network dense read, reduce number nodes (filter degree), increase edge transparency, remove edges weight threshold.Colour distinguishability: Test figures greyscale colour-blindness simulators. Viridis-based palettes (used palette_sci()) designed .Export verification: exporting, open file target tool (Gephi, VOSviewer) verify node attributes, edge weights, labels transferred correctly.

heading: 21.7 Limitations and responsible use
text: Visualisation analysis. network figure communication tool. visual impression can mislead layout, filtering, colour encoding obscures structure. Always report quantitative metrics alongside figures.Layout geography. Absolute positions force-directed layout meaningless; relative distances carry information. Never compare positions across different layouts runs without seed.Large networks unreadable. Beyond ~500 nodes, static network plots become unintelligible. Use interactive visualisation, backbone extraction (19.4), subnetwork extraction large data.Export formats lose information. GraphML preserves attributes; Pajek format limited. Always verify exports document lost (Hicks et al. 2015).

heading: 21.9 Common pitfalls
text: Forgetting set.seed(). Without fixed seed, force-directed layouts change every render, making figures irreproducible across HTML PDF.Encoding many variables. Mapping degree size, community colour, betweenness transparency creates visual overload. Choose two visual channels .Using default igraph plots. Base R plot.igraph() produces low-quality figures. Use ggraph publication visNetwork interactive exploration.Exporting without coordinates. export network Gephi without coordinates, Gephi recompute layout, producing different figure R output.

heading: 22.1 Learning objectives
text: completing chapter, able :Build text corpus OpenAlex titles abstractsApply standard preprocessing steps: tokenisation, stopword removal, stemmingConstruct document-term matrix (DTM) document-feature matrix (DFM)Compute TF-IDF weights identify distinctive termsVisualise term frequency patterns across time groups

heading: 22.2 Setup
code:
library(tidyverse)
library(openalexR)
library(quanteda)
library(quanteda.textstats)
library(glue)
library(gt)

set.seed(20260509)

source(here::here("R", "api_helpers.R"))
source(here::here("R", "utils.R"))
source(here::here("R", "sci_palette.R"))

heading: 22.3 Conceptual background
text: Bibliometric metadata — titles, abstracts, keywords — constitutes rich noisy text corpus. Text mining transforms unstructured data structured representations suitable statistical analysis. pipeline typically follows three stages: preprocessing, representation, analysis.Preprocessing converts raw text standardised form. Tokenisation splits text individual words n-grams. Lowercasing removes case variation. Stopword removal eliminates high-frequency function words (“”, “”, “”) carry little topical information. Stemming lemmatisation reduces words root forms (“computing”, “computed”, “computation” → “comput” “compute”). step involves trade-offs: aggressive stemming can merge distinct concepts, minimal preprocessing retains noise.document-term matrix (DTM) document-feature matrix (DFM) fundamental representation. Rows documents; columns terms; cells contain counts weights. Raw term frequencies overweight common words. TF-IDF (term frequency–inverse document frequency) addresses upweighting terms frequent document rare across corpus, highlighting words distinguish one document others.quanteda (Aria Cuccurullo 2017) provides fast, well-designed toolkit text analysis R. corpus → tokens → dfm pipeline integrates naturally tidyverse. large corpora, quanteda uses sparse matrix representations scale millions documents.Text mining complements network-based methods (18.3). Co-word networks reveal term associations; text mining provides frequency distributions, temporal trends, discriminative features characterise field’s vocabulary.

heading: 22.4.1 Building a text corpus
code:
works <- oa_fetch(
  entity = "works",
  primary_location.source.id = "S148561398",
  from_publication_date = "2019-01-01",
  to_publication_date = "2023-12-31",
  options = list(sample = 500, seed = 42)
)

text_df <- works |>
  filter(!is.na(abstract), nchar(abstract) > 50) |>
  transmute(
    doc_id = id,
    text = paste(display_name, abstract, sep = ". "),
    year = year(publication_date)
  )

cat(glue("Documents with abstracts: {nrow(text_df)}\n"))#> Documents with abstracts: 136
corp <- corpus(text_df, docid_field = "doc_id", text_field = "text")
docvars(corp, "year") <- text_df$year

cat(glue("Corpus size: {ndoc(corp)} documents\n"))#> Corpus size: 136 documents
cat(glue("Total tokens: {sum(ntoken(corp))}\n"))#> Total tokens: 32335

heading: 22.4.2 Tokenisation and preprocessing
code:
toks <- tokens(corp,
               remove_punct = TRUE,
               remove_numbers = TRUE,
               remove_symbols = TRUE) |>
  tokens_tolower() |>
  tokens_remove(stopwords("en")) |>
  tokens_remove(c("also", "however", "using", "based", "study",
                   "paper", "results", "research", "analysis")) |>
  tokens_wordstem()

cat(glue("Tokens after preprocessing: {sum(ntoken(toks))}\n"))#> Tokens after preprocessing: 16218

heading: 22.4.3 Document-feature matrix
text: Figure 22.1: Top 20 frequent terms Scientometrics corpus.
code:
dfmat <- dfm(toks) |>
  dfm_trim(min_termfreq = 5, min_docfreq = 3)

cat(glue("DFM dimensions: {nrow(dfmat)} docs x {ncol(dfmat)} features\n"))#> DFM dimensions: 136 docs x 689 features
cat(glue("Sparsity: {scales::percent(sparsity(dfmat))}\n"))#> Sparsity: 92%
top_terms <- topfeatures(dfmat, 20) |>
  enframe(name = "term", value = "frequency") |>
  mutate(term = fct_reorder(term, frequency))

ggplot(top_terms, aes(x = frequency, y = term)) +
  geom_col(fill = palette_sci(1)) +
  labs(x = "Frequency", y = NULL) +
  theme_sci()

heading: 22.4.4 TF-IDF weighting
code:
dfmat_tfidf <- dfm_tfidf(dfmat)

tfidf_by_year <- text_df |>
  group_by(year) |>
  group_keys() |>
  pull(year) |>
  map_dfr(function(yr) {
    docs <- docvars(dfmat_tfidf, "year") == yr
    if (sum(docs) < 5) return(tibble())
    top <- topfeatures(dfmat_tfidf[docs, ], 10)
    tibble(year = yr, term = names(top), tfidf = unname(top))
  })

tfidf_by_year |>
  group_by(year) |>
  slice_max(tfidf, n = 5) |>
  gt() |>
  fmt_number(columns = tfidf, decimals = 1)

heading: 22.4.5 Term frequency over time
text: Figure 22.2: Relative frequency selected terms time.
code:
target_terms <- c("bibliometr", "open", "network", "impact", "collabor")

freq_by_year <- map_dfr(unique(text_df$year), function(yr) {
  docs <- docvars(dfmat, "year") == yr
  if (sum(docs) < 5) return(tibble())
  freq <- colSums(dfmat[docs, ]) / sum(dfmat[docs, ])
  tibble(
    year = yr,
    term = target_terms,
    rel_freq = freq[target_terms]
  )
})

ggplot(freq_by_year, aes(x = year, y = rel_freq, colour = term)) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 1) +
  geom_point(size = 2) +
  scale_colour_manual(values = palette_sci(length(target_terms))) +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = scales::percent_format(accuracy = 0.01)) +
  labs(x = "Year", y = "Relative frequency", colour = "Term") +
  theme_sci()

heading: 22.5 Diagnostics and interpretation
text: Vocabulary size vs. documents: typical DFM far features documents. Trim aggressively (minimum document frequency 3–5) reduce noise computation.Sparsity: Bibliometric DFMs typically 95–99% sparse. normal. Sparse matrix representations keep memory usage manageable.Preprocessing sensitivity: Results change preprocessing choices. Always report stopword list, stemming method, trimming thresholds.Abstract availability: OpenAlex records abstracts. Report coverage rate consider whether missing abstracts introduce bias (e.g., older papers, certain publishers).

heading: 22.7 Limitations and responsible use
text: Abstracts summaries. capture main claims miss nuance, methodology details, negative results. Full-text analysis (9.3) provides richer data harder obtain.Language bias. Text mining tools work best English. Non-English abstracts may poorly tokenised, incorrectly stemmed, excluded entirely. biases results toward Anglophone research (Visser et al. 2021).Stemming conflates concepts. “Stem” “stemming” merged, “cell” (biology) “cell” (spreadsheet). Lemmatisation precise slower.Bag--words ignores context. TF-IDF frequency-based methods treat documents unordered collections words. Phrase meaning (“machine learning” vs. “learning machine”) lost unless use n-grams embeddings (25.3).

heading: 22.9 Common pitfalls
text: removing domain-specific stop words. Generic stopword lists miss terms like “study”, “results”, “paper” dominate academic text without conveying topical information.Applying TF-IDF trimming. Rare terms get extreme TF-IDF scores. Trim DFM first, apply TF-IDF.Comparing raw frequencies across groups different sizes. year 200 papers higher raw counts year 50. Always normalise relative frequency.Stemming titles abstracts (vice versa). Apply identical preprocessing text fields.

heading: 22.10 Exercises
text: Bigram analysis. Build DFM using bigrams (two-word sequences) instead unigrams. meaningful phrases emerge single words miss?Bigram analysis. Build DFM using bigrams (two-word sequences) instead unigrams. meaningful phrases emerge single words miss?Lexical diversity. Compute type-token ratio (TTR) publication year. vocabulary growing diverse standardised time?Lexical diversity. Compute type-token ratio (TTR) publication year. vocabulary growing diverse standardised time?Keyness analysis. Use quanteda.textstats::textstat_keyness() identify terms distinguish one year others. terms characterise 2023 specifically?Keyness analysis. Use quanteda.textstats::textstat_keyness() identify terms distinguish one year others. terms characterise 2023 specifically?Preprocessing sensitivity. Run analysis without stemming. top terms differ? version interpretable?Preprocessing sensitivity. Run analysis without stemming. top terms differ? version interpretable?

heading: 23.1 Learning objectives
text: completing chapter, able :Explain generative assumptions LDA STMPrepare document-feature matrix suitable topic modellingFit LDA STM models select number topicsEvaluate topic quality using coherence exclusivity metricsInterpret label topics using top words representative documents

heading: 23.2 Setup
code:
library(tidyverse)
library(openalexR)
library(quanteda)
library(topicmodels)
library(tidytext)
library(stm)
library(glue)
library(gt)

set.seed(20260509)

source(here::here("R", "api_helpers.R"))
source(here::here("R", "utils.R"))
source(here::here("R", "sci_palette.R"))

heading: 23.3 Conceptual background
text: topic model unsupervised statistical model discovers latent themes (topics) collection documents. topic probability distribution words; document mixture topics. model “reverse-engineers” writing process: given observed word frequencies, infers topics exist distributed across documents.Latent Dirichlet Allocation (LDA) (Blei et al. 2003) foundational topic model. assumes generative process: document, draw topic mixture Dirichlet prior; word position, draw topic mixture, draw word topic’s word distribution. Inference (fitting model) recovers topic-word document-topic distributions best explain observed data.Structural Topic Model (STM) (Roberts et al. 2019) extends LDA allowing document-level covariates (e.g., publication year, journal) influence topic prevalence word usage. valuable bibliometrics expect topics vary systematically time, discipline, publication venue. STM also estimates “content” component lets word usage within topic vary covariate.Choosing number topics (K) consequential modelling decision. topics produce overly broad themes; many fragment coherent themes noise. Quantitative approaches include held-log-likelihood, semantic coherence, exclusivity metrics. practice, running models multiple K values inspecting resulting topics essential.Topic models widely used scientometrics map research fields, track thematic evolution, identify emerging areas, compare topical focus journals, institutions, countries.

heading: 23.4.1 Preparing the corpus
code:
works <- oa_fetch(
  entity = "works",
  primary_location.source.id = "S148561398",
  from_publication_date = "2018-01-01",
  to_publication_date = "2023-12-31",
  options = list(sample = 400, seed = 42)
)

text_df <- works |>
  filter(!is.na(abstract), nchar(abstract) > 100) |>
  transmute(
    doc_id = id,
    text = paste(display_name, abstract, sep = ". "),
    year = year(publication_date)
  )

cat(glue("Documents for topic modelling: {nrow(text_df)}\n"))#> Documents for topic modelling: 92
corp <- corpus(text_df, docid_field = "doc_id", text_field = "text")
docvars(corp, "year") <- text_df$year

toks <- tokens(corp, remove_punct = TRUE, remove_numbers = TRUE) |>
  tokens_tolower() |>
  tokens_remove(stopwords("en")) |>
  tokens_remove(c("study", "paper", "results", "research", "analysis",
                   "also", "however", "using", "based"))

dfmat <- dfm(toks) |>
  dfm_trim(min_termfreq = 5, min_docfreq = 3)

cat(glue("DFM: {nrow(dfmat)} docs x {ncol(dfmat)} features\n"))#> DFM: 92 docs x 495 features

heading: 23.4.2 Fitting LDA
text: Figure 23.1: Top 10 terms per LDA topic.
code:
dtm <- quanteda::convert(dfmat, to = "topicmodels")

lda_model <- LDA(dtm, k = 8, control = list(seed = 42))

lda_topics <- tidy(lda_model, matrix = "beta") |>
  rename(topic = topic, term = term, beta = beta)

cat(glue("LDA fitted with K = 8 topics\n"))#> LDA fitted with K = 8 topics
lda_top_terms <- lda_topics |>
  group_by(topic) |>
  slice_max(beta, n = 10) |>
  ungroup() |>
  mutate(term = reorder_within(term, beta, topic))

ggplot(lda_top_terms, aes(x = beta, y = term, fill = factor(topic))) +
  geom_col(show.legend = FALSE) +
  facet_wrap(~ paste("Topic", topic), scales = "free_y", ncol = 4) +
  scale_y_reordered() +
  scale_fill_manual(values = palette_sci(8)) +
  labs(x = "Word probability (beta)", y = NULL) +
  theme_sci(base_size = 9)

heading: 23.4.3 Fitting STM with year covariate
text: Figure 23.2: Top terms per STM topic, showing FREX (frequent exclusive) words.
code:
stm_dfm <- quanteda::convert(dfmat, to = "stm")

stm_model <- stm(
  documents = stm_dfm$documents,
  vocab = stm_dfm$vocab,
  K = 8,
  prevalence = ~ year,
  data = stm_dfm$meta,
  seed = 42,
  verbose = FALSE
)

cat(glue("STM fitted with K = 8 topics, year as prevalence covariate\n"))#> STM fitted with K = 8 topics, year as prevalence covariate
plot(stm_model, type = "summary", n = 5,
     main = "STM Topic Proportions and Top FREX Terms")

heading: 23.4.4 Topic prevalence over time
text: Figure 23.3: Estimated topic prevalence publication year (STM).
code:
effect <- estimateEffect(1:8 ~ year, stm_model, meta = stm_dfm$meta)

effect_df <- map_dfr(1:8, function(k) {
  eff <- summary(effect, topics = k)$tables[[1]]
  tibble(
    topic = k,
    term = c("intercept", "year"),
    estimate = eff[, "Estimate"],
    se = eff[, "Std. Error"]
  )
}) |>
  filter(term == "year")

ggplot(effect_df, aes(x = factor(topic), y = estimate)) +
  geom_col(fill = palette_sci(1)) +
  geom_errorbar(aes(ymin = estimate - 1.96 * se, ymax = estimate + 1.96 * se),
                width = 0.3) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0, linetype = "dashed", colour = "grey50") +
  labs(x = "Topic", y = "Year effect on prevalence") +
  theme_sci()

heading: 23.4.5 Model diagnostics
code:
coherence <- semanticCoherence(stm_model, stm_dfm$documents)
exclusivity <- exclusivity(stm_model)

diag_df <- tibble(
  topic = 1:8,
  coherence = round(coherence, 2),
  exclusivity = round(exclusivity, 2)
)

diag_df |> gt()

heading: 23.5 Diagnostics and interpretation
text: Coherence: High coherence means top words topic tend co-occur documents. Low coherence indicates “junk” topic mixing unrelated terms.Exclusivity: High exclusivity means top words distinctive topic. Topics low exclusivity share vocabulary topics, suggesting may need split.Coherence-exclusivity trade-: Adding topics generally improves exclusivity may decrease coherence. Plot metrics across K values find balance.Human validation: Always read sample documents assigned topic. topic labelled domain expert, may represent meaningful theme.

heading: 23.7 Limitations and responsible use
text: Topics ground truth. Topic models statistical constructs, natural categories. Different random seeds, preprocessing choices, K values produce different topics. Never claim topic model reveals “” structure field.Abstract-models miss depth. Abstracts compressed summaries. Topics derived abstracts coarser full text.Number topics researcher choice. objectively correct K. Sensitivity analysis across multiple K values essential.Temporal confounds. STM, topic “grows” time may reflect genuine intellectual shift simply increased coverage database recent years (Priem et al. 2022).Replication. Topic models sensitive preprocessing random initialisation. Report parameters fix seeds reproducibility.

heading: 23.9 Common pitfalls
text: preprocessing enough. Rare terms, numbers, generic academic vocabulary produce noisy topics. Trim DFM fitting.Choosing K perplexity alone. Held-perplexity often favours large K values produce uninterpretable topics. Use coherence exclusivity alongside perplexity.Treating topic proportions precise measurements. Document-topic proportions probabilistic estimates uncertainty. report three decimal places.Comparing topics across models. LDA STM may number define topics differently. Align topics content, index.

Plot coherence K select best value. sensitive results?STM journal covariate. Fetch data two different journals. Fit STM journal prevalence covariate. topics differ journals?STM journal covariate. Fetch data two different journals. Fit STM journal prevalence covariate. topics differ journals?Topic labelling. best LDA model, read titles 5 documents highest proportion topic. Assign human-readable label topic.Topic labelling. best LDA model, read titles 5 documents highest proportion topic. Assign human-readable label topic.LDA vs. STM comparison. Compare topics LDA STM K. similar? STM’s year covariate add?LDA vs. STM comparison. Compare topics LDA STM K. similar? STM’s year covariate add?

heading: 24.1 Learning objectives
text: completing chapter, able :Explain RAKE TextRank keyword extraction algorithmsApply keyword extraction bibliometric abstracts using R packagesCompare automatically extracted keywords author-assigned keywordsEvaluate extraction quality using precision, recall, F1 metricsDecide automatic keyword extraction adds value existing metadata

heading: 24.2 Setup
code:
library(tidyverse)
library(openalexR)
library(quanteda)
library(quanteda.textstats)
library(glue)
library(gt)

set.seed(20260509)

source(here::here("R", "api_helpers.R"))
source(here::here("R", "utils.R"))
source(here::here("R", "sci_palette.R"))

heading: 24.3 Conceptual background
text: Keywords serve compact descriptors document’s content. bibliometrics, used co-word analysis (18.3), topic mapping, search. Three sources keywords exist:Author keywords: Selected paper’s authors. Intentional inconsistent — different authors use different terms concept.Indexed terms: Assigned database curators (MeSH PubMed, descriptors WoS). Consistent labour-intensive available databases.Extracted keywords: Automatically derived titles abstracts using algorithms. Scalable consistent may miss domain nuance.RAKE (Rapid Automatic Keyword Extraction) identifies candidate keywords finding sequences content words delimited stopwords punctuation. scores candidates ratio word degree (number distinct co-occurring words) word frequency, favouring multi-word phrases appear specific contexts. RAKE fast unsupervised consider document-level corpus-level statistics.TextRank adapts PageRank algorithm text: words nodes graph, edges connect co-occurring words within sliding window, importance computed iteratively. High-ranking words selected keywords. TextRank captures contextual importance better frequency-based methods computationally expensive.methods extract keywords individual documents. TF-IDF (22.3) provides complementary corpus-level view, identifying terms distinguish document broader collection.Evaluating keyword extraction requires ground truth. Author keywords common benchmark, imperfect: authors may omit obvious keywords include overly specific terms. Agreement extracted author keywords typically 30–50%, considered reasonable given subjectivity keyword assignment.

heading: 24.4.1 Fetching data with keywords
code:
works <- oa_fetch(
  entity = "works",
  primary_location.source.id = "S148561398",
  from_publication_date = "2021-01-01",
  to_publication_date = "2023-12-31",
  options = list(sample = 200, seed = 42)
)

text_df <- works |>
  filter(!is.na(abstract), nchar(abstract) > 100) |>
  transmute(
    doc_id = id,
    title = display_name,
    abstract = abstract,
    text = paste(display_name, abstract, sep = ". ")
  )

cat(glue("Documents with abstracts: {nrow(text_df)}\n"))#> Documents with abstracts: 62

heading: 24.4.2 RAKE keyword extraction
code:
rake_extract <- function(text, n_keywords = 10) {
  toks <- tokens(text, remove_punct = TRUE, remove_numbers = TRUE) |>
    tokens_tolower() |>
    tokens_remove(stopwords("en"))

  dfmat <- dfm(toks)
  freq <- topfeatures(dfmat, n = n_keywords)
  names(freq)
}

text_df <- text_df |>
  mutate(rake_keywords = map(text, \(t) rake_extract(t, n_keywords = 8)))

text_df |>
  head(3) |>
  select(title, rake_keywords) |>
  mutate(rake_keywords = map_chr(rake_keywords, paste, collapse = "; ")) |>
  gt()

heading: 24.4.3 TF-IDF based keyword extraction
code:
corp <- corpus(text_df, docid_field = "doc_id", text_field = "text")

toks <- tokens(corp, remove_punct = TRUE, remove_numbers = TRUE) |>
  tokens_tolower() |>
  tokens_remove(stopwords("en")) |>
  tokens_remove(c("study", "paper", "results", "research", "analysis"))

dfmat <- dfm(toks) |>
  dfm_trim(min_termfreq = 2)
dfmat_tfidf <- dfm_tfidf(dfmat)

tfidf_keywords <- map(seq_len(nrow(dfmat_tfidf)), function(i) {
  row <- dfmat_tfidf[i, ]
  vals <- as.numeric(row)
  names(vals) <- featnames(dfmat_tfidf)
  names(sort(vals, decreasing = TRUE))[1:8]
})

text_df$tfidf_keywords <- tfidf_keywords

heading: 24.4.4 Corpus-level keyword frequency
text: Figure 24.1: Top 20 keywords total extraction frequency across corpus.
code:
all_kw <- text_df |>
  pull(rake_keywords) |>
  unlist() |>
  tibble(keyword = _) |>
  count(keyword, sort = TRUE)

all_kw |>
  head(20) |>
  mutate(keyword = fct_reorder(keyword, n)) |>
  ggplot(aes(x = n, y = keyword)) +
  geom_col(fill = palette_sci(1)) +
  labs(x = "Extraction frequency", y = NULL) +
  theme_sci()

heading: 24.4.5 Comparing extraction methods
text: Figure 24.2: Distribution Jaccard similarity RAKE TF-IDF keyword sets.
code:
overlap <- map2_dbl(text_df$rake_keywords, text_df$tfidf_keywords,
                    \(r, t) length(intersect(r, t)) / length(union(r, t)))

cat(glue("Mean Jaccard overlap (RAKE vs TF-IDF): {round(mean(overlap, na.rm = TRUE), 3)}\n"))#> Mean Jaccard overlap (RAKE vs TF-IDF): 0.504
tibble(jaccard = overlap) |>
  ggplot(aes(x = jaccard)) +
  geom_histogram(binwidth = 0.05, fill = palette_sci(1), colour = "white") +
  labs(x = "Jaccard similarity", y = "Documents") +
  theme_sci()

heading: 24.5 Diagnostics and interpretation
text: Extraction quality: Compare extracted keywords author keywords random sample 20–30 papers. Precision 30% typical.Redundancy: Check near-duplicate keywords (singular/plural, abbreviation/full form). Post-processing merge variants improves quality.Domain specificity: Generic terms (“model”, “method”, “data”) often extracted carry topical information. Consider domain-specific stopword list.Coverage: documents yield meaningful keywords, especially short abstracts. Flag documents fewer 3 keywords extracted.

heading: 24.7 Limitations and responsible use
text: method comprehensive. Keyword extraction captures salient terms misses concepts expressed circumlocution implicit reference. Human keywords remain valuable.Language dependence. RAKE TextRank work best English. Stopword lists tokenisation rules may fail languages.Evaluation subjective. single correct keyword set document. Low agreement methods extracted author keywords necessarily indicate failure.replacement indexing. Professional indexing (e.g., MeSH) follows controlled vocabularies domain expertise. Automated extraction complement, substitute (Hicks et al. 2015).

heading: 24.9 Common pitfalls
text: Using raw frequencies keywords. frequent words document often stopwords near-stopwords. TF-IDF RAKE scoring essential.normalising keywords. “bibliometric” “bibliometrics” keyword. Apply stemming lemmatisation evaluation.Evaluating titles . Titles short reliable keyword extraction. Use abstracts title + abstract combined.Ignoring multi-word terms. Single-word extraction misses important phrases (“citation analysis”, “open access”). Use n-grams phrase detection.

heading: 25.1 Learning objectives
text: completing chapter, able :Explain word document embeddings differ bag--words representationsTrain simple word embeddings bibliometric corpusUse pre-trained embeddings compute document similarityApply UMAP t-SNE visualise document clusters embedding spaceAssess embeddings add value simpler text representations

heading: 25.2 Setup
code:
library(tidyverse)
library(openalexR)
library(quanteda)
library(word2vec)
library(uwot)
library(glue)
library(gt)

set.seed(20260509)

source(here::here("R", "api_helpers.R"))
source(here::here("R", "utils.R"))
source(here::here("R", "sci_palette.R"))

heading: 25.3 Conceptual background
text: Bag--words representations (22.3) treat word independent dimension. “Citation analysis” “bibliometric study” share features despite semantically related. Word embeddings address mapping words dense, low-dimensional vector space semantically similar words close together. word2vec algorithm learns representations predicting words context (skip-gram) context words (CBOW) large training corpus.Document embeddings extend idea whole documents. Simple approaches average word vectors document’s words (mean pooling). sophisticated approaches include Doc2Vec (Paragraph Vector), learns document-specific vectors alongside word vectors, transformer-based models like SPECTER, pre-trained scientific paper titles abstracts using citation-based training signals.scientometrics, embeddings enable several applications:Semantic similarity: Finding papers similar meaning, even use different terminology.Clustering: Grouping papers content embedding space, complementing topic models (23.3).Visualisation: Projecting embeddings 2D UMAP t-SNE create interpretable “maps” corpus.Anomaly detection: Identifying papers semantically distant assigned field cluster.trade-interpretability: TF-IDF features directly readable (word “citation” specific weight), embedding dimensions opaque. Embeddings powerful similarity clustering resist kind term-level interpretation co-word analysis provides.

heading: 25.4.1 Preparing text data
code:
works <- oa_fetch(
  entity = "works",
  primary_location.source.id = "S148561398",
  from_publication_date = "2019-01-01",
  to_publication_date = "2023-12-31",
  options = list(sample = 400, seed = 42)
)

text_df <- works |>
  filter(!is.na(abstract), nchar(abstract) > 100) |>
  transmute(
    doc_id = id,
    title = display_name,
    text = paste(display_name, abstract, sep = ". "),
    year = year(publication_date),
    cited_by_count
  )

cat(glue("Documents for embedding: {nrow(text_df)}\n"))#> Documents for embedding: 106

heading: 25.4.2 Training word2vec embeddings
code:
clean_text <- text_df$text |>
  str_to_lower() |>
  str_replace_all("[^a-z ]", " ") |>
  str_squish()

model <- word2vec(clean_text, dim = 100, iter = 20, min_count = 3,
                  type = "skip-gram", threads = 1)

cat(glue("Vocabulary size: {nrow(as.matrix(model))}\n"))#> Vocabulary size: 1280
if ("citation" %in% rownames(as.matrix(model))) {
  nn <- predict(model, newdata = "citation", type = "nearest", top_n = 10)
  cat("Nearest neighbours of 'citation':\n")
  print(nn)
}#> Nearest neighbours of 'citation':
#> $citation
#>       term1        term2 similarity rank
#> 1  citation   normalized      0.720    1
#> 2  citation  comparisons      0.705    2
#> 3  citation  measurement      0.703    3
#> 4  citation    indicated      0.695    4
#> 5  citation       counts      0.694    5
#> 6  citation    citations      0.689    6
#> 7  citation correlations      0.687    7
#> 8  citation        parts      0.684    8
#> 9  citation          ten      0.684    9
#> 10 citation         rank      0.677   10

heading: 25.4.3 Computing document embeddings via mean pooling
code:
word_matrix <- as.matrix(model)

doc_embed <- function(text, wm) {
  words <- str_split(str_to_lower(str_replace_all(text, "[^a-z ]", " ")),
                     "\\s+")[[1]]
  matched <- words[words %in% rownames(wm)]
  if (length(matched) == 0) return(rep(NA_real_, ncol(wm)))
  colMeans(wm[matched, , drop = FALSE])
}

doc_embeddings <- map(text_df$text, \(t) doc_embed(t, word_matrix))
embed_mat <- do.call(rbind, doc_embeddings)

valid <- complete.cases(embed_mat)
embed_mat <- embed_mat[valid, ]
text_df_valid <- text_df[valid, ]

cat(glue("Valid document embeddings: {nrow(embed_mat)}\n"))#> Valid document embeddings: 106

heading: 25.4.4 Dimensionality reduction with UMAP
text: Figure 25.1: UMAP projection document embeddings, coloured publication year.
code:
umap_result <- umap(embed_mat, n_neighbors = 15, min_dist = 0.1,
                    n_components = 2, ret_model = FALSE)

text_df_valid$umap_x <- umap_result[, 1]
text_df_valid$umap_y <- umap_result[, 2]
ggplot(text_df_valid, aes(x = umap_x, y = umap_y, colour = factor(year))) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.6, size = 1.5) +
  scale_colour_manual(values = palette_sci(n_distinct(text_df_valid$year))) +
  labs(x = "UMAP 1", y = "UMAP 2", colour = "Year") +
  theme_sci() +
  theme(axis.text = element_blank(), axis.ticks = element_blank())

heading: 25.4.5 Document similarity search
code:
cosine_sim <- function(a, b) sum(a * b) / (sqrt(sum(a^2)) * sqrt(sum(b^2)))

query_idx <- 1
sims <- map_dbl(seq_len(nrow(embed_mat)),
                \(i) cosine_sim(embed_mat[query_idx, ], embed_mat[i, ]))

top_similar <- order(sims, decreasing = TRUE)[2:6]

cat(glue("Query: {text_df_valid$title[query_idx]}\n\n"))#> Query: The link between countries’ economic and scientific wealth has a complex dependence on technological activity and research policy
cat("Most similar documents:\n")#> Most similar documents:
walk(top_similar, \(i) {
  cat(glue("  [{round(sims[i], 3)}] {text_df_valid$title[i]}\n"))
})#> [0.927] COVID-19 enabled co-authoring networks: a country-case analysis[0.927] Sample size in bibliometric analysis[0.923] Draw me Science[0.92] A bibliometric methodology to unveil territorial inequities in the scientific wealth to combat COVID-19[0.916] Analysis of the evolution and collaboration networks of citizen science scientific publications

heading: 25.4.6 Embedding clusters vs. citation impact
text: Figure 25.2: UMAP embedding coloured citation impact (log scale).
code:
ggplot(text_df_valid, aes(x = umap_x, y = umap_y,
                          colour = log1p(cited_by_count))) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.6, size = 1.5) +
  scale_colour_viridis_c(option = "C", name = "log(cites + 1)") +
  labs(x = "UMAP 1", y = "UMAP 2") +
  theme_sci() +
  theme(axis.text = element_blank(), axis.ticks = element_blank())

heading: 25.5 Diagnostics and interpretation
text: Vocabulary coverage: Check fraction abstract words appear embedding vocabulary. Low coverage (< 80%) indicates training corpus small preprocessing aggressive.Nearest neighbours: Inspect nearest neighbours key domain terms. “citation” closest “references” “bibliometric”, embeddings capture domain structure.UMAP stability: UMAP stochastic. Run multiple seeds check whether global cluster structure stable. Individual point positions vary clusters persist.Embedding dimensionality: 100–300 dimensions standard. 50, embeddings lose semantic resolution; 300, training becomes slow diminishing returns.

heading: 25.7 Limitations and responsible use
text: Embeddings opaque. Unlike TF-IDF weights, embedding dimensions interpretable meaning. can measure similarity explain two documents similar without additional analysis.Training data bias. Embeddings reflect biases training corpus. corpus overrepresents certain topics perspectives, embeddings .Small corpora produce poor embeddings. Word2vec needs tens thousands documents reliable training. small bibliometric corpora (< 1,000 papers), use pre-trained embeddings instead.UMAP/t-SNE distort distances. methods preserve local structure distort global distances. Documents appear close 2D may similar high-dimensional space (Hicks et al. 2015).

heading: 25.9 Common pitfalls
text: Training documents. Word2vec 200 abstracts produces unreliable embeddings. Use least 5,000 documents switch pre-trained models.Averaging without weighting. Simple mean pooling treats every word equally. TF-IDF-weighted averaging gives weight distinctive terms often produces better document embeddings.-interpreting UMAP clusters. Visual clusters UMAP may reflect projection artifacts. Validate clusters separate method (e.g., k-means full embedding space).Comparing embeddings different models. Embedding spaces aligned across different training runs. Cosine similarity meaningful within single embedding space.

library(tidyverse)

heading: 26.3 Conceptual background
text: emerging topic research area experiencing rapid growth attention — new publications, new keywords, increasing citation flows. Detecting emergence early strategic value researchers, funders, policymakers. distinguishing genuine intellectual emergence database artefacts (journal newly indexed, conference proceedings batch-imported) requires careful methodology.Burst detection formalises intuition keyword topic “burst” frequency increases sharply relative baseline. Kleinberg (2003) introduced state-based model: keyword exists “normal” state (baseline frequency) “burst” state (elevated frequency). algorithm identifies time intervals keyword burst state. Cascading bursts multiple time scales reveal short-lived spikes sustained surges.Temporal topic tracking extends topic models (23.3) fitting models time-sliced corpora using dynamic topic models topic distributions evolve smoothly time. simpler approach uses rolling windows: fit model 3-year window track topic proportions change windows.Novelty transience indicators operate document level. paper novel content (measured embedding distance topic distribution) distant prior literature. paper transient distant subsequent literature — attracted brief attention generate follow-work. Genuinely emerging topics characterised papers novel non-transient: introduce new ideas field continues develop.Growth-rate metrics simplest emergence indicators: year--year growth rate publications containing keyword, acceleration citation rates, increase number distinct institutions working topic. intuitive sensitive baseline effects (keyword going 1 3 papers 200% growth meaningful emergence).

heading: 26.4.1 Preparing temporal keyword data
code:
works <- oa_fetch(
  entity = "works",
  primary_location.source.id = "S148561398",
  from_publication_date = "2015-01-01",
  to_publication_date = "2023-12-31",
  options = list(sample = 600, seed = 42)
)

text_df <- works |>
  filter(!is.na(abstract), nchar(abstract) > 50) |>
  transmute(
    doc_id = id,
    text = paste(display_name, abstract, sep = ". "),
    year = year(publication_date)
  )

cat(glue("Documents: {nrow(text_df)}\n"))#> Documents: 117
cat(glue("Year range: {min(text_df$year)}--{max(text_df$year)}\n"))#> Year range: 2015--2023
corp <- corpus(text_df, docid_field = "doc_id", text_field = "text")
docvars(corp, "year") <- text_df$year

toks <- tokens(corp, remove_punct = TRUE, remove_numbers = TRUE) |>
  tokens_tolower() |>
  tokens_remove(stopwords("en")) |>
  tokens_remove(c("study", "paper", "results", "research", "analysis",
                   "also", "however", "using", "based"))

dfmat <- dfm(toks) |>
  dfm_trim(min_termfreq = 10, min_docfreq = 5)

kw_by_year <- quanteda::convert(dfm_group(dfmat, groups = year), to = "data.frame") |>
  pivot_longer(-doc_id, names_to = "term", values_to = "count") |>
  rename(year = doc_id) |>
  mutate(year = as.integer(year))

cat(glue("Terms tracked: {n_distinct(kw_by_year$term)}\n"))#> Terms tracked: 255

heading: 26.4.2 Growth rate detection
code:
growth <- kw_by_year |>
  group_by(term) |>
  arrange(year) |>
  mutate(
    growth = (count - lag(count)) / pmax(lag(count), 1),
    total = sum(count)
  ) |>
  ungroup() |>
  filter(!is.na(growth), total >= 20)

recent_growth <- growth |>
  filter(year >= 2021) |>
  group_by(term) |>
  summarise(mean_growth = mean(growth), total = first(total),
            .groups = "drop") |>
  filter(mean_growth > 0) |>
  arrange(desc(mean_growth))

recent_growth |>
  head(15) |>
  gt() |>
  fmt_percent(columns = mean_growth, decimals = 0)

heading: 26.4.3 Simple burst detection
code:
detect_burst <- function(counts, threshold = 2) {
  mean_count <- mean(counts)
  sd_count <- sd(counts)
  if (sd_count == 0) return(rep(FALSE, length(counts)))
  z_scores <- (counts - mean_count) / sd_count
  z_scores > threshold
}

bursts <- kw_by_year |>
  group_by(term) |>
  filter(n() >= 5, sum(count) >= 20) |>
  arrange(year) |>
  mutate(is_burst = detect_burst(count, threshold = 1.5)) |>
  ungroup()

burst_summary <- bursts |>
  filter(is_burst) |>
  group_by(term) |>
  summarise(
    burst_years = paste(year, collapse = ", "),
    max_count = max(count),
    .groups = "drop"
  ) |>
  arrange(desc(max_count))

burst_summary |> head(15) |> gt()

heading: 26.4.4 Visualising keyword trajectories
text: Figure 26.1: Temporal trajectories emerging keywords burst periods highlighted.
code:
top_burst_terms <- burst_summary |> head(6) |> pull(term)

burst_plot_data <- bursts |>
  filter(term %in% top_burst_terms)

ggplot(burst_plot_data, aes(x = year, y = count)) +
  geom_rect(data = burst_plot_data |> filter(is_burst),
            aes(xmin = year - 0.5, xmax = year + 0.5,
                ymin = -Inf, ymax = Inf),
            fill = "gold", alpha = 0.3, inherit.aes = FALSE) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 0.8) +
  geom_point(size = 1.5) +
  facet_wrap(~ term, scales = "free_y", ncol = 3) +
  labs(x = "Year", y = "Frequency") +
  theme_sci(base_size = 9)

heading: 26.4.5 Rolling-window topic tracking
text: Figure 26.2: Top terms 3-year rolling window, showing vocabulary evolution.
code:
window_size <- 3
years <- sort(unique(text_df$year))
windows <- years[1:(length(years) - window_size + 1)]

window_top_terms <- map_dfr(windows, function(start_year) {
  end_year <- start_year + window_size - 1
  docs <- docvars(dfmat, "year") >= start_year &
          docvars(dfmat, "year") <= end_year
  if (sum(docs) < 10) return(tibble())
  top <- topfeatures(dfmat[docs, ], 10)
  tibble(window = glue("{start_year}-{end_year}"),
         term = names(top), frequency = unname(top))
})
window_top_terms |>
  group_by(window) |>
  slice_max(frequency, n = 8) |>
  mutate(term = reorder_within(term, frequency, window)) |>
  ggplot(aes(x = frequency, y = term)) +
  geom_col(fill = palette_sci(1)) +
  facet_wrap(~ window, scales = "free_y", ncol = 3) +
  scale_y_reordered() +
  labs(x = "Frequency", y = NULL) +
  theme_sci(base_size = 8)

heading: 26.5 Diagnostics and interpretation
text: Baseline effects: keyword going 2 6 occurrences shows 200% growth may represent meaningful emergence. Set minimum frequency thresholds computing growth rates.Database artefacts: newly indexed journal batch import conference proceedings can create spurious “bursts”. Cross-check independent evidence.Burst duration: Short bursts (1 year) may reflect single influential paper special issue. Sustained bursts (3+ years) likely indicate genuine emergence.Window sensitivity: Rolling-window results change window width. Report chosen width test sensitivity wider narrower windows.

heading: 26.7 Limitations and responsible use
text: Emergence retrospective. time bibliometric methods detect emergence, topic already gained momentum. methods identify “recently emerged” topics, future breakthroughs.Coverage bias. OpenAlex coverage expanded rapidly, meaning recent years records. Growth rates may reflect database growth rather intellectual emergence (Priem et al. 2022).Language emergence. English-language terms dominate; emerging topics non-English literatures may invisible translated adopted English-language publications.predictive. Burst detection identifies past patterns. predict topics continue growing. Many bursts transient (Kleinberg 2003).Strategic gaming. emergence metrics used funding decisions, researchers may strategically choose “hot” keywords, creating feedback loops (Hicks et al. 2015).

heading: 26.9 Common pitfalls
text: Confusing absolute growth relative emergence. field publishes 10,000 papers/year growing 5% adds 500 papers. niche area growing 10 30 papers shows 200% growth. niche area “emerging” large field adding absolute volume.controlling corpus growth. overall number publications corpus grows, keywords grow. Use relative frequency (keyword count / total documents) rather absolute counts.Overreacting single-year spikes. One conference proceedings volume special issue can create one-year spike. Require multi-year sustained growth declaring emergence.Ignoring synonyms. “Deep learning” “neural networks” may refer emerging trend. Merge synonyms analysis.

heading: 27.1 Learning objectives
text: completing chapter, able :Define altmetrics explain complement citation-based indicatorsRetrieve altmetric-related data OpenAlex (open access status, type, counts)Compute correlations citation counts alternative indicatorsInterpret altmetric signals critically — distinguishing attention impactDiscuss limitations altmetrics research evaluation

heading: 27.2 Setup
code:
library(tidyverse)
library(openalexR)
library(glue)
library(gt)

set.seed(20260509)

source(here::here("R", "api_helpers.R"))
source(here::here("R", "utils.R"))
source(here::here("R", "sci_palette.R"))

heading: 27.3 Conceptual background
text: Citations measure one dimension scholarly impact: influence subsequent research. scholarly work can also influence policy, education, clinical practice, public understanding — none captured citation counts. Altmetrics (alternative metrics) attempt fill gap tracking online traces attention: social media mentions, news coverage, blog posts, Wikipedia citations, Mendeley reader counts, policy document references.term coined Priem et al. (2010), argued speed diversity online scholarly communication demanded new metrics beyond Journal Impact Factor. Altmetrics typically faster citations (social media attention peaks within days publication, citations take years) broader (capture engagement non-researchers).However, altmetrics come significant caveats. Social media attention correlates weakly scientific quality. Metrics easily gamed (bots, self-promotion). Coverage uneven: biomedical research attracts far Twitter/X attention mathematics engineering. platforms providing altmetric data (Altmetric.com, PlumX) commercial products proprietary algorithms (Hicks et al. 2015).OpenAlex provides open-access status citation counts directly provide social media metrics. altmetric data, external APIs (Altmetric.com, Crossref Event Data) needed. chapter, demonstrate can done OpenAlex metadata alone discuss extend analysis external sources.

heading: 27.4.1 Fetching data with OA indicators
code:
works <- oa_fetch(
  entity = "works",
  primary_location.source.id = "S148561398",
  from_publication_date = "2020-01-01",
  to_publication_date = "2023-12-31",
  options = list(sample = 400, seed = 42)
)

works_slim <- works |>
  transmute(
    id, display_name,
    year = year(publication_date),
    cited_by_count,
    oa_status = oa_status,
    type,
    referenced_works_count
  )

cat(glue("Works retrieved: {nrow(works_slim)}\n"))#> Works retrieved: 400

heading: 27.4.2 Open access as an altmetric dimension
text: Figure 27.1: Citation distributions open access status.
code:
oa_summary <- works_slim |>
  count(oa_status, sort = TRUE) |>
  mutate(pct = scales::percent(n / sum(n)))

oa_summary |> gt()
works_slim |>
  filter(!is.na(oa_status)) |>
  ggplot(aes(x = oa_status, y = cited_by_count + 1)) +
  geom_boxplot(fill = palette_sci(1), alpha = 0.7) +
  scale_y_log10() +
  labs(x = "OA status", y = "Citations (log scale)") +
  theme_sci()

heading: 27.4.3 Citation count distribution
text: Figure 27.2: Citation distribution sample, showing characteristic right skew.
code:
ggplot(works_slim, aes(x = cited_by_count)) +
  geom_histogram(binwidth = 5, fill = palette_sci(1), colour = "white") +
  labs(x = "Citation count", y = "Number of papers") +
  theme_sci()

heading: 27.4.4 Proxy altmetric: reference list length
text:
Reference list length correlates engagement breadth can serve simple proxy scholarly thoroughness.
Figure 27.3: Relationship reference list length citation count.
code:
ggplot(works_slim, aes(x = referenced_works_count, y = cited_by_count)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.3, colour = palette_sci(1)) +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm", se = FALSE, colour = palette_sci(2)[2]) +
  labs(x = "Number of references", y = "Citation count") +
  theme_sci()
cor_val <- cor(works_slim$referenced_works_count, works_slim$cited_by_count,
               use = "complete.obs")
cat(glue("Pearson correlation (references vs. citations): {round(cor_val, 3)}\n"))#> Pearson correlation (references vs. citations): 0.097

heading: 27.5 Diagnostics and interpretation
text: OA advantage: Open-access articles often receive citations, may reflect self-selection (higher-quality papers likely made OA) rather causal effect open access.Platform coverage: Social media altmetrics biased toward English-language, biomedical, controversial topics. Low altmetric scores indicate low impact.Temporal dynamics: Altmetric attention peaks immediately publication decays rapidly. Citations follow slower, sustained trajectory.Gaming risk: Social media metrics easily manipulated citations. Interpret high altmetric scores caution.

heading: 27.7 Limitations and responsible use
text: Attention ≠ impact. viral tweet paper mean paper scientifically significant. Altmetrics measure visibility, quality (Hicks et al. 2015).Disciplinary bias. Fields active social media communities (public health, climate science) generate far altmetric data fields (pure mathematics, medieval history).Platform dependence. Altmetric data depends commercial platforms whose coverage, algorithms, existence guaranteed.Never use altmetrics individual evaluation. Leiden Manifesto DORA caution using single metric — including altmetrics — proxy research quality (American Society Cell Biology 2012).

heading: 27.9 Common pitfalls
text: Treating Mendeley readers “citations.” Mendeley reader counts reflect saved paper, cited . overlap partial.Comparing altmetrics across fields. paper public health 50 tweets unremarkable; count algebraic topology extraordinary.Ignoring bots self-promotion. social media mentions automated accounts coordinated campaigns. Commercial altmetric services attempt filter , coverage imperfect.Confusing Altmetric Attention Score quality indicator. score weighted sum online mentions, peer-reviewed quality assessment.

heading: 28.1 Learning objectives
text: completing chapter, able :Classify open access types (gold, green, hybrid, bronze) using OpenAlex metadataCompute OA rates year, journal, disciplineTrack growth preprint posting preprint--publication pathwaysAssess data availability statement coverage corpusDiscuss relationship open science practices citation impact

heading: 28.2 Setup
code:
library(tidyverse)
library(openalexR)
library(glue)
library(gt)

set.seed(20260509)

source(here::here("R", "api_helpers.R"))
source(here::here("R", "utils.R"))
source(here::here("R", "sci_palette.R"))

heading: 28.3 Conceptual background
text: open science movement aims make research processes outputs freely accessible. Bibliometric analysis can measure extent ideals realised, track trends time, identify disparities across disciplines geographies.Open access (OA) measurable dimension open science. OpenAlex classifies work’s OA status using Unpaywall data: gold (published fully OA journal), green (available repository), hybrid (OA subscription journal via APC), bronze (free read without explicit open license), closed (Priem et al. 2022).Preprints manuscripts posted publicly peer review. growth preprint servers (bioRxiv, arXiv, medRxiv) accelerated since 2015, dramatic spike COVID-19 pandemic. Bibliometric analysis can track preprint posting rates, time preprint journal publication, whether preprints receive citations non-preprint papers.Data availability statements (DAS) indicate whether data underlying study accessible. prevalence growing journals adopt mandatory data-sharing policies, quality DAS varies: many state “data available upon request” without providing actual access.Measuring open science important policy. Funders increasingly require OA publication data sharing; institutions report OA rates indicators compliance impact. However, Hicks et al. (2015) caution metrics-driven OA mandates can produce perverse incentives (predatory journals, superficial compliance).

heading: 28.4.1 OA rates by year
text: Figure 28.1: Open access rates year OA type.
code:
works <- oa_fetch(
  entity = "works",
  primary_location.source.id = "S148561398",
  from_publication_date = "2015-01-01",
  to_publication_date = "2023-12-31",
  type = "article",
  options = list(sample = 600, seed = 42)
)

works_oa <- works |>
  transmute(
    id, year = year(publication_date),
    oa_status = oa_status,
    cited_by_count
  )

cat(glue("Works: {nrow(works_oa)}\n"))#> Works: 600
oa_by_year <- works_oa |>
  count(year, oa_status) |>
  group_by(year) |>
  mutate(pct = n / sum(n)) |>
  ungroup()

ggplot(oa_by_year, aes(x = factor(year), y = pct, fill = oa_status)) +
  geom_col() +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = scales::percent) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = palette_sci(n_distinct(oa_by_year$oa_status))) +
  labs(x = "Publication year", y = "Proportion", fill = "OA status") +
  theme_sci()

heading: 28.4.2 OA citation advantage
code:
oa_cites <- works_oa |>
  filter(!is.na(oa_status)) |>
  mutate(is_oa = oa_status != "closed") |>
  group_by(is_oa) |>
  summarise(
    n = n(),
    mean_cites = round(mean(cited_by_count), 1),
    median_cites = median(cited_by_count),
    .groups = "drop"
  )

oa_cites |> gt()

  count(oa_status, sort = TRUE) |>
  mutate(pct = scales::percent(n / sum(n))) |>
  gt() |>
  cols_label(oa_status = "OA Status", n = "Count", pct = "Percentage")

heading: 28.5 Diagnostics and interpretation
text: Classification accuracy: OpenAlex OA classification relies Unpaywall, may lag behind publisher changes. Spot-check sample “closed” articles verify truly behind paywall.Bronze ambiguity: Bronze OA means free--read without explicit license. Publishers can revoke access time. treat bronze equivalent gold green.Year effects: OA rates risen time due funder mandates policy changes. Compare year-normalised rates rather pooling across years.Self-selection: Higher OA rates among highly cited papers may reflect self-selection (better papers likely made OA) rather causal OA effect.

heading: 28.7 Limitations and responsible use
text: OA ≠ quality. Open access status property publication venue access model, research quality. Predatory journals often gold OA.Coverage gaps. Green OA via institutional repositories underreported databases. Actual OA rates may higher measured.Data sharing claims vs. reality. data availability statement saying “data available upon request” often results response requests made. Measuring DAS presence overestimates actual data sharing.Equity implications. Gold OA via article processing charges shifts costs readers authors, potentially disadvantaging researchers without institutional funding (Hicks et al. 2015).

heading: 28.9 Common pitfalls
text: Treating OA equivalent. Gold, green, hybrid, bronze OA different implications access, sustainability, cost.Ignoring embargo periods. Green OA articles may 6–24 month embargoes become freely available. “closed” embargo.Double-counting. article available gold OA journal green repository counted . OpenAlex classifies “best” OA type.Using OA rate performance indicator. OA rates reflect policy funding environment, research quality. Mandated OA inflates rates without changing underlying work.

heading: 29.1 Learning objectives
text: completing chapter, able :Explain challenges gender inference author namesApply name-based gender classification assess limitationsAnalyse gender representation authorship positions (first, last, corresponding)Compute gender-disaggregated citation productivity statisticsArticulate gender binary name-based inference introduces systematic bias

heading: 29.2 Setup
code:
library(tidyverse)
library(openalexR)
library(glue)
library(gt)

set.seed(20260509)

source(here::here("R", "api_helpers.R"))
source(here::here("R", "utils.R"))
source(here::here("R", "sci_palette.R"))

heading: 29.3 Conceptual background
text: Research gender disparities science documented persistent gaps publication rates, citation counts, funding success, career progression. Bibliometric analysis can quantify gaps scale, methods used raise serious ethical technical concerns.Gender inference names common approach. Services like genderize.io, Gender API, R package gender assign probabilistic gender (typically male/female) based first names country origin. approach fundamental limitations: enforces binary classification, fails names common across genders, low accuracy non-Western names, misclassifies non-binary transgender researchers. Despite limitations, name-based inference remains widely used self-reported gender data rarely available bibliometric databases.Authorship position carries different significance across disciplines. biomedical sciences, last author typically senior researcher first author primary contributor. fields, authorship alphabetical. Gender analysis authorship positions must account disciplinary conventions.Documented gender disparities include: women underrepresented first last authors fields (Larivière et al. 2013); women’s papers receive fewer citations average, even controlling field year; women less likely invited reviewers editors. patterns reflect systemic inequities, differences research quality.Hicks et al. (2015) American Society Cell Biology (2012) emphasise metrics used ways perpetuate existing biases. Gender analysis bibliometrics aim reveal disparities policy intervention, evaluate individual researchers.

heading: 29.4.1 Extracting author data
code:
works <- oa_fetch(
  entity = "works",
  primary_location.source.id = "S148561398",
  from_publication_date = "2020-01-01",
  to_publication_date = "2023-12-31",
  options = list(sample = 300, seed = 42)
)

authors <- works |>
  select(work_id = id, authorships) |>
  unnest(authorships, names_sep = "_") |>
  select(work_id, author_name = authorships_display_name,
         position = authorships_id) |>
  mutate(
    first_name = str_extract(author_name, "^\\S+"),
    first_name = str_to_lower(first_name)
  ) |>
  filter(!is.na(first_name), nchar(first_name) >= 2)

cat(glue("Author records: {nrow(authors)}\n"))#> Author records: 933
cat(glue("Unique first names: {n_distinct(authors$first_name)}\n"))#> Unique first names: 717

heading: 29.4.2 Simple name-based gender proxy
text: use minimal heuristic demonstration. production work, use validated gender-inference service probabilistic output country context.
code:
common_female <- c("maria", "anna", "li", "sarah", "jennifer", "jessica",
                   "elena", "nina", "laura", "julia", "diana", "sandra")
common_male <- c("john", "david", "michael", "james", "robert", "peter",
                 "mark", "thomas", "paul", "daniel", "andreas", "martin")

authors <- authors |>
  mutate(
    gender_proxy = case_when(
      first_name %in% common_female ~ "female",
      first_name %in% common_male ~ "male",
      TRUE ~ NA_character_
    )
  )

classified <- authors |> filter(!is.na(gender_proxy))
cat(glue("Classified: {nrow(classified)} / {nrow(authors)} ({scales::percent(nrow(classified)/nrow(authors))})\n"))#> Classified: 37 / 933 (4%)

heading: 29.4.3 Gender representation
text: Figure 29.1: Gender representation among classifiable author names.
code:
classified |>
  count(gender_proxy) |>
  mutate(pct = n / sum(n)) |>
  ggplot(aes(x = gender_proxy, y = pct, fill = gender_proxy)) +
  geom_col(show.legend = FALSE) +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = scales::percent) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = palette_sci(2)) +
  labs(x = "Inferred gender", y = "Proportion") +
  theme_sci()

heading: 29.4.4 Authorship position analysis
text: Figure 29.2: Gender representation authorship position.
code:
position_data <- works |>
  select(work_id = id, authorships) |>
  unnest(authorships, names_sep = "_") |>
  group_by(work_id) |>
  mutate(
    n_authors = n(),
    author_position = case_when(
      row_number() == 1 ~ "first",
      row_number() == n() & n() > 1 ~ "last",
      TRUE ~ "middle"
    )
  ) |>
  ungroup() |>
  mutate(first_name = str_to_lower(str_extract(authorships_display_name, "^\\S+"))) |>
  mutate(
    gender_proxy = case_when(
      first_name %in% common_female ~ "female",
      first_name %in% common_male ~ "male",
      TRUE ~ NA_character_
    )
  ) |>
  filter(!is.na(gender_proxy))

position_summary <- position_data |>
  count(author_position, gender_proxy) |>
  group_by(author_position) |>
  mutate(pct = n / sum(n)) |>
  ungroup()

position_summary |> gt()
ggplot(position_summary, aes(x = author_position, y = pct, fill = gender_proxy)) +
  geom_col(position = "dodge") +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = scales::percent) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = palette_sci(2)) +
  labs(x = "Author position", y = "Proportion", fill = "Inferred gender") +
  theme_sci()

heading: 29.5 Diagnostics and interpretation
text: Classification rate: low classification rate (< 40%) means results may representative. Names East Asia, South Asia, Africa particularly poorly served English-trained gender classifiers.Confidence thresholds: Use probabilistic classifiers confidence thresholds (e.g., classify names > 90% probability). Report threshold proportion unclassified names.Disciplinary norms: Authorship order conventions vary. fields alphabetical ordering, “first author” special significance.Sample size: Gender disparities may small. Ensure sample sizes sufficient meaningful comparisons.

heading: 29.7 Limitations and responsible use
text: Gender binary. Name-based inference forces male/female classification excludes non-binary, genderqueer, gender-non-conforming researchers. fundamental limitation, merely technical one.Name-based inference systematically biased. Accuracy lower non-Western names, transliterated names, unisex names. Results always report classification accuracy unclassified rates name origin.Correlation causation. women receive fewer citations, may reflect systemic bias citing behaviour, differences subfield distribution, career interruptions, structural factors — differences research quality.Privacy consent. Inferring gender names without consent raises ethical concerns. Report aggregate statistics, never individual-level gender assignments.Use equity, evaluation. Gender analysis identify systemic disparities policy intervention, never evaluate individual researchers (Hicks et al. 2015; American Society Cell Biology 2012).

heading: 29.9 Common pitfalls
text: Reporting gender ratios without uncertainty. Name-based inference probabilistic. Report confidence intervals sensitivity analyses.Ignoring unclassified names. Dropping unclassified names biases sample toward Western, easily classified names.Assuming authorship order universal. Alphabetical ordering mathematics economics means authorship position analysis meaningless.Conflating gender sex. Bibliometric gender analysis infers social gender names, biological sex. two distinct concepts.

heading: 29.10 Exercises
text: Cross-journal comparison. Compare gender representation across three journals different fields. differences proportion female first authors?Cross-journal comparison. Compare gender representation across three journals different fields. differences proportion female first authors?Temporal trends. Track proportion female first authors year. evidence increasing representation time?Temporal trends. Track proportion female first authors year. evidence increasing representation time?Citation gap. Compare mean citations papers female vs. male first authors, controlling publication year. gap look like?Citation gap. Compare mean citations papers female vs. male first authors, controlling publication year. gap look like?

heading: 30.2 Setup

library(tidyverse)
library(openalexR)
library(glue)
library(gt)

set.seed(20260509)

source(here::here("R", "api_helpers.R"))
source(here::here("R", "utils.R"))

heading: 30.3 Conceptual background
text: Retraction formal mechanism scholarly record corrects . paper retracted found contain fabricated data, serious errors, plagiarism, ethical violations. theory, retraction halt influence flawed work. practice, retracted papers continue cited — sometimes years decades retraction.growth retractions dramatic: dozen per year 1990s 10,000 recent years. increase reflects misconduct detection (plagiarism software, image forensics) publications overall. Retraction rates vary field: biomedical sciences highest absolute numbers, partly larger publication volumes greater scrutiny.Citation persistence studied bibliometric aspect retractions. Studies consistently find retracted papers continue accumulate positive citations (.e., citations treat retracted work valid) long retraction. occurs : (1) citing authors may aware retraction; (2) database retraction notices inconsistently displayed; (3) citations already-published papers retroactively removed.OpenAlex marks retracted works specific type retraction flag, though coverage depends publisher reporting. Retraction Watch database provides comprehensive curated list retractions directly integrated bibliometric databases.Bibliometric analysis retractions must approached carefully. goal improve self-correction mechanism, stigmatise individuals institutions.

heading: 30.4.1 Finding retracted works
code:
retracted <- oa_fetch(
  entity = "works",
  is_retracted = "true",
  from_publication_date = "2010-01-01",
  to_publication_date = "2023-12-31",
  options = list(sample = 200, seed = 42)
)

cat(glue("Retracted works retrieved: {nrow(retracted)}\n"))#> Retracted works retrieved: 200
retraction_years <- retracted |>
  mutate(year = year(publication_date)) |>
  count(year, sort = FALSE)

retraction_years |> gt()

heading: 30.4.2 Citation counts of retracted papers
text: Figure 30.1: Citation count distribution retracted papers.
code:
ggplot(retracted, aes(x = cited_by_count)) +
  geom_histogram(binwidth = 5, fill = palette_sci(1), colour = "white") +
  labs(x = "Citations received", y = "Number of retracted papers") +
  theme_sci()
cite_stats <- retracted |>
  summarise(
    n = n(),
    mean_cites = round(mean(cited_by_count), 1),
    median_cites = median(cited_by_count),
    max_cites = max(cited_by_count),
    pct_uncited = scales::percent(mean(cited_by_count == 0))
  )

cite_stats |> gt()

heading: 30.4.3 Retracted papers by source
text: Figure 30.2: Top journals retracted papers sample.
code:
retracted |>
  filter(!is.na(source_display_name)) |>
  count(source_display_name, sort = TRUE) |>
  head(10) |>
  mutate(source_display_name = fct_reorder(source_display_name, n)) |>
  ggplot(aes(x = n, y = source_display_name)) +
  geom_col(fill = palette_sci(1)) +
  labs(x = "Retracted papers", y = NULL) +
  theme_sci()

heading: 30.4.4 Citation persistence over time
text: Figure 30.3: Mean citations publication age retracted papers.
code:
retracted |>
  mutate(age = 2024 - year(publication_date)) |>
  filter(age >= 1, age <= 12) |>
  group_by(age) |>
  summarise(mean_cites = mean(cited_by_count), n = n(), .groups = "drop") |>
  ggplot(aes(x = age, y = mean_cites)) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 1, colour = palette_sci(1)) +
  geom_point(size = 2, colour = palette_sci(1)) +
  labs(x = "Years since publication", y = "Mean citations") +
  theme_sci()

heading: 30.5 Diagnostics and interpretation
text: Coverage: OpenAlex retraction coverage depends publisher metadata. retractions flagged. Cross-check Retraction Watch comprehensive coverage.Retraction vs. publication date: publication date paper published, retracted. gap dates varies months decades.Positive vs. negative citations: Citation counts distinguish “paper found X” (positive, problematic) “paper retracted Y” (negative, appropriate). Manual checking citing contexts needed.Denominator: Retraction rates require total number publications denominator. Raw retraction counts favour large journals.

heading: 30.7 Limitations and responsible use
text: Retractions equal. retractions reflect honest error, others fraud. reason retraction matters interpretation often coded bibliometric databases.Stigmatisation risk. Listing authors institutions high retraction counts can stigmatising. Report aggregate patterns, individual-level data, unless specifically studying misconduct.Database lag. Retraction notices may take months years propagate databases. Recent retraction data incomplete.Self-correction broader retraction. Corrections, expressions concern, errata also contribute self-correction harder track bibliometrically.equate retractions research quality. Fields scrutiny detect problems. high retraction rate may indicate effective oversight, worse science (Hicks et al. 2015).

heading: 30.9 Common pitfalls
text: Counting retractions without denominators. journal 10 retractions 50,000 papers lower retraction rate one 5 1,000.Assuming post-retraction citations problematic. citing papers may specifically discuss retraction cite paper methodology remains valid despite data issues.Ignoring retraction reasons. Honest errors distinguished misconduct analysis research integrity.Using retraction analysis individual evaluation. Authors retracted papers may victims co-author misconduct. Never use retraction counts performance indicators.

heading: 31.2 Setup
code:
library(tidyverse)
library(openalexR)
library(glue)

source(here::here("R", "api_helpers.R"))
