heading: 31.3 Conceptual background
text: Funding agencies invest billions research want understand investment produces. Funding acknowledgement analysis extracts funder names grant identifiers publication metadata full text, linking grants papers supported.OpenAlex includes funder information many works, derived Crossref’s funding metadata (turn comes publisher-reported FundRef data). Coverage varies: journals participate Crossref’s FundRef initiative good coverage; others may funding data (Priem et al. 2022).Key analytical questions include: many publications funder support? citation impact funded vs. unfunded research? funders frequently co-fund research? funder’s portfolio map onto landscape science?fundamental challenge attribution: paper acknowledging three funders seven authors result contributions . Assigning full credit funder double-counts; fractional credit principled harder implement. Similar issues arise multi-source funding: paper may acknowledge infrastructure grant, project grant, fellowship, different levels contribution.

heading: 31.4.1 Extracting funder data
code:
works <- oa_fetch(
  entity = "works",
  primary_location.source.id = "S148561398",
  from_publication_date = "2020-01-01",
  to_publication_date = "2023-12-31",
  options = list(sample = 400, seed = 42)
)

funded <- works |>
  select(id, display_name, cited_by_count, funders) |>
  filter(map_lgl(funders, \(g) !is.null(g) && length(g) > 0))

cat(glue("Works with funder data: {nrow(funded)} / {nrow(works)} ({scales::percent(nrow(funded)/nrow(works))})\n"))#> Works with funder data: 400 / 400 (100%)
funder_data <- funded |>
  unnest(funders, names_sep = "_") |>
  select(work_id = id, funder = funders_display_name,
         cited_by_count)

cat(glue("Funder-work pairs: {nrow(funder_data)}\n"))#> Funder-work pairs: 509
cat(glue("Unique funders: {n_distinct(funder_data$funder)}\n"))#> Unique funders: 178

heading: 31.4.2 Top funders by publication count
text: Figure 31.1: Top 15 funders number supported publications.
code:
funder_counts <- funder_data |>
  count(funder, sort = TRUE)

funder_counts |>
  head(15) |>
  mutate(funder = fct_reorder(funder, n)) |>
  ggplot(aes(x = n, y = funder)) +
  geom_col(fill = palette_sci(1)) +
  labs(x = "Publications supported", y = NULL) +
  theme_sci()

heading: 31.4.3 Citation impact by funder
code:
funder_impact <- funder_data |>
  group_by(funder) |>
  summarise(
    n_pubs = n(),
    mean_cites = round(mean(cited_by_count), 1),
    median_cites = median(cited_by_count),
    .groups = "drop"
  ) |>
  filter(n_pubs >= 5) |>
  arrange(desc(mean_cites))

funder_impact |> head(10) |> gt()

heading: 31.4.4 Funded vs. unfunded comparison
text: Figure 31.2: Citation distribution funded vs. unfunded papers.
code:
works |>
  mutate(has_funding = map_lgl(funders, \(g) !is.null(g) && length(g) > 0)) |>
  ggplot(aes(x = has_funding, y = cited_by_count + 1)) +
  geom_boxplot(fill = palette_sci(1), alpha = 0.7) +
  scale_y_log10() +
  labs(x = "Has funding acknowledgement", y = "Citations (log scale)") +
  theme_sci()

heading: 31.5 Diagnostics and interpretation
text: Coverage rate: fraction papers funder data depends journal, publisher, field. Report rate interpreting funder statistics.Funder name normalisation: funder may appear different names (“NSF”, “National Science Foundation”, “US NSF”). Merge variants using funder IDs available.Selection bias: Papers funding acknowledgements may systematically different unfunded papers (larger teams, resources). funded/unfunded citation gap reflects selection, necessarily causal effect funding.Multi-funder attribution: Papers acknowledging multiple funders inflate publication count . Consider fractional counting.

heading: 31.7 Limitations and responsible use
text: Coverage incomplete. publishers report funding data Crossref. Missing funder acknowledgements mean research unfunded.Attribution ambiguous. grant acknowledgement specify part work grant supported. Infrastructure grants, salary support, project funding appear identically.Correlation ≠ causation. Funded papers receive citations, may reflect better-resourced labs produce funding applications cited papers.rank funders citation impact. Funders support different fields, career stages, risk levels. Comparing mean citations across funders misleading without field normalisation (Hicks et al. 2015).

heading: 31.9 Common pitfalls
text: Ignoring funder coverage rates. Comparing funder portfolios without accounting differential coverage produces biased results.Treating every acknowledgement equal. paper may acknowledge major project grant minor travel grant. appear identically metadata.Comparing across fields. biomedical funder’s publications higher citation counts humanities funder’s publications due field-level citation norms.Using funder data individual evaluation. Whether researcher funding reflects many factors beyond research quality: field norms, career stage, institutional support, luck.

heading: 31.10 Exercises
text: Funder co-occurrence. Identify papers multiple funders. funder pairs frequently co-fund research?Funder co-occurrence. Identify papers multiple funders. funder pairs frequently co-fund research?Funder portfolios. two major funders, fetch supported publications compare topical profiles using keyword analysis.Funder portfolios. two major funders, fetch supported publications compare topical profiles using keyword analysis.Temporal trends. Track number papers funding acknowledgements year. coverage rate increasing time?Temporal trends. Track number papers funding acknowledgements year. coverage rate increasing time?

heading: 32.1 Learning objectives
text: completing chapter, able :Explain significance patent–paper citations understanding research impactIdentify OpenAlex works cited patent documentsAnalyse journals, fields, institutions produce patent-cited researchDiscuss limitations patent citations indicators technological impactDistinguish direct citation (paper cited patent) indirect influence

heading: 32.2 Setup
code:
library(tidyverse)
library(openalexR)
library(glue)
library(gt)

set.seed(20260509)

source(here::here("R", "api_helpers.R"))
source(here::here("R", "utils.R"))
source(here::here("R", "sci_palette.R"))

heading: 32.3 Conceptual background
text: patent examiner inventor cites scientific paper patent document, creates observable link knowledge production (science) knowledge application (technology). patent–paper citations (also called non-patent literature NPL citations) one quantitative measures science–technology interface.Patent citations serve legal function: define prior art patent’s novelty assessed. patent–paper citations reflect genuine intellectual influence — added examiners legal completeness, inventor actually used cited research. Nevertheless, aggregate level, patent citation patterns reveal fields, journals, institutions produce research feeds technological innovation.biomedical chemical sciences dominate patent–paper linkages pharmaceutical chemical patents routinely cite scientific literature. Physics engineering moderate linkages. Social sciences humanities rarely cited patents, lack impact, impact operates different channels (policy, education, culture).OpenAlex records whether work cited patents provides count, though source completeness data varies. Lens.org database provides comprehensive open mapping patent–paper citations.

heading: 32.4.1 Identifying patent-cited papers
code:
works <- oa_fetch(
  entity = "works",
  from_publication_date = "2015-01-01",
  to_publication_date = "2020-12-31",
  type = "article",
  options = list(sample = 500, seed = 42)
)

works_patent <- works |>
  transmute(
    id, display_name, source_display_name,
    year = year(publication_date),
    cited_by_count,
    referenced_works_count,
    type
  )

cat(glue("Total works: {nrow(works_patent)}\n"))#> Total works: 500

heading: 32.4.2 Citation distribution analysis
text: Figure 32.1: Citation count distribution, highlighting -cited papers potential patent-cited candidates.
code:
p90 <- quantile(works_patent$cited_by_count, 0.90)

ggplot(works_patent, aes(x = cited_by_count)) +
  geom_histogram(binwidth = 10, fill = palette_sci(1), colour = "white") +
  geom_vline(xintercept = p90, linetype = "dashed", colour = "red") +
  annotate("text", x = p90 + 20, y = Inf, label = "90th percentile",
           vjust = 2, colour = "red", size = 3) +
  labs(x = "Citation count", y = "Papers") +
  theme_sci()

heading: 32.4.3 Journals producing highly cited papers
text: Figure 32.2: Top 15 journals number highly cited papers (90th percentile).
code:
works_patent |>
  filter(cited_by_count >= p90, !is.na(source_display_name)) |>
  count(source_display_name, sort = TRUE) |>
  head(15) |>
  mutate(source_display_name = fct_reorder(source_display_name, n)) |>
  ggplot(aes(x = n, y = source_display_name)) +
  geom_col(fill = palette_sci(1)) +
  labs(x = "Highly cited papers", y = NULL) +
  theme_sci()

heading: 32.4.4 Citation impact by document characteristics
code:
works_patent |>
  mutate(high_cite = cited_by_count >= p90) |>
  group_by(high_cite) |>
  summarise(
    n = n(),
    mean_refs = round(mean(referenced_works_count, na.rm = TRUE), 1),
    mean_year = round(mean(year), 1),
    .groups = "drop"
  ) |>
  gt()

heading: 32.5 Diagnostics and interpretation
text: Patent citation coverage: databases provide patent citation data. OpenAlex’s coverage varies field time period. Cross-reference Lens.org comprehensive data.Field normalisation: Biomedical papers far likely cited patents social science papers. Compare patent citation rates within fields, across .Time lag: median time paper publication patent citation 5–10 years. Recent papers time accumulate patent citations.Examiner vs. inventor citations: Patent examiners add citations inventors may used. inflates apparent science–technology linkage.

heading: 32.7 Limitations and responsible use
text: Patent citations narrow measure impact. Much research influences technology without cited patents — consulting, training, open-source software, informal knowledge transfer.Field bias extreme. Humanities, social sciences, mathematics effectively invisible patent citation analysis. Using patent citations impact indicator systematically disadvantages fields (Hicks et al. 2015).Geographic bias. Patent systems vary country. US European patents different citation practices. Analyses based one patent office may generalise.evaluative individuals. researcher whose paper cited patent may involvement technology. Patent citation reflects relevance knowledge, researcher’s intent innovate.

heading: 32.9 Common pitfalls
text: Equating patent citations commercialisation. patent citation means paper prior art, research led product.Ignoring time lags. Comparing patent citation rates papers published 2023 vs. 2010 meaningless — 2023 papers time cited patents.Double-counting across patent families. invention may patented multiple countries. Count patent family level, individual patent level.Using patent counts metric basic science. Basic research definition distant application. Low patent citation counts fundamental research expected, failure.

heading: 33.1 Learning objectives
text: completing chapter, able :Distinguish causal correlational claims bibliometric researchApply difference--differences evaluate effect policy changeUse propensity score matching create comparable treatment control groupsInterpret regression discontinuity designs threshold-based interventionsRecognise assumptions underlying causal inference observational bibliometric data

heading: 33.2 Setup
code:
library(tidyverse)
library(openalexR)
library(glue)
library(gt)

set.seed(20260509)

source(here::here("R", "api_helpers.R"))
source(here::here("R", "utils.R"))
source(here::here("R", "sci_palette.R"))

heading: 33.3 Conceptual background
text: Bibliometrics rich correlations: OA papers cited closed-access papers; funded research receives citations; papers prestigious journals cited . correlation imply causation. OA citation advantage may reflect self-selection (better papers likely made OA), causal effect open access. Separating cause confound essential evidence-based science policy.Difference--differences () exploits policy changes natural experiments. funder mandates OA starting year T, papers funder T serve treatment group, papers similar funder without mandate serve control. causal effect estimated difference trends treatment control groups. key assumption parallel trends: absent intervention, groups followed trajectory.Regression discontinuity design (RDD) applies treatment assigned threshold. example, grants funded score cutoff; papers accepted review score threshold. Comparing outcomes just just threshold isolates causal effect treatment, assumption units near threshold comparable.Matching methods create comparable groups observational data. Propensity score matching pairs treated units (e.g., OA papers) control units (closed-access papers) similar observable characteristics (field, journal, year, team size). reduces confounding eliminate unmeasured confounders.three methods require strong assumptions. bibliometrics, violations common: parallel trends assumption may fail concurrent policy changes; RDD threshold may manipulable; matching address unmeasured confounders like paper quality. Hicks et al. (2015) emphasise quantitative analysis, however sophisticated, replace expert judgement research evaluation.

heading: 33.4.1 Setting up a DiD analysis: OA status and citations
code:
works <- oa_fetch(
  entity = "works",
  primary_location.source.id = "S148561398",
  from_publication_date = "2016-01-01",
  to_publication_date = "2023-12-31",
  type = "article",
  options = list(sample = 500, seed = 42)
)

did_data <- works |>
  transmute(
    id, year = year(publication_date),
    cited_by_count,
    is_oa = oa_status != "closed",
    post_2020 = year >= 2020,
    referenced_works_count
  )

cat(glue("Papers: {nrow(did_data)}\n"))#> Papers: 500
cat(glue("OA: {sum(did_data$is_oa)}, Closed: {sum(!did_data$is_oa)}\n"))#> OA: 205, Closed: 295

heading: 33.4.2 Descriptive comparison
text: Figure 33.1: Mean citations OA status time period.
code:
did_summary <- did_data |>
  group_by(is_oa, post_2020) |>
  summarise(mean_cites = mean(cited_by_count), n = n(), .groups = "drop") |>
  mutate(period = ifelse(post_2020, "2020-2023", "2016-2019"),
         group = ifelse(is_oa, "Open access", "Closed"))

ggplot(did_summary, aes(x = period, y = mean_cites, fill = group)) +
  geom_col(position = "dodge") +
  scale_fill_manual(values = palette_sci(2)) +
  labs(x = "Period", y = "Mean citations", fill = "Access") +
  theme_sci()

heading: 33.4.3 DiD regression
code:
did_model <- lm(cited_by_count ~ is_oa * post_2020 + referenced_works_count,
                data = did_data)

tidy_did <- broom::tidy(did_model)
tidy_did |>
  mutate(across(where(is.numeric), \(x) round(x, 3))) |>
  gt()

heading: 33.4.4 Simple matching comparison
code:
oa_papers <- did_data |> filter(is_oa)
closed_papers <- did_data |> filter(!is_oa)

matched <- oa_papers |>
  mutate(match_year = year) |>
  inner_join(
    closed_papers |> select(year, cited_by_count_closed = cited_by_count),
    by = "year",
    relationship = "many-to-many"
  ) |>
  group_by(id) |>
  slice_sample(n = 1) |>
  ungroup()

cat(glue("Matched pairs: {nrow(matched)}\n"))#> Matched pairs: 205
cat(glue("Mean cites (OA): {round(mean(matched$cited_by_count), 1)}\n"))#> Mean cites (OA): 33.9
cat(glue("Mean cites (closed match): {round(mean(matched$cited_by_count_closed), 1)}\n"))#> Mean cites (closed match): 23.4

heading: 33.4.5 Visualising the parallel trends assumption
text: Figure 33.2: Citation trends OA status time (checking parallel trends).
code:
trends <- did_data |>
  group_by(year, is_oa) |>
  summarise(mean_cites = mean(cited_by_count), .groups = "drop") |>
  mutate(group = ifelse(is_oa, "OA", "Closed"))

ggplot(trends, aes(x = year, y = mean_cites, colour = group)) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 1) +
  geom_point(size = 2) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = 2019.5, linetype = "dashed", colour = "grey50") +
  scale_colour_manual(values = palette_sci(2)) +
  labs(x = "Year", y = "Mean citations", colour = "Access") +
  theme_sci()

heading: 33.5 Diagnostics and interpretation
text: Parallel trends: Inspect pre-treatment trends visually. OA closed papers diverging trends 2020, estimate biased.Covariate balance: matching, check matched pairs similar observable characteristics (year, reference count, journal).Effect size interpretation: statistically significant coefficient mean effect practically important. Report effect sizes alongside p-values.Robustness checks: Run analysis different control groups, time windows, matching criteria. Consistent results increase confidence.

heading: 33.7 Limitations and responsible use
text: Observational data prove causation. causal inference methods applied bibliometric data make assumptions fully tested. Report assumptions explicitly.Unmeasured confounders. Paper quality, author reputation, marketing effort rarely observable bibliometric data strongly affect citations.Treatment definition. “OA” single treatment — encompasses gold, green, hybrid, bronze, different mechanisms.Generalisability. Causal estimates one journal, field, time period may generalise others.Policy implications require judgement. Even well-estimated causal effect one input policy decisions, sufficient basis mandates (Hicks et al. 2015; American Society Cell Biology 2012).

heading: 33.9 Common pitfalls
text: Claiming causation regression coefficients. positive OA coefficient regression prove OA causes citations without addressing confounders.Ignoring parallel trends violations. pre-treatment trends parallel, estimates biased. ignore reporting.Matching covariates. Matching year alone leaves many confounders unaddressed. Include many relevant covariates possible.-interpreting p-values. p < 0.05 mean result practically significant causal assumptions hold.

heading: 33.10 Exercises
text: Extended . Add additional covariates (document type, number authors) regression. OA interaction term change?Extended . Add additional covariates (document type, number authors) regression. OA interaction term change?Placebo test. Run analysis using 2018 treatment year instead 2020. interaction term significant, parallel trends assumption may violated.Placebo test. Run analysis using 2018 treatment year instead 2020. interaction term significant, parallel trends assumption may violated.Matched comparison. Implement propensity score matching (using MatchIt package) year, reference count, document type covariates. Compare matched OA effect unmatched effect.Matched comparison. Implement propensity score matching (using MatchIt package) year, reference count, document type covariates. Compare matched OA effect unmatched effect.

heading: 34.1 Learning objectives
text: completing chapter, able :Define reproducibility indicators explain role research evaluationSearch data availability statements open badges bibliometric metadataAnalyse prevalence reproducibility practices across journals fieldsDiscuss relationship transparency practices citation impactRecognise gap reproducibility signalling actual reproducibility

heading: 34.2 Setup
code:
library(tidyverse)
library(openalexR)
library(glue)
library(gt)

set.seed(20260509)

source(here::here("R", "api_helpers.R"))
source(here::here("R", "utils.R"))
source(here::here("R", "sci_palette.R"))

heading: 34.3 Conceptual background
text: “replication crisis” prompted widespread adoption transparency practices: open data, open code, pre-registration, registered reports. Bibliometrics can measure uptake practices scale, providing evidence policy interventions.Open badges visual indicators (icons) attached published papers certify availability data, materials, pre-registration. Journals like Psychological Science pioneered use. Studies suggest badges increase data sharing rates, though quality shared data varies.Research Resource Identifiers (RRIDs) persistent identifiers research resources: antibodies, cell lines, model organisms, software tools. Including RRIDs methods sections improves reproducibility enabling precise identification materials used. RRID initiative adopted 1,000 journals.Data availability statements (DAS) declare whether data underlying study available find . Mandatory DAS policies become common, many statements say “data available upon request” without providing actual access. presence DAS easy detect bibliometrically; quality actual data sharing much harder assess.Code availability increasingly expected, especially computational fields. Journals like Nature require code availability statements. Platforms like GitHub, Zenodo, Code Ocean facilitate code sharing, DOIs code repositories enable citation tracking.practices measurable proxies reproducibility, reproducibility . paper open badge may unusable data; paper without one may perfectly reproducible. Hicks et al. (2015) warn using transparency indicators mechanical evaluation criteria.

heading: 34.4.1 Analysing data availability patterns
code:
works <- oa_fetch(
  entity = "works",
  primary_location.source.id = "S148561398",
  from_publication_date = "2018-01-01",
  to_publication_date = "2023-12-31",
  type = "article",
  options = list(sample = 400, seed = 42)
)

works_repro <- works |>
  transmute(
    id, display_name,
    year = year(publication_date),
    cited_by_count,
    oa_status,
    type,
    has_abstract = !is.na(abstract)
  )

cat(glue("Works: {nrow(works_repro)}\n"))#> Works: 400

heading: 34.4.2 OA as a transparency proxy
text: Figure 34.1: Proportion open access articles year transparency indicator.
code:
works_repro |>
  group_by(year) |>
  summarise(oa_rate = mean(oa_status != "closed", na.rm = TRUE),
            .groups = "drop") |>
  ggplot(aes(x = year, y = oa_rate)) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 1, colour = palette_sci(1)) +
  geom_point(size = 2, colour = palette_sci(1)) +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = scales::percent, limits = c(0, 1)) +
  labs(x = "Year", y = "OA rate") +
  theme_sci()

heading: 34.4.3 Abstract availability as metadata completeness
text: Figure 34.2: Abstract availability rate year.
code:
works_repro |>
  group_by(year) |>
  summarise(abstract_rate = mean(has_abstract), .groups = "drop") |>
  ggplot(aes(x = year, y = abstract_rate)) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 1, colour = palette_sci(1)) +
  geom_point(size = 2, colour = palette_sci(1)) +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = scales::percent, limits = c(0, 1)) +
  labs(x = "Year", y = "Abstract available") +
  theme_sci()

heading: 34.4.4 Transparency and citation impact
text: Figure 34.3: Citation count OA status, proxy transparency–impact relationship.
code:
works_repro |>
  mutate(is_oa = ifelse(oa_status != "closed", "OA", "Closed")) |>
  ggplot(aes(x = is_oa, y = cited_by_count + 1)) +
  geom_boxplot(fill = palette_sci(1), alpha = 0.7) +
  scale_y_log10() +
  labs(x = "Access status", y = "Citations (log scale)") +
  theme_sci()

heading: 34.4.5 Summary statistics
code:
works_repro |>
  mutate(is_oa = oa_status != "closed") |>
  group_by(is_oa) |>
  summarise(
    n = n(),
    mean_cites = round(mean(cited_by_count), 1),
    abstract_rate = scales::percent(mean(has_abstract)),
    .groups = "drop"
  ) |>
  gt()

heading: 34.5 Diagnostics and interpretation
text: Proxy vs. reality: OA status abstract availability proxies transparency, direct measures reproducibility. Actual data code sharing require checking external repositories.Journal policies: Changes journal policies (mandatory DAS, badge adoption) can cause step changes transparency indicators. Control journal comparing across time.Selection effects: Papers open data may higher quality (authors confident enough share), creating spurious transparency–citation correlation.Field differences: Data sharing norms vary enormously. Genomics shares data routinely; clinical research often due privacy. Compare within fields.

heading: 34.7 Limitations and responsible use
text: Signals substance. data availability statement guarantee data actually available, usable, correct. Badges certify process, outcome.Mandates inflate compliance without improving practice. journals require DAS, compliance rises “available upon request” becomes default, genuine sharing.Reproducibility multidimensional. single indicator captures . Data availability, code availability, methods transparency, pre-registration, statistical rigour contribute independently.penalise fields sharing constrained. Clinical data (privacy), indigenous data (sovereignty), sensitive social data freely shared. Mandating open data policies uniformly across fields inappropriate (Hicks et al. 2015).

heading: 34.9 Common pitfalls
text: Equating open data reproducibility. Data can open undocumented, incorrectly formatted, insufficient reproduce analysis.Counting badges without checking quality. badge means journal certified availability publication time. link may broken data may removed.Ignoring negative results. Reproducibility also requires publishing null results failed replications. poorly captured current transparency indicators.Using transparency metrics performance indicators. Transparency practices valued, mechanically rewarding creates perverse incentives (superficial compliance).

text: DAS detection. papers full abstracts, search keywords like “data availability”, “code availability”, “github.com” abstract text. proportion mention data code sharing?DAS detection. papers full abstracts, search keywords like “data availability”, “code availability”, “github.com” abstract text. proportion mention data code sharing?Cross-journal comparison. Compare abstract availability OA rates three journals. journals higher OA rates also better metadata completeness?Cross-journal comparison. Compare abstract availability OA rates three journals. journals higher OA rates also better metadata completeness?Temporal trends. Track proportion papers abstracts, OA status, high citation counts time. transparency proxies correlated citation impact within years?Temporal trends. Track proportion papers abstracts, OA status, high citation counts time. transparency proxies correlated citation impact within years?

heading: 34.11 Solutions
text: Solutions provided 2.11.

heading: 34.12 Further reading
text: Hicks et al. (2015) — Leiden Manifesto: responsible use metrics, including transparency indicators.Priem et al. (2022) — OpenAlex metadata basis reproducibility analysis.American Society Cell Biology (2012) — DORA: assessment consider research practices, just outputs.

heading: 35.1 Learning objectives
text: completing chapter, able :Explain ad hoc scripts insufficient reproducible bibliometric analysisDefine targets pipeline dependency tracking selective re-executionUse renv snapshot restore R package environmentsUnderstand Docker containers ensure cross-platform reproducibilityStructure bibliometric project long-term reproducibility

heading: 35.2 Setup
code:
library(tidyverse)
library(glue)

set.seed(20260509)

source(here::here("R", "sci_palette.R"))

heading: 35.3 Conceptual background
text: bibliometric analysis multi-step process: fetch data API, clean deduplicate records, compute indicators, build networks, fit models, generate figures. step depends earlier ones. upstream data changes (new API query, updated corpus), downstream results must regenerated — affected parts.Ad hoc scripts (running scripts manually sequence) fail scale: track dependencies, re-run everything even one input changed, break run different machine different package versions.targets R pipeline tool solves dependency problem. define analysis step “target” (function takes inputs produces outputs). targets builds dependency graph, executes targets whose inputs changed, caches results. faster (unnecessary re-computation) safer (stale intermediate results).renv snapshots exact package versions used analysis renv.lock file. collaborator (future self) can restore identical package environment renv::restore(). eliminates “works machine” problem R packages.Docker takes reproducibility containerising entire compute environment: operating system, R version, system libraries, package versions. Dockerfile defines environment declaratively; anyone Docker can rebuild identically. Docker essential long-term reproducibility, since R versions system libraries evolve.tools complement : targets manages computation, renv manages packages, Docker manages environment. Together, make bibliometric analysis fully reproducible years originally run.

heading: 35.4.1 Defining a targets pipeline
text: _targets.R file defines pipeline. show structure typical bibliometric analysis.

heading: 35.4.2 Running and inspecting the pipeline
code:
# Run the pipeline
targets::tar_make()

# Visualise the dependency graph
targets::tar_visnetwork()

# Read a specific target
targets::tar_read(citation_stats)

heading: 35.4.3 Project structure
code:
# Recommended project layout:
#
# my-bibliometric-project/
# ├── _targets.R          # Pipeline definition
# ├── R/                  # Helper functions
# │   ├── fetch.R
# │   ├── clean.R
# │   └── analyse.R
# ├── renv.lock           # Package snapshot
# ├── Dockerfile          # Environment definition
# ├── data/               # Cached data (Parquet)
# ├── output/             # Figures and tables
# └── report.qmd          # Quarto report consuming targets

heading: 35.4.4 Using renv
code:
# Initialise renv in a new project
renv::init()

# Snapshot current package state
renv::snapshot()

# Restore packages on a new machine
renv::restore()

heading: 35.4.5 Dockerfile for bibliometric analysis
code:
# Example Dockerfile (not R code — shown for reference)
#
# FROM rocker/verse:4.4.1
# RUN install2.r targets renv openalexR igraph
# COPY renv.lock /project/renv.lock
# WORKDIR /project
# RUN R -e "renv::restore()"
# CMD ["R", "-e", "targets::tar_make()"]

heading: 35.5 Diagnostics and interpretation
text: Target status: tar_outdated() lists targets need re-running. everything date, tar_make() completes instantly.Pipeline visualisation: tar_visnetwork() shows dependency graph. Disconnected subgraphs indicate independent analysis streams.Cache management: Targets stores cached results _targets/. directory can grow large. Use tar_prune() remove unused targets.renv consistency: Run renv::status() check lockfile matches installed packages.

heading: 35.7 Limitations and responsible use
text: API rate limits: targets re-run API calls inputs change. Use file-based caching (Parquet) avoid hitting API rate limits every pipeline run.Docker learning curve: Docker adds complexity. solo projects, renv alone may suffice. Docker becomes essential team reproducibility long-term archiving.Overhead small projects: 50-line analysis script need targets. Use pipelines analysis 5+ interdependent steps re-run multiple times.Reproducibility eternal: Even Docker, operating system patches hardware changes can affect results. Archive final outputs alongside pipeline.

heading: 35.9 Common pitfalls
text: separating data retrieval analysis. API calls analysis targets re-fetch data every change. Separate fetching target file-based caching.Forgetting commit renv.lock. lockfile reproducibility guarantee. Always version-control .**Ignoring _targets/ directory .gitignore.** Cached results committed git (can regenerated). Add _targets/ .gitignore.Writing monolithic targets. target one thing. Fine-grained targets enable selective re-execution.

Initialise renv project, install new package, snapshot. Delete package restore lockfile.renv snapshot. Initialise renv project, install new package, snapshot. Delete package restore lockfile.Branching. Use tar_target() dynamic branching run analysis across multiple journals parallel.Branching. Use tar_target() dynamic branching run analysis across multiple journals parallel.

heading: 36.1 Learning objectives
text: completing chapter, able :Create Quarto dashboard layout multiple panels pagesConvert ggplot2 figures interactive plotly chartsBuild searchable, filterable data tables DT packageDesign effective dashboard layouts bibliometric data explorationDeploy dashboards self-contained HTML files

heading: 36.2 Setup
code:
library(tidyverse)
library(openalexR)
library(plotly)
library(DT)
library(glue)

set.seed(20260509)

source(here::here("R", "api_helpers.R"))
source(here::here("R", "utils.R"))
source(here::here("R", "sci_palette.R"))

heading: 36.3 Conceptual background
text: Bibliometric analysis often produces data can communicated static figures tables. Interactive dashboards allow stakeholders — research managers, librarians, policy makers — explore data terms: filtering year, hovering details, sorting tables, drilling specific subsets.Quarto dashboards use format: dashboard output type arrange content cards, rows, columns. support static interactive content side side, can deployed self-contained HTML files (server needed) server-backed applications.plotly converts ggplot2 figures interactive JavaScript visualisations hover tooltips, zooming, panning, selection. ggplotly() function provides zero-effort upgrade path static interactive figures, though fine-tuning often requires direct plotly syntax.DT (DataTables) renders data frames interactive HTML tables search, sort, pagination, filtering. bibliometric data, DT invaluable: users can search specific authors, sort citation count, filter year — without writing code.key design principle dashboards progressive disclosure: show important summary first, let users drill details. Avoid overwhelming users every metric .

heading: 36.4.1 Preparing dashboard data
code:
works <- oa_fetch(
  entity = "works",
  primary_location.source.id = "S148561398",
  from_publication_date = "2019-01-01",
  to_publication_date = "2023-12-31",
  options = list(sample = 500, seed = 42)
)

dash_data <- works |>
  transmute(
    id, title = display_name,
    year = year(publication_date),
    cited_by_count, oa_status, type, source_display_name
  )

cat(glue("Dashboard data: {nrow(dash_data)} records\n"))#> Dashboard data: 500 records

heading: 36.4.2 Interactive trend chart
text: Figure 36.1: Interactive publication trend hover details.
code:
# Interactive plotly widget — only rendered for HTML output.
trend <- dash_data |>
  count(year)

p <- ggplot(trend, aes(x = year, y = n)) +
  geom_line(colour = palette_sci(1), linewidth = 1) +
  geom_point(colour = palette_sci(1), size = 3) +
  labs(x = "Year", y = "Publications") +
  theme_sci()

ggplotly(p)

heading: 36.4.3 Interactive citation distribution
text: Figure 36.2: Interactive citation distribution hover details.
code:
# Interactive plotly widget — only rendered for HTML output.
p2 <- ggplot(dash_data, aes(x = cited_by_count)) +
  geom_histogram(binwidth = 5, fill = palette_sci(1), colour = "white") +
  labs(x = "Citation count", y = "Papers") +
  theme_sci()

ggplotly(p2)

heading: 36.4.4 Searchable data table
code:
# Interactive DT widget — only rendered for HTML output.
dash_data |>
  select(title, year, cited_by_count, oa_status) |>
  arrange(desc(cited_by_count)) |>
  datatable(
    options = list(pageLength = 10, searchHighlight = TRUE),
    filter = "top",
    rownames = FALSE
  )

heading: 36.4.5 OA status breakdown (interactive)
text: Figure 36.3: Interactive OA status breakdown year.
code:
# Interactive plotly widget — only rendered for HTML output.
oa_trend <- dash_data |>
  count(year, oa_status)

p3 <- ggplot(oa_trend, aes(x = factor(year), y = n, fill = oa_status)) +
  geom_col() +
  scale_fill_manual(values = palette_sci(n_distinct(oa_trend$oa_status))) +
  labs(x = "Year", y = "Count", fill = "OA Status") +
  theme_sci()

ggplotly(p3)

heading: 36.5 Diagnostics and interpretation
text: Performance: Large datasets (> 10,000 rows) can make plotly DT slow. Aggregate sample data rendering interactive widgets.Accessibility: Interactive figures accessible screen readers. Always provide alt text consider static fallbacks.File size: Self-contained HTML dashboards embed data JavaScript libraries. Files can reach 5–50 MB complex dashboards.Browser compatibility: Test Chrome, Firefox, Safari. plotly features render differently across browsers.

heading: 36.7 Limitations and responsible use
text: Interactivity analysis. dashboard lets users explore data, exploration without statistical rigour can produce misleading impressions. Pair dashboards proper analysis.Dashboards can mislead. Default axis ranges, binning, colour choices affect interpretation. Design dashboards prevent misreading, enable .Data privacy. Interactive tables may expose individual-level data (author names, paper titles). Consider whether dashboard’s audience see level detail.Maintenance burden. Dashboards query live APIs need updating APIs change. Static dashboards based cached data sustainable (Hicks et al. 2015).

heading: 36.9 Common pitfalls
text: many widgets. dashboard 15 interactive panels overwhelming. Start 3–5 add users need .filtering large datasets. Rendering 50,000 rows DT 100,000 points plotly crash browser. Pre-aggregate.Ignoring PDF fallbacks. Quarto book renders PDF, interactive widgets become static. Ensure static version still informative.Using ggplotly without cleanup. ggplotly() produces verbose tooltips. Customise hover text tooltip parameter direct plotly.

Create Quarto dashboard (format: dashboard) two pages: “Overview” (trend + citation distribution) “Details” (searchable table).Linked brushing. Use plotly’s selection features highlight papers scatter plot simultaneously filter DT table .Linked brushing. Use plotly’s selection features highlight papers scatter plot simultaneously filter DT table .Custom tooltips. Modify trend chart show “Year: 2023, Papers: 87, Mean cites: 12.3” hover instead default tooltip.Custom tooltips. Modify trend chart show “Year: 2023, Papers: 87, Mean cites: 12.3” hover instead default tooltip.

heading: 37.1 Learning objectives
text: completing chapter, able :Define parameters Quarto document’s YAML headerAccess parameters R code chunks drive data retrieval analysisRender parameterized reports command line scriptsBuild template journal-level bibliometric profilesGenerate multiple reports batch using purrr shell loops

heading: 37.2 Setup
code:
library(tidyverse)
library(openalexR)
library(glue)
library(gt)

set.seed(20260509)

source(here::here("R", "api_helpers.R"))
source(here::here("R", "utils.R"))
source(here::here("R", "sci_palette.R"))

heading: 37.3 Conceptual background
text: Many bibliometric tasks repetitive: generating analysis 10 journals, 5 departments, 20 funders. Writing separate scripts entity error-prone hard maintain. Parameterized reports solve defining variables (parameters) change report’s content without changing structure.Quarto supports parameters via params field YAML header. Parameters can strings, numbers, lists. Inside document, params$journal_id (params$institution) drives data fetching, filtering, labelling. render time, parameter values passed via command line rendering script.approach scales naturally: loop purrr::walk() call renders dozens reports different parameters, producing complete portfolio bibliometric profiles consistent methodology formatting.Parameterized reports especially valuable institutional reporting (annual bibliometric profiles department), journal analytics (quarterly reports publisher’s journal portfolio), funding assessment (per-grant output summaries).

heading: 37.4.1 Defining parameters
text: parameterized Quarto document starts parameters YAML header:
code:
# Example YAML header (not executable R):
#
# ---
# title: "Journal Bibliometric Profile"
# params:
#   journal_id: "S148561398"
#   journal_name: "Scientometrics"
#   start_year: 2020
#   end_year: 2023
# ---

heading: 37.4.2 Using parameters in code
code:
journal_id <- "S148561398"
journal_name <- "Scientometrics"
start_year <- 2020
end_year <- 2023
works <- oa_fetch(
  entity = "works",
  primary_location.source.id = journal_id,
  from_publication_date = paste0(start_year, "-01-01"),
  to_publication_date = paste0(end_year, "-12-31"),
  type = "article",
  options = list(sample = 300, seed = 42)
)

cat(glue("Journal: {journal_name}\n"))#> Journal: Scientometrics
cat(glue("Period: {start_year}--{end_year}\n"))#> Period: 2020--2023
cat(glue("Articles retrieved: {nrow(works)}\n"))#> Articles retrieved: 300

heading: 37.4.3 Automated summary statistics
code:
summary_stats <- works |>
  summarise(
    total_articles = n(),
    mean_citations = round(mean(cited_by_count), 1),
    median_citations = median(cited_by_count),
    h_index = compute_h_index(cited_by_count),
    oa_rate = round(mean(oa_status != "closed", na.rm = TRUE) * 100, 1)
  ) |>
  mutate(across(everything(), as.character)) |>
  pivot_longer(everything(), names_to = "Metric", values_to = "Value")

summary_stats |> gt()

heading: 37.4.4 Parameterized figure
text: Figure 37.1: Annual publication count selected journal.
code:
works |>
  mutate(year = year(publication_date)) |>
  count(year) |>
  ggplot(aes(x = factor(year), y = n)) +
  geom_col(fill = palette_sci(1)) +
  labs(x = "Year", y = "Articles",
       title = glue("{journal_name} — Annual Output")) +
  theme_sci()

heading: 37.4.5 Batch rendering
code:
# Render for multiple journals:
journals <- tibble(
  journal_id = c("S148561398", "S202381698", "S137773608"),
  journal_name = c("Scientometrics", "PLOS ONE", "Nature")
)

walk2(journals$journal_id, journals$journal_name, function(jid, jname) {
  quarto::quarto_render(
    input = "journal_profile.qmd",
    execute_params = list(
      journal_id = jid,
      journal_name = jname,
      start_year = 2020,
      end_year = 2023
    ),
    output_file = glue("reports/{jname}_profile.html")
  )
})

heading: 37.5 Diagnostics and interpretation
text: Parameter validation: Check parameters produce valid API queries. Invalid journal IDs return empty results silently.Sample consistency: using options = list(sample = N), sample changes underlying corpus changes. Use seed stability.Output naming: Automated file naming avoid special characters. Sanitise journal names using file paths.Error handling: Batch rendering catch log errors individual reports without stopping entire batch.

heading: 37.7 Limitations and responsible use
text: Automated ≠ mindless. Parameterized reports produce consistent output still require human review. automated report data error produce wrong results consistently across entities.One template fits ? Different entities may need different analyses. template designed large journal may produce misleading results small one (e.g., sparse yearly bins).API costs. Batch rendering 100 journals makes 100 API calls. Respect rate limits cache results.Report fatigue. Generating many reports can overwhelm decision-makers. Produce reports entities need , every possible parameter value (Hicks et al. 2015).

heading: 37.9 Common pitfalls
text: Hardcoding values parameters. find editing date ID script, parameter.testing extreme parameters. journal 5 papers per year needs different visualisations one 5,000. Test edge cases.Rendering without caching. render re-executes code. Use Quarto’s cache: true file-based caching avoid redundant API calls.Forgetting output format compatibility. Interactive widgets work HTML PDF. Ensure template degrades gracefully.

heading: 37.10 Exercises
text: Institutional template. Create parameterized Quarto document institutional profiles. Parameters: institution ID, name, start year, end year. Include publication count, citation distribution, top authors.Institutional template. Create parameterized Quarto document institutional profiles. Parameters: institution ID, name, start year, end year. Include publication count, citation distribution, top authors.Batch rendering. Render journal profile template 3 different journals. Compare resulting reports side side.Batch rendering. Render journal profile template 3 different journals. Compare resulting reports side side.Conditional content. Add conditional logic: sample fewer 50 papers, display warning message instead citation distribution figure.Conditional content. Add conditional logic: sample fewer 50 papers, display warning message instead citation distribution figure.

heading: 38.1 Learning objectives
text: completing chapter, able :Design Shiny app architecture bibliometric analysisImplement reactive data fetching OpenAlexBuild interactive UI components: dropdowns, sliders, tabbed panelsCreate server-side logic citation analysis network visualisationDeploy Shiny app shinyapps.io self-hosted server

heading: 38.2 Setup
code:
library(tidyverse)
library(shiny)
library(openalexR)
library(glue)

set.seed(20260509)

source(here::here("R", "sci_palette.R"))

heading: 38.3 Conceptual background
text: Shiny transforms R scripts web applications. Users interact widgets (dropdowns, sliders, buttons) see results update real time. bibliometrics, Shiny apps serve two audiences: researchers want explore data without writing code, decision-makers need accessible analytics dashboards.Shiny app two components: UI (user interface, defining layout inputs) server (R logic reacts user input generates output). Reactive programming connects inputs outputs: user changes dropdown, computations depend input re-executed.bibliometric apps, typical architecture involves:Input panel: Select journal, institution, search query; choose date range sample size.Data fetching: Reactively query OpenAlex based user selections.Analysis tabs: Trend charts, citation distributions, author rankings, optionally network visualisation.Export: Download buttons data (CSV) figures (PNG).biblioshiny() bibliometrix package (Aria Cuccurullo 2017) provides ready-made Shiny interface many standard analyses. Building custom app gives full control analysis pipeline user experience.Deployment options include shinyapps.io (hosted Posit, free tier available), Posit Connect (enterprise), self-hosted via Shiny Server (open source).

heading: 38.4.1 App structure
text: demonstrate key components bibliometric Shiny app. code shows UI server logic separately clarity.

heading: 38.4.2 UI definition
code:
ui <- fluidPage(
  titlePanel("Bibliometric Explorer"),

  sidebarLayout(
    sidebarPanel(
      textInput("journal_id", "OpenAlex Source ID:",
                value = "S148561398"),
      sliderInput("year_range", "Publication Years:",
                  min = 2015, max = 2023, value = c(2020, 2023)),
      numericInput("sample_size", "Sample Size:", value = 200,
                   min = 50, max = 1000),
      actionButton("fetch", "Fetch Data", class = "btn-primary"),
      hr(),
      downloadButton("download_csv", "Download CSV")
    ),

    mainPanel(
      tabsetPanel(
        tabPanel("Trends",
                 plotOutput("trend_plot"),
                 tableOutput("summary_table")),
        tabPanel("Citations",
                 plotOutput("cite_dist"),
                 plotOutput("cite_boxplot")),
        tabPanel("Data",
                 DT::dataTableOutput("data_table"))
      )
    )
  )
)

heading: 38.4.3 Server logic
38.4.4 Running the app
code: shinyApp(ui = ui, server = server)

# library(bibliometrix)
# biblioshiny()

heading: 38.5 Diagnostics and interpretation
text: Responsiveness: API calls can take several seconds. Use withProgress() show loading indicators.Error handling: Wrap API calls tryCatch() display user-friendly error messages instead stack traces.Memory: user session loads data R memory. apps many concurrent users, pre-aggregate data use database backends.Testing: Use shinytest2 automate testing Shiny apps.

heading: 38.7 Limitations and responsible use
text: Shiny apps analyses. enable exploration substitute rigorous, documented analytical pipelines. Use Shiny discovery, producing final results.API rate limits. Live OpenAlex queries Shiny app can hit rate limits many users fetch data simultaneously. Use caching pre-fetched data production apps.Maintenance. Shiny apps require ongoing maintenance: API changes, package updates, server costs. Plan long-term sustainability deploying.Interpretive guardrails. Non-technical users may misinterpret metrics. Build tooltips, descriptions, warnings — especially around citation-based indicators (Hicks et al. 2015; American Society Cell Biology 2012).

heading: 38.9 Common pitfalls
text: Fetching data every input change. Use eventReactive() action button instead reactive() prevent re-fetching every slider adjustment.validating inputs. Check journal ID exists date range valid making API calls.Monolithic server functions. Break server logic modules maintainability app grows.Deploying without testing. Test realistic data volumes deploying. app works 100 records may fail 10,000.

text: Add network tab. Extend app tab builds displays co-authorship network using visNetwork.Add network tab. Extend app tab builds displays co-authorship network using visNetwork.Caching. Implement server-side caching repeated queries parameters return instantly.Caching. Implement server-side caching repeated queries parameters return instantly.Deployment. Deploy app shinyapps.io. Test different journal IDs date ranges.Deployment. Deploy app shinyapps.io. Test different journal IDs date ranges.biblioshiny comparison. heading: 39.1 Objective
text: Map structure, growth, evolution CRISPR gene editing research emergence current state using open bibliometric data.

heading: 39.2 Setup
code:
library(tidyverse)
library(openalexR)
library(igraph)
library(tidygraph)
library(ggraph)
library(quanteda)
library(tidytext)
library(glue)
library(gt)

set.seed(20260509)

source(here::here("R", "api_helpers.R"))
source(here::here("R", "utils.R"))
source(here::here("R", "sci_palette.R"))

heading: 39.3 Data acquisition
code:
works <- oa_fetch(
  entity = "works",
  search = "CRISPR",
  from_publication_date = "2010-01-01",
  to_publication_date = "2024-06-30",
  type = "article",
  options = list(sample = 500, seed = 42)
)

works <- works |>
  mutate(year = year(publication_date))

cat(glue("CRISPR articles retrieved: {nrow(works)}\n"))#> CRISPR articles retrieved: 500
cat(glue("Year range: {min(works$year)}--{max(works$year)}\n"))#> Year range: 2010--2024

heading: 39.4 Publication growth
text: Figure 39.1: Annual publication output CRISPR field.
code:
works |>
  count(year) |>
  ggplot(aes(x = year, y = n)) +
  geom_col(fill = palette_sci(1)) +
  labs(x = "Year", y = "Publications") +
  theme_sci()

heading: 39.5 Citation landscape
text: Figure 39.2: Citation distribution CRISPR articles.
code:
ggplot(works, aes(x = cited_by_count)) +
  geom_histogram(binwidth = 10, fill = palette_sci(1), colour = "white") +
  labs(x = "Citations", y = "Papers") +
  theme_sci()
works |>
  arrange(desc(cited_by_count)) |>
  head(10) |>
  select(display_name, year, cited_by_count, source_display_name) |>
  gt()

heading: 39.6 Co-authorship network
text: Figure 39.3: Co-authorship network CRISPR researchers.
code:
author_data <- works |>
  select(id, authorships) |>
  unnest(authorships, names_sep = "_") |>
  select(work_id = id, author_id = authorships_id,
         author_name = authorships_display_name) |>
  filter(!is.na(author_id))

edges <- author_data |>
  inner_join(author_data, by = "work_id", suffix = c("_1", "_2"),
             relationship = "many-to-many") |>
  filter(author_id_1 < author_id_2) |>
  count(author_id_1, author_id_2, name = "weight")

g <- graph_from_data_frame(
  edges |> select(author_id_1, author_id_2, weight),
  directed = FALSE
) |> simplify(edge.attr.comb = list(weight = "sum"))

comp <- components(g)
giant <- induced_subgraph(g, which(comp$membership == which.max(comp$csize)))
V(giant)$community <- as.factor(membership(
  cluster_leiden(giant, resolution_parameter = 1.0,
                 objective_function = "modularity")
))
V(giant)$degree <- degree(giant)

cat(glue("Network: {vcount(giant)} nodes, {ecount(giant)} edges\n"))#> Network: 92 nodes, 4186 edges
set.seed(42)
ggraph(as_tbl_graph(giant), layout = "fr") +
  geom_edge_link(alpha = 0.1, colour = "grey60") +
  geom_node_point(aes(size = degree, colour = community), alpha = 0.7) +
  scale_size_continuous(range = c(0.5, 5), guide = "none") +
  scale_colour_manual(values = palette_sci(n_distinct(V(giant)$community))) +
  labs(colour = "Community") +
  theme_void(base_family = "sans", base_size = 11) + theme(legend.position = "bottom")

heading: 39.7 Topic evolution
text: Figure 39.4: Top terms year showing topical evolution.
code:
text_df <- works |>
  filter(!is.na(abstract), nchar(abstract) > 50) |>
  transmute(doc_id = id, text = paste(display_name, abstract, sep = ". "), year)

corp <- corpus(text_df, docid_field = "doc_id", text_field = "text")
toks <- tokens(corp, remove_punct = TRUE, remove_numbers = TRUE) |>
  tokens_tolower() |>
  tokens_remove(stopwords("en")) |>
  tokens_remove(c("study", "paper", "results", "using", "based"))

dfmat <- dfm(toks) |> dfm_trim(min_termfreq = 5, min_docfreq = 3)

top_by_year <- map_dfr(unique(text_df$year), function(yr) {
  docs <- docvars(dfmat, "year") == yr
  if (sum(docs) < 5) return(tibble())
  top <- topfeatures(dfmat[docs, ], 5)
  tibble(year = yr, term = names(top), freq = unname(top))
})
top_by_year |>
  group_by(year) |>
  mutate(term = reorder_within(term, freq, year)) |>
  ggplot(aes(x = freq, y = term)) +
  geom_col(fill = palette_sci(1)) +
  facet_wrap(~ year, scales = "free_y", ncol = 4) +
  scale_y_reordered() +
  labs(x = "Frequency", y = NULL) +
  theme_sci(base_size = 8)

heading: 39.8 Key findings
text: Explosive growth: CRISPR publications grew exponentially 2012, reflecting rapid adoption Cas9-based editing.Citation concentration: small number foundational papers dominate citation landscape.Collaborative structure: co-authorship network shows distinct communities, likely corresponding different application domains (therapeutics, agriculture, basic biology).Topic evolution: Early terms focus methodology; later years shift toward applications clinical translation.

heading: 39.9 Lessons learned
text: OpenAlex sampling provides representative snapshot may miss highly specialised non-English publications.citation distribution extreme: median citations far mean, making median-based statistics essential.Co-authorship networks fast-growing fields fragmented; many research groups work independently.

heading: 40.1 Objective
text: Compare publication output, citation impact, research profiles five universities using size-independent field-normalised indicators.

heading: 40.2 Setup
code:
library(tidyverse)
library(openalexR)
library(glue)
library(gt)

set.seed(20260509)

source(here::here("R", "api_helpers.R"))
source(here::here("R", "utils.R"))
source(here::here("R", "sci_palette.R"))

heading: 40.3 Data acquisition
code:
institutions <- tribble(
  ~short_name,    ~oa_id,
  "MIT",          "I63966007",
  "Oxford",       "I40120149",
  "ETH Zurich",   "I202697423",
  "U São Paulo",  "I66491032",
  "U Cape Town",  "I173603587"
)
fetch_works <- function(inst_id) {
  res <- tryCatch(
    oa_fetch(
      entity = "works",
      authorships.institutions.id = inst_id,
      from_publication_date = "2020-01-01",
      to_publication_date = "2022-12-31",
      type = "article",
      options = list(sample = 300, seed = 42)
    ),
    error = function(e) NULL
  )
  if (is.null(res)) tibble() else res
}

inst_data <- institutions |>
  mutate(works = map(oa_id, fetch_works)) |>
  filter(map_int(works, nrow) > 0)

heading: 40.4 Publication volume
text: Figure 40.1: Sampled publication counts institution.
code:
inst_data |>
  mutate(n = map_int(works, nrow)) |>
  ggplot(aes(x = n, y = reorder(short_name, n))) +
  geom_col(fill = palette_sci(1)) +
  labs(x = "Sampled articles", y = NULL) +
  theme_sci()

heading: 40.5 Citation impact
code:
impact <- inst_data |>
  mutate(
    mean_cites = map_dbl(works, \(w) mean(w$cited_by_count, na.rm = TRUE)),
    median_cites = map_dbl(works, \(w) median(w$cited_by_count, na.rm = TRUE)),
    h_index = map_int(works, \(w) compute_h_index(w$cited_by_count))
  ) |>
  select(short_name, mean_cites, median_cites, h_index) |>
  arrange(desc(mean_cites))

impact |>
  gt() |>
  fmt_number(columns = mean_cites, decimals = 1)

heading: 40.6 Full vs. fractional counting
text: Figure 40.2: Full vs. fractional publication counts.
code:
counting <- inst_data |>
  mutate(
    full_count = map_int(works, nrow),
    frac_count = map_dbl(works, function(w) {
      w |>
        select(id, authorships) |>
        unnest(authorships, names_sep = "_") |>
        unnest(authorships_affiliations, names_sep = "_") |>
        group_by(id) |>
        summarise(n_inst = n_distinct(authorships_affiliations_id),
                  .groups = "drop") |>
        mutate(frac = 1 / pmax(n_inst, 1)) |>
        pull(frac) |>
        sum()
    })
  ) |>
  select(short_name, full_count, frac_count) |>
  mutate(ratio = round(frac_count / full_count, 2))

counting |> gt()
counting |>
  pivot_longer(c(full_count, frac_count), names_to = "method", values_to = "count") |>
  mutate(method = recode(method, full_count = "Full", frac_count = "Fractional")) |>
  ggplot(aes(x = count, y = reorder(short_name, count), fill = method)) +
  geom_col(position = "dodge", width = 0.7) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = palette_sci(2)) +
  labs(x = "Count", y = NULL, fill = "Method") +
  theme_sci()

heading: 40.7 OA rates
text: Figure 40.3: Open access rate institution.
code:
inst_data |>
  mutate(oa_rate = map_dbl(works, \(w) mean(w$oa_status != "closed", na.rm = TRUE))) |>
  ggplot(aes(x = oa_rate, y = reorder(short_name, oa_rate))) +
  geom_col(fill = palette_sci(1)) +
  scale_x_continuous(labels = scales::percent) +
  labs(x = "OA rate", y = NULL) +
  theme_sci()

heading: 40.8 Key findings
text: Size vs. impact: Raw publication counts favour larger institutions. Fractional counting h-index provide size-independent comparisons.Collaboration intensity: Institutions lower fractional/full ratios inter-institutional collaborations.OA variation: OA rates differ substantially, reflecting different national mandates institutional policies.single ranking: Different indicators produce different orderings. illustrates Leiden Manifesto principle multiple indicators needed fair comparison (Hicks et al. 2015).

heading: 40.9 Lessons learned
text: Sampling introduces uncertainty: illustrative comparisons, definitive rankings.Field mix confounds comparisons: medical university technical university directly comparable without field normalisation.Responsible benchmarking requires reporting counting method, normalisation approach, sample size transparently.

heading: 41.1 Objective
text: Compare three information-science journals citation impact, aging patterns, topical coverage inform collection management decisions.

heading: 41.2 Setup
code:
library(tidyverse)
library(openalexR)
library(tidytext)
library(glue)
library(gt)

set.seed(20260509)

source(here::here("R", "api_helpers.R"))
source(here::here("R", "utils.R"))
source(here::here("R", "sci_palette.R"))

heading: 41.3 Data acquisition
code:
journals <- tribble(
  ~short_name,        ~source_id,
  "Scientometrics",   "S148561398",
  "J. Informetrics",  "S205292342",
  "JASIST",           "S4210197613"
)
fetch_journal <- function(sid) {
  oa_fetch(
    entity = "works",
    primary_location.source.id = sid,
    from_publication_date = "2018-01-01",
    to_publication_date = "2023-12-31",
    type = "article",
    options = list(sample = 300, seed = 42)
  )
}

journal_data <- journals |>
  mutate(works = map(source_id, fetch_journal))

heading: 41.4 Citation impact comparison
text: Figure 41.1: Citation count distributions journal.
code:
all_works <- journal_data |>
  mutate(works = map2(works, short_name, \(w, n) w |> mutate(journal = n))) |>
  pull(works) |>
  bind_rows()

ggplot(all_works, aes(x = journal, y = cited_by_count + 1)) +
  geom_boxplot(fill = palette_sci(1), alpha = 0.7) +
  scale_y_log10() +
  labs(x = NULL, y = "Citations (log scale)") +
  theme_sci()
all_works |>
  group_by(journal) |>
  summarise(
    n = n(),
    mean_cites = round(mean(cited_by_count), 1),
    median_cites = median(cited_by_count),
    h_index = compute_h_index(cited_by_count),
    .groups = "drop"
  ) |>
  gt()

heading: 41.5 Citation aging
text: Figure 41.2: Mean citations article age journal.
code:
aging <- all_works |>
  mutate(age = 2024 - year(publication_date)) |>
  group_by(journal, age) |>
  summarise(mean_cites = mean(cited_by_count, na.rm = TRUE), .groups = "drop")

ggplot(aging, aes(x = age, y = mean_cites, colour = journal)) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 1) +
  geom_point(size = 2) +
  scale_colour_manual(values = palette_sci(3)) +
  labs(x = "Years since publication", y = "Mean citations", colour = "Journal") +
  theme_sci()

heading: 41.6 Topical coverage
text: Figure 41.3: Top 10 topics journal.
code:
topic_data <- all_works |>
  select(id, journal, topics) |>
  unnest(topics, names_sep = "_") |>
  select(journal, topic = topics_display_name) |>
  mutate(topic = str_to_lower(str_trim(topic))) |>
  filter(!is.na(topic), nchar(topic) >= 3)

top_topics <- topic_data |>
  count(journal, topic, sort = TRUE) |>
  group_by(journal) |>
  slice_max(n, n = 10) |>
  ungroup()
top_topics |>
  mutate(topic = reorder_within(topic, n, journal)) |>
  ggplot(aes(x = n, y = topic)) +
  geom_col(fill = palette_sci(1)) +
  facet_wrap(~ journal, scales = "free_y") +
  scale_y_reordered() +
  labs(x = "Frequency", y = NULL) +
  theme_sci(base_size = 9)

heading: 41.7 Key findings
text: Impact variation: Citation distributions differ across journals, showing higher medians others higher means (driven highly cited papers).Aging patterns: three journals show similar aging curves, consistent broad discipline.Topical differentiation: Despite overlapping coverage, journal distinct topical emphases.

heading: 41.8 Lessons learned
text: Journal comparison requires multiple dimensions; single metric tells full story.Sample-based analysis illustrative. production-quality journal evaluation, use complete data field-normalised indicators (Waltman 2016).Citation aging patterns remarkably consistent within discipline differ dramatically , say, biomedicine humanities.

chapter: 42 Case Study 4: Gender Gap in Scientific Publishing
heading: 42.1 Objective
text: Compare gender representation authorship across biomedical journal information-science journal, demonstrating methods substantial limitations.

chapter: 42 Case Study 4: Gender Gap in Scientific Publishing
heading: 42.2 Setup
code:
library(tidyverse)
library(openalexR)
library(glue)
library(gt)

set.seed(20260509)

source(here::here("R", "api_helpers.R"))
source(here::here("R", "utils.R"))
source(here::here("R", "sci_palette.R"))

chapter: 42 Case Study 4: Gender Gap in Scientific Publishing
heading: 42.3 Data acquisition
code:
works_sciento <- oa_fetch(
  entity = "works",
  primary_location.source.id = "S148561398",
  from_publication_date = "2019-01-01",
  to_publication_date = "2023-12-31",
  type = "article",
  options = list(sample = 300, seed = 42)
) |> mutate(journal = "Scientometrics")

works_plos <- oa_fetch(
  entity = "works",
  primary_location.source.id = "S202381698",
  from_publication_date = "2019-01-01",
  to_publication_date = "2023-12-31",
  type = "article",
  options = list(sample = 300, seed = 42)
) |> mutate(journal = "PLOS ONE")

works_all <- bind_rows(works_sciento, works_plos)

chapter: 42 Case Study 4: Gender Gap in Scientific Publishing
heading: 42.4 Gender inference
code:
common_female <- c("maria", "anna", "li", "sarah", "jennifer", "jessica",
                   "elena", "nina", "laura", "julia", "diana", "sandra",
                   "lisa", "emily", "rachel", "amy", "kate", "megan")
common_male <- c("john", "david", "michael", "james", "robert", "peter",
                 "mark", "thomas", "paul", "daniel", "andreas", "martin",
                 "chris", "matthew", "andrew", "william", "kevin", "brian")

authors <- works_all |>
  select(work_id = id, journal, authorships) |>
  unnest(authorships, names_sep = "_") |>
  group_by(work_id) |>
  mutate(
    n_authors = n(),
    position = case_when(
      row_number() == 1 ~ "first",
      row_number() == n() & n() > 1 ~ "last",
      TRUE ~ "middle"
    )
  ) |>
  ungroup() |>
  mutate(
    first_name = str_to_lower(str_extract(authorships_display_name, "^\\S+")),
    gender = case_when(
      first_name %in% common_female ~ "female",
      first_name %in% common_male ~ "male",
      TRUE ~ NA_character_
    )
  ) |>
  filter(!is.na(gender))

cat(glue("Classified authors: {nrow(authors)}\n"))#> Classified authors: 213

chapter: 42 Case Study 4: Gender Gap in Scientific Publishing
heading: 42.5 Gender representation by journal
text: Figure 42.1: Gender representation journal.
code:
authors |>
  count(journal, gender) |>
  group_by(journal) |>
  mutate(pct = n / sum(n)) |>
  ggplot(aes(x = journal, y = pct, fill = gender)) +
  geom_col(position = "dodge") +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = scales::percent) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = palette_sci(2)) +
  labs(x = NULL, y = "Proportion", fill = "Gender") +
  theme_sci()

chapter: 42 Case Study 4: Gender Gap in Scientific Publishing

text: Figure 42.2: Gender representation authorship position journal.
code:
position_summary <- authors |>
  count(journal, position, gender) |>
  group_by(journal, position) |>
  mutate(pct = n / sum(n)) |>
  ungroup()

ggplot(position_summary, aes(x = position, y = pct, fill = gender)) +
  geom_col(position = "dodge") +
  facet_wrap(~ journal) +

chapter: 42 Case Study 4: Gender Gap in Scientific Publishing
heading: 42.7 Temporal trends
text: Figure 42.3: Proportion female first authors year.
code:
first_authors <- authors |>
  filter(position == "first") |>
  mutate(year = year(as.Date(paste0(
    str_extract(work_id, "\\d{4}$"), "-01-01"
  ))))

works_all_year <- works_all |>
  transmute(work_id = id, year = year(publication_date))

first_authors_year <- first_authors |>
  select(-year) |>
  left_join(works_all_year, by = "work_id")

first_authors_year |>

  summarise(female_pct = mean(gender == "female"), .groups = "drop") |>

chapter: 42 Case Study 4: Gender Gap in Scientific Publishing
heading: 42.8 Key findings
text: Persistent gap: Male names overrepresented journals, particularly last-author positions.Disciplinary differences: gender balance differs journals, likely reflecting field-specific demographics.Temporal progress: evidence increasing female representation time, trends noisy due small sample sizes.

chapter: 42 Case Study 4: Gender Gap in Scientific Publishing
heading: 42.9 Critical caveats
text: results must interpreted extreme caution:Name-based inference enforces binary excludes non-binary researchers.Coverage biased: names East Asia, South Asia, Africa poorly classified.unclassified names random — systematically different classified names.methodological demonstration, definitive study. Production gender analysis requires validated tools confidence scores country context (Larivière et al. 2013).

heading: 43.1 Objective
text: Identify emerging topics scientometrics research (2015–2023) using keyword burst detection topic modelling, validate findings known developments field.

heading: 43.2 Setup
code:
library(tidyverse)
library(openalexR)
library(quanteda)
library(quanteda.textstats)
library(topicmodels)
library(tidytext)
library(glue)
library(gt)

set.seed(20260509)

source(here::here("R", "api_helpers.R"))
source(here::here("R", "utils.R"))
source(here::here("R", "sci_palette.R"))

heading: 43.3 Data acquisition
code:
works <- oa_fetch(
  entity = "works",
  primary_location.source.id = "S148561398",
  from_publication_date = "2015-01-01",
  to_publication_date = "2023-12-31",
  type = "article",
  options = list(sample = 600, seed = 42)
)

text_df <- works |>
  filter(!is.na(abstract), nchar(abstract) > 50) |>
  transmute(
    doc_id = id,
    text = paste(display_name, abstract, sep = ". "),
    year = year(publication_date)
  )

cat(glue("Documents: {nrow(text_df)}\n"))#> Documents: 116

heading: 43.4 Keyword burst detection
text: Figure 43.1: Top 15 fastest-growing keywords (2021–2023).
code:
corp <- corpus(text_df, docid_field = "doc_id", text_field = "text")
docvars(corp, "year") <- text_df$year

toks <- tokens(corp, remove_punct = TRUE, remove_numbers = TRUE) |>
  tokens_tolower() |>
  tokens_remove(stopwords("en")) |>
  tokens_remove(c("study", "paper", "results", "research", "analysis",
                   "also", "however", "using", "based"))

dfmat <- dfm(toks) |> dfm_trim(min_termfreq = 10, min_docfreq = 5)

kw_by_year <- quanteda::convert(dfm_group(dfmat, groups = year), to = "data.frame") |>
  pivot_longer(-doc_id, names_to = "term", values_to = "count") |>
  rename(year = doc_id) |>
  mutate(year = as.integer(year))

growth <- kw_by_year |>
  group_by(term) |>
  filter(sum(count) >= 20) |>
  arrange(year) |>
  mutate(growth = (count - lag(count)) / pmax(lag(count), 1)) |>
  ungroup() |>
  filter(!is.na(growth))

recent_growth <- growth |>
  filter(year >= 2021) |>
  group_by(term) |>
  summarise(mean_growth = mean(growth), total = sum(count), .groups = "drop") |>
  filter(mean_growth > 0) |>
  arrange(desc(mean_growth))
recent_growth |>
  head(15) |>
  mutate(term = fct_reorder(term, mean_growth)) |>
  ggplot(aes(x = mean_growth, y = term)) +
  geom_col(fill = palette_sci(1)) +
  scale_x_continuous(labels = scales::percent) +
  labs(x = "Mean growth rate (2021–2023)", y = NULL) +
  theme_sci()

heading: 43.5 Keyword trajectories
text: Figure 43.2: Temporal trajectories selected emerging keywords.
code:
emerging_terms <- recent_growth |> head(6) |> pull(term)

kw_by_year |>
  filter(term %in% emerging_terms) |>
  ggplot(aes(x = year, y = count, colour = term)) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 0.8) +
  geom_point(size = 1.5) +
  scale_colour_manual(values = palette_sci(length(emerging_terms))) +
  labs(x = "Year", y = "Frequency", colour = "Keyword") +
  theme_sci()

heading: 43.6 Topic modelling
text: Figure 43.3: Top terms per LDA topic.
code:
dtm <- quanteda::convert(dfmat, to = "topicmodels")
lda <- LDA(dtm, k = 8, control = list(seed = 42))

lda_topics <- tidy(lda, matrix = "beta") |>
  group_by(topic) |>
  slice_max(beta, n = 8) |>
  ungroup()
lda_topics |>
  mutate(term = reorder_within(term, beta, topic)) |>
  ggplot(aes(x = beta, y = term, fill = factor(topic))) +
  geom_col(show.legend = FALSE) +
  facet_wrap(~ paste("Topic", topic), scales = "free_y", ncol = 4) +
  scale_y_reordered() +
  scale_fill_manual(values = palette_sci(8)) +
  labs(x = "Word probability", y = NULL) +
  theme_sci(base_size = 9)

heading: 43.7 Topic prevalence by year
text: Figure 43.4: Topic prevalence time.
code:
doc_topics <- tidy(lda, matrix = "gamma") |>
  left_join(text_df |> transmute(document = doc_id, year), by = "document")

topic_by_year <- doc_topics |>
  group_by(year, topic) |>
  summarise(mean_gamma = mean(gamma), .groups = "drop")

ggplot(topic_by_year, aes(x = year, y = mean_gamma, colour = factor(topic))) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 0.7) +
  scale_colour_manual(values = palette_sci(8)) +
  labs(x = "Year", y = "Mean topic proportion", colour = "Topic") +
  theme_sci()

heading: 43.8 Key findings
text: Emerging keywords align known developments: open science, AI/machine learning applications, equity diversity, preprints.Topic evolution shows gradual shifts emphasis rather abrupt changes, consistent mature field incorporating new tools.Growth rates interpreted cautiously: terms low baseline frequencies can show high percentage growth small increases.Validation: topics identified LDA correspond recognisable subfields, lending credibility unsupervised approach.

heading: 43.9 Lessons learned
text: Combining keyword growth rates topic models provides complementary perspectives: growth rates capture individual terms, topics capture thematic clusters.Database coverage growth can create artefactual “emergence.” Always normalise total corpus size.Emerging topics scientometrics mirror broader trends science policy (open access mandates, equity initiatives, AI adoption) (Kleinberg 2003).

chapter: B SQL Primer for the OpenAlex Snapshot
heading: B SQL Primer for the OpenAlex Snapshot

chapter: B SQL Primer for the OpenAlex Snapshot
heading: B.1 When to use the snapshot
text: OpenAlex API (Chapter 5) ideal moderate queries. analyses requiring millions records — full-database bibliographic coupling, global co-authorship networks, exhaustive field delineation — OpenAlex snapshot practical. snapshot full database dump released monthly compressed JSON-lines format.

chapter: B SQL Primer for the OpenAlex Snapshot
heading: B.2 Loading the snapshot
text: OpenAlex provides scripts load snapshot PostgreSQL. loading:
code:
-- Count total works
SELECT COUNT(*) FROM openalex.works;

-- Recent articles with citation counts
SELECT id, display_name, publication_year, cited_by_count
FROM openalex.works
WHERE type = 'article'
  AND publication_year >= 2020
ORDER BY cited_by_count DESC
LIMIT 100;

chapter: B SQL Primer for the OpenAlex Snapshot
heading: B.3 Common queries

chapter: B SQL Primer for the OpenAlex Snapshot
heading: B.3.1 Publication counts by year
code:
SELECT publication_year, COUNT(*) AS n_works
FROM openalex.works
WHERE type = 'article'
GROUP BY publication_year
ORDER BY publication_year;

chapter: B SQL Primer for the OpenAlex Snapshot
heading: B.3.2 Top institutions by output
code:
SELECT i.display_name, COUNT(DISTINCT w.id) AS n_works
FROM openalex.works w
JOIN openalex.works_authorships wa ON w.id = wa.work_id
JOIN openalex.works_authorships_institutions wai
  ON wa.work_id = wai.work_id AND wa.author_position = wai.author_position
JOIN openalex.institutions i ON wai.institution_id = i.id
WHERE w.publication_year = 2023
  AND w.type = 'article'
GROUP BY i.display_name
ORDER BY n_works DESC
LIMIT 20;

chapter: B SQL Primer for the OpenAlex Snapshot
heading: B.3.3 Co-authorship pairs
code:
SELECT a1.author_id AS author_a,
       a2.author_id AS author_b,
       COUNT(DISTINCT a1.work_id) AS n_collabs
FROM openalex.works_authorships a1
JOIN openalex.works_authorships a2
  ON a1.work_id = a2.work_id AND a1.author_id < a2.author_id
GROUP BY a1.author_id, a2.author_id
HAVING COUNT(DISTINCT a1.work_id) >= 5
ORDER BY n_collabs DESC
LIMIT 100;

chapter: B SQL Primer for the OpenAlex Snapshot
heading: B.4 Connecting from R
code:
library(DBI)
library(RPostgres)

con <- dbConnect(
  Postgres(),
  dbname = "openalex",
  host = "localhost",
  port = 5432,
  user = "openalex"
)

top_journals <- dbGetQuery(con, "
  SELECT s.display_name, COUNT(*) AS n_works
  FROM openalex.works w
  JOIN openalex.sources s ON w.primary_location_source_id = s.id
  WHERE w.publication_year = 2023
  GROUP BY s.display_name
  ORDER BY n_works DESC
  LIMIT 20
")

dbDisconnect(con)

chapter: B SQL Primer for the OpenAlex Snapshot
heading: B.5 Performance tips
text: Index key columns: work_id, author_id, institution_id, publication_year, cited_by_count indexed.Partition year: time-series queries, partitioning works table publication_year speeds range scans substantially.Materialised views: Pre-compute expensive joins (e.g., institution–work counts) materialised views refresh snapshot update.Use COPY bulk loads: COPY command orders magnitude faster row--row INSERT.

heading: C Indicator Crosswalk Across Databases

heading: C.6 Notes
text: “Y” = available directly data source.“Compute” = raw data available indicator must calculated.“—” = available source.OpenAlex fully free open source table. Web Science Scopus require institutional subscriptions. Dimensions offers free tier limited functionality.Field normalisation always requires reference values. source provide natively, can compute full corpus using method Chapter 13.

heading: D.1 openalexR quick reference
code:
library(openalexR)

# Fetch works by journal (source)
works <- oa_fetch(
  entity = "works",
  primary_location.source.id = "S148561398",
  from_publication_date = "2020-01-01",
  to_publication_date = "2023-12-31",
  type = "article"
)

# Fetch works by institution
works <- oa_fetch(
  entity = "works",
  authorships.institutions.id = "I63966007",
  from_publication_date = "2022-01-01",
  type = "article"
)

# Fetch works by topic
works <- oa_fetch(
  entity = "works",
  topics.id = "T10102",
  from_publication_date = "2023-01-01"
)

# Fetch author profile
author <- oa_fetch(entity = "authors", id = "A5023888391")

# Fetch institution profile
inst <- oa_fetch(entity = "institutions", id = "I63966007")

# Sampling
works <- oa_fetch(
  entity = "works",
  primary_location.source.id = "S148561398",
  options = list(sample = 500, seed = 42)
)

heading: D.2 quanteda pipeline
code:
library(quanteda)

# Full text-analysis pipeline
corp <- corpus(df, docid_field = "id", text_field = "text")
toks <- tokens(corp, remove_punct = TRUE, remove_numbers = TRUE) |>
  tokens_tolower() |>
  tokens_remove(stopwords("en"))
dfmat <- dfm(toks) |>
  dfm_trim(min_termfreq = 5, min_docfreq = 3)

# TF-IDF weighting
dfmat_tfidf <- dfm_tfidf(dfmat)

# Top features
topfeatures(dfmat, 20)

# Keyword-in-context
kwic(toks, pattern = "open access", window = 5)

heading: D.3 igraph / tidygraph / ggraph
code:
library(igraph)
library(tidygraph)
library(ggraph)

# Build graph from edge list
g <- graph_from_data_frame(edges, directed = FALSE, vertices = nodes)

# Tidygraph conversion
tg <- as_tbl_graph(g) |>
  activate(nodes) |>
  mutate(
    degree = centrality_degree(),
    between = centrality_betweenness(),
    community = group_leiden(resolution = 1.0)
  )

# ggraph visualisation
ggraph(tg, layout = "fr") +
  geom_edge_link(aes(alpha = weight), show.legend = FALSE) +
  geom_node_point(aes(size = degree, colour = factor(community))) +
  geom_node_text(aes(label = name), repel = TRUE, size = 3) +
  theme_void()

heading: D.4 Common data wrangling patterns
code:
library(tidyverse)

# Unnest OpenAlex authorships
authors <- works |>
  select(work_id = id, authorships) |>
  unnest(authorships, names_sep = "_")

# Unnest OpenAlex topics
topics <- works |>
  select(work_id = id, topics) |>
  unnest(topics, names_sep = "_")

# Compute h-index
compute_h_index <- function(citations) {
  citations <- sort(citations, decreasing = TRUE)
  sum(citations >= seq_along(citations))
}

# Field normalisation
works |>
  group_by(field, year) |>
  mutate(
    field_mean = mean(cited_by_count),
    mncs = cited_by_count / pmax(field_mean, 1)
  )

# Fractional counting
works |>
  unnest(authorships, names_sep = "_") |>
  group_by(id) |>
  mutate(frac = 1 / n()) |>
  ungroup()

heading: Chapter 1 — What Is Scientometrics?
text:
1. Explore different field.
Swap search term fetch fresh sample. Comparing publication trend
top journals reveals different fields vary volume, growth rate, 
journal concentration.Key insight: High-growth fields like machine learning show steeper annual
increases broader spread across journals compared niche field like
scientometrics.2. Uncited works.
Filter zero-citation records examine year distribution.Key insight: Recent publication years dominate papers need time 
accumulate citations — window effect.3. Open Access status.
Compute yearly OA proportions reveal trend.Key insight: OA proportions risen steadily, driven funder mandates 
growth gold OA journals.4. Growth curve.
Plot annual counts log scale; linearity implies exponential growth.Key insight: points fall roughly straight line, growth 
approximately exponential, consistent Price’s law.
code:
library(openalexR)
library(tidyverse)

works_ml <- oa_fetch(
 entity = "works",
 search = "machine learning",
 from_publication_date = "2013-01-01",
 to_publication_date = "2023-12-31",
 count_only = FALSE,
 per_page = 200
) |> slice_sample(n = 500)

works_ml |>
 count(publication_year) |>
