 labs(title = "Papers with funding acknowledgements over time",
      x = "Year", y = "Share with funding data")

heading: Chapter 31 — Patent-Paper Linkages
text:
1. Field comparison.
Compare proportion highly cited papers across fields.Key insight: Biomedical research likely cited patents 
direct translational applications. Higher citation rates 
automatically imply greater technological relevance.2. Time lag estimation.
Estimate citation accumulation trajectory cohort papers.Key insight: Papers typically peak citation accumulation age 2-4 years,
depending field. lag matters evaluating recent research.3. Institutional patent linkage.
Compare proportion top-cited papers across institutions.Key insight: higher proportion top-percentile papers may suggest greater
potential technological impact, causation established without
actual patent citation data.
code:
library(openalexR)
library(tidyverse)

biomed <- oa_fetch(entity = "works",
                   search = "drug delivery",
                   from_publication_date = "2018-01-01",
                   to_publication_date = "2020-12-31") |>
 slice_sample(n = 200) |> mutate(field = "Biomedical")

socsci <- oa_fetch(entity = "works",
                   search = "social inequality",
                   from_publication_date = "2018-01-01",
                   to_publication_date = "2020-12-31") |>
 slice_sample(n = 200) |> mutate(field = "Social Science")

combined <- bind_rows(biomed, socsci)

combined |>
 group_by(field) |>
 summarise(
   pct_top10 = mean(cited_by_count >=
                     quantile(cited_by_count, 0.9)),
   mean_cites = mean(cited_by_count)
 )
cohort_2015 <- works |>
 filter(publication_year == 2015, !is.null(counts_by_year))

trajectory <- cohort_2015 |>
 unnest(counts_by_year) |>
 mutate(age = year - 2015) |>
 filter(age >= 0) |>
 group_by(age) |>
 summarise(median_cites = median(cited_by_count))

ggplot(trajectory, aes(age, median_cites)) +
 geom_line() +
 geom_point() +
 labs(title = "Citation trajectory for 2015 cohort",
      x = "Age (years since publication)",
      y = "Median annual citations")

# Peak year
peak_age <- trajectory$age[which.max(trajectory$median_cites)]
cat(sprintf("Peak citation year: age %d\n", peak_age))
inst_ids <- c("I123456789", "I987654321")

inst_top_papers <- map(inst_ids, \(iid) {
 w <- oa_fetch(entity = "works",
               authorships.institutions.lineage = iid,
               from_publication_date = "2018-01-01",
               to_publication_date = "2020-12-31")
 p90 <- quantile(w$cited_by_count, 0.9)
 tibble(
   institution = iid,
   n_papers = nrow(w),
   pct_above_p90 = mean(w$cited_by_count >= p90)
 )
}) |> list_rbind()

inst_top_papers

heading: Chapter 32 — Causal Inference in Scientometrics
text:
1. Extended .
Add covariates difference--differences regression.Key insight: interaction term changes substantially added
covariates, simple model suffers omitted variable bias.2. Placebo test.
Use false treatment date test parallel trends assumption.Key insight: non-significant placebo interaction (p > 0.05) supports 
parallel trends assumption. significant result suggests pre-existing
divergence OA non-OA papers.3. Matched comparison.
Use propensity score matching estimate OA effect.Key insight: Matching reduces selection bias creating comparable groups.
matched unmatched effects differ substantially, selection 
observables important.
code:
library(tidyverse)

# Assuming works_did has: cited_by_count, is_oa, post_treatment,
# type, n_authors, referenced_works_count
did_extended <- lm(
 cited_by_count ~ is_oa * post_treatment +
   type + n_authors + referenced_works_count,
 data = works_did
)

summary(did_extended)

# Compare with simple model
did_simple <- lm(cited_by_count ~ is_oa * post_treatment,
                 data = works_did)

cat(sprintf("Simple interaction: %.2f\n",
            coef(did_simple)["is_oaTRUE:post_treatmentTRUE"]))
cat(sprintf("Extended interaction: %.2f\n",
            coef(did_extended)["is_oaTRUE:post_treatmentTRUE"]))
# Placebo: pretend treatment happened in 2018 instead of 2020
works_placebo <- works_did |>
 filter(publication_year <= 2019) |>  # pre-treatment only
 mutate(post_placebo = publication_year >= 2018)

did_placebo <- lm(cited_by_count ~ is_oa * post_placebo,
                  data = works_placebo)

summary(did_placebo)

# If the interaction is NOT significant, parallel trends hold
cat(sprintf("Placebo p-value: %.4f\n",
            summary(did_placebo)$coefficients[
             "is_oaTRUE:post_placeboTRUE", "Pr(>|t|)"
            ]))
library(MatchIt)
library(tidyverse)

# Propensity score matching
matched <- matchit(
 is_oa ~ publication_year + referenced_works_count + type,
 data = works_did,
 method = "nearest",
 ratio = 1
)

summary(matched)
matched_data <- match.data(matched)

# Estimate treatment effect on matched sample
did_matched <- lm(cited_by_count ~ is_oa, data = matched_data)
summary(did_matched)

cat(sprintf("Unmatched OA effect: %.2f\n",
            coef(did_simple)["is_oaTRUE"]))
cat(sprintf("Matched OA effect: %.2f\n",
            coef(did_matched)["is_oaTRUE"]))

heading: Chapter 33 — Reproducibility, RRIDs, and Badges
text:
1. DAS detection.
Search abstracts data/code availability statements.Key insight: DAS detection via abstract text lower bound; many statements
appear dedicated sections captured abstracts.2. Cross-journal comparison.
Compare metadata completeness OA rates across journals.Key insight: Journals higher OA rates tend better metadata
completeness, correlation perfect.3. Temporal trends.
Track transparency proxies time.Key insight: Transparency proxies citation impact may weakly correlated
within years, confounding factors (field, journal prestige) complicate
interpretation.
code:
library(tidyverse)

das_keywords <- c("data availability", "code availability",
                  "github\\.com", "zenodo", "figshare",
                  "data are available", "code is available")

das_pattern <- paste(das_keywords, collapse = "|")

works |>
 filter(!is.na(ab)) |>
 mutate(has_das = str_detect(tolower(ab), das_pattern)) |>
 summarise(
   total = n(),
   with_das = sum(has_das),
   das_rate = mean(has_das)
 )
journal_ids <- c("S4210174107", "S49861241", "S137773608")

journal_quality <- map(journal_ids, \(jid) {
 w <- oa_fetch(entity = "works",
               primary_location.source.id = jid,
               from_publication_date = "2020-01-01") |>
   slice_sample(n = 200)
 tibble(
   journal = jid,
   abstract_rate = mean(!is.na(w$ab)),
   oa_rate = mean(w$is_oa, na.rm = TRUE),
   doi_rate = mean(!is.na(w$doi))
 )
}) |> list_rbind()

journal_quality
transparency_trends <- works |>
 group_by(publication_year) |>
 summarise(
   abstract_rate = mean(!is.na(ab)),
   oa_rate = mean(is_oa, na.rm = TRUE),
   high_cite_rate = mean(cited_by_count >= 10)
 ) |>
 pivot_longer(-publication_year, names_to = "metric",
              values_to = "value")

ggplot(transparency_trends, aes(publication_year, value,
                                 colour = metric)) +
 geom_line() +
 scale_y_continuous(labels = scales::percent) +
 labs(title = "Transparency and impact proxies over time",
      x = "Year", y = "Proportion")

heading: Chapter 34 — Reproducible Pipelines
text:
1. Build pipeline.
Create _targets.R file bibliometric workflow.Key insight: Changing summary function re-runs summary_stats 
trend_plot, raw_data clean_data — power 
dependency-based pipelines.2. renv snapshot.
Demonstrate dependency management renv.Key insight: renv creates project-local library lockfile, ensuring
exact reproducibility package versions across machines time.3. Branching.
Use dynamic branching analyse multiple journals parallel.Key insight: Dynamic branching avoids duplicating target definitions; 
journal processed independently can run parallel 
tar_make_future().
code:
library(targets)

# _targets.R content:
tar_option_set(packages = c("openalexR", "tidyverse"))

list(
 tar_target(raw_data, {
   oa_fetch(entity = "works", search = "bibliometrics",
            from_publication_date = "2020-01-01")
 }),

 tar_target(clean_data, {
   raw_data |>
     filter(!is.na(doi), !is.na(publication_year)) |>
     select(id, doi, display_name, publication_year,
            cited_by_count, is_oa)
 }),

 tar_target(summary_stats, {
   clean_data |>
     group_by(publication_year) |>
     summarise(n = n(), mean_cites = mean(cited_by_count))
 }),

 tar_target(trend_plot, {
   ggplot(summary_stats, aes(publication_year, n)) +
     geom_col() +
     labs(title = "Publication trend", x = "Year", y = "Works")
 })
)

# Run with: tar_make()
# Verify: tar_visnetwork()
# Step 1: Initialise renv
# renv::init()

# Step 2: Install a package
# install.packages("glue")
# library(glue)

# Step 3: Snapshot
# renv::snapshot()
# This creates/updates renv.lock

# Step 4: Simulate loss --- remove the package
# remove.packages("glue")
# library(glue)  # Error: package not found

# Step 5: Restore
# renv::restore()
# library(glue)  # Works again
library(targets)

# _targets.R with dynamic branching:
tar_option_set(packages = c("openalexR", "tidyverse"))

list(
 tar_target(journal_ids,
            c("S4210174107", "S137773608", "S49861241")),

 tar_target(journal_data,
   oa_fetch(entity = "works",
            primary_location.source.id = journal_ids,
            from_publication_date = "2020-01-01"),
   pattern = map(journal_ids)
 ),

 tar_target(journal_summary,
   journal_data |>
     summarise(
       journal = first(so),
       n = n(),
       mean_cites = mean(cited_by_count)
     ),
   pattern = map(journal_data)
 )
)

heading: Chapter 35 — Interactive Dashboards
text:
1. Multi-page dashboard.
Create Quarto dashboard two pages.Key insight: Quarto dashboards use page-level headings (#) navigation
tabs row/column-level headings (##) layout.2. Linked brushing.
Use plotly selection filter DT table.Key insight: crosstalk::SharedData enables linked brushing 
htmlwidget supports , without needing Shiny.3. Custom tooltips.
Add informative hover text plotly chart.Key insight: Custom tooltips using text hoverinfo = "text" provide
context-rich information hover, improving dashboard usability.
code:
# Save as dashboard.qmd: # --- # title: "Bibliometric Dashboard" # format: dashboard # --- # # # Overview # # ## Row # # ```{r} # library(plotly) # plot_ly(summary_data, x = ~year, y = ~n, type = "bar") # ``` # # ## Row # # ```{r} # plot_ly(works, x = ~cited_by_count, type = "histogram") # ``` # # # Details # # ## Row # # ```{r} # library(DT) # datatable(works |> select(display_name, publication_year, # cited_by_count, is_oa)) # ``` library(plotly) library(crosstalk) library(DT) shared_data <- SharedData$new(works) bscols( plot_ly(shared_data, x = ~publication_year, y = ~cited_by_count, type = "scatter", mode = "markers") |> layout(dragmode = "select"), datatable(shared_data, options = list(pageLength = 10), selection = "multiple") ) library(plotly) library(tidyverse) tooltip_data <- works |> group_by(publication_year) |> summarise(n = n(), mean_cites = round(mean(cited_by_count), 1)) plot_ly(tooltip_data, x = ~publication_year, y = ~n, type = "bar", text = ~paste0("Year: ", publication_year, "
Papers: ", n, "
Mean cites: ", mean_cites), hoverinfo = "text")

heading: Chapter 36 — Parameterized Quarto Reports
text:
1. Institutional template.
Create parameterized report institutional profiles.Key insight: Parameters make template reusable institution;
batch rendering generates portfolio reports efficiently.2. Batch rendering.
Render template multiple journals.Key insight: Batch rendering quarto_render() parameterised
templates scales hundreds reports minimal code.3. Conditional content.
Add warning sample small.Key insight: Conditional logic results: asis enables dynamic content
switching — essential robust automated reports handle edge cases.
code:
# Save as inst_profile.qmd:
# ---
# title: "Institutional Profile"
# params:
#   inst_id: "I123456789"
#   inst_name: "University of Example"
#   start_year: 2020
#   end_year: 2023
# ---
#
# ```{r}
# library(openalexR)
# library(tidyverse)
#
# works <- oa_fetch(
#   entity = "works",
#   authorships.institutions.lineage = params$inst_id,
#   from_publication_date = paste0(params$start_year, "-01-01"),
#   to_publication_date = paste0(params$end_year, "-12-31")
# )
#
# cat(sprintf("Total publications: %d\n", nrow(works)))
# ```
#
# ## Publication Trend
# ```{r}
# works |>
#   count(publication_year) |>
#   ggplot(aes(publication_year, n)) +
#   geom_col() +
#   labs(title = params$inst_name)
# ```
#
# ## Citation Distribution
# ```{r}
# ggplot(works, aes(cited_by_count)) +
#   geom_histogram(bins = 30) +
#   scale_x_log10()
# ```
#
# ## Top Authors
# ```{r}
# works |> unnest(author) |>
#   count(au_display_name, sort = TRUE) |>
#   head(10)
# ```
library(quarto)

journals <- list(
 list(journal_id = "S4210174107", journal_name = "Scientometrics"),
 list(journal_id = "S137773608", journal_name = "Nature Physics"),
 list(journal_id = "S49861241", journal_name = "PLOS ONE")
)

walk(journals, \(j) {
 quarto_render(
   "journal_profile.qmd",
   execute_params = list(
     journal_id = j$journal_id,
     journal_name = j$journal_name,
     start_year = 2020,
     end_year = 2023
   ),
   output_file = paste0("report_", j$journal_name, ".html")
 )
})
# In the parameterized .qmd file:
#
# ```{r}
# #| results: asis
# if (nrow(works) < 50) {
#   cat("<div class="callout callout-warn">\n")
#   cat("This sample contains fewer than 50 papers. ")
#   cat("The citation distribution may not be reliable.\n")
#   cat(":::\n")
# } else {
#   ggplot(works, aes(cited_by_count)) +
#     geom_histogram(bins = 30) +
#     scale_x_log10() +
#     labs(title = "Citation distribution")
# }
# ```

heading: Chapter 37 — Building a Bibliometric Shiny App
text:
1. Add network tab.
Extend app co-authorship network visualisation.Key insight: Limiting network papers 2-10 authors avoids
overwhelming visualisation hyper-authored papers.2. Caching.
Implement server-side caching repeated queries.Key insight: Disk-backed caching persists across sessions, query
never hits API twice. Use cachem::cache_disk() production apps.3. Deployment.
Deploy shinyapps.io.Key insight: Test variety inputs deploying; edge cases (empty
results, large corpora) handled gracefully validation
messages.4. biblioshiny comparison.
Run biblioshiny compare features.Key insight: biblioshiny excellent exploration lacks
reproducibility customisation. custom app better production
workflows specific analytical requirements.
code:
library(shiny)
library(visNetwork)
library(igraph)
library(tidyverse)

# Add to UI:
# tabPanel("Network",
#   visNetworkOutput("coauth_network", height = "600px")
# )

# Add to server:
# output$coauth_network <- renderVisNetwork({
#   req(data())
#   edges <- data() |>
#     unnest(author) |>
#     group_by(id) |>
#     filter(n() >= 2, n() <= 10) |>
#     reframe(pair = combn(au_display_name, 2, simplify = FALSE)) |>
#     unnest_wider(pair, names_sep = "_") |>
#     count(pair_1, pair_2, name = "weight") |>
#     rename(from = pair_1, to = pair_2)
#
#   g <- graph_from_data_frame(edges, directed = FALSE)
#   comm <- cluster_leiden(g, resolution = 1.0)
#
#   nodes <- tibble(
#     id = V(g)$name, label = V(g)$name,
#     group = as.character(membership(comm))
#   )
#
#   visNetwork(nodes, edges) |>
#     visOptions(highlightNearest = TRUE, selectedBy = "group")
# })
library(shiny)
library(memoise)

# Create a cached version of the data fetching function
fetch_cached <- memoise(function(journal_id, start_date, end_date) {
 oa_fetch(
   entity = "works",
   primary_location.source.id = journal_id,
   from_publication_date = start_date,
   to_publication_date = end_date
 )
}, cache = cachem::cache_disk("shiny_cache"))

# In the server:
# data <- reactive({
#   fetch_cached(
#     input$journal_id,
#     paste0(input$start_year, "-01-01"),
#     paste0(input$end_year, "-12-31")
#   )
# })
# Step 1: Install rsconnect
# install.packages("rsconnect")

# Step 2: Configure account
# rsconnect::setAccountInfo(
#   name = "your-account",
#   token = "your-token",
#   secret = "your-secret"
# )

# Step 3: Deploy
# rsconnect::deployApp(
#   appDir = ".",
#   appName = "bibliometric-explorer",
#   appTitle = "Bibliometric Explorer"
# )

# Step 4: Test with different inputs
# - Try different journal IDs
# - Test with narrow and wide date ranges
# - Verify that caching works (second load should be instant)
library(bibliometrix)
# biblioshiny()

# Feature comparison table:
# | Feature              | biblioshiny | Custom app |
# |---------------------|-------------|------------|
# | Data sources        | WoS, Scopus | OpenAlex   |
# | Network analysis    | Yes         | Basic      |
# | Topic analysis      | Yes         | No         |
# | Custom indicators   | Limited     | Full       |
# | Parameterization    | No          | Yes        |
# | Deployment          | Local only  | shinyapps  |
# | Reproducibility     | GUI-based   | Code-based |
#
# biblioshiny is comprehensive but not easily customisable;
# a custom app trades breadth for tailored functionality.

heading: Glossary
text: Altmetrics. Metrics derived online activity (social media mentions, news coverage, policy documents) rather citations. See Chapter 26.Bibliographic coupling. Two documents bibliographically coupled share one references. Strength increases number shared references. See Chapter 16.Bibliometrics. quantitative study scholarly publications, production, dissemination, impact. subfield scientometrics focused documents.Burst detection. method identifying terms topics whose frequency increases sharply given time window, signalling emerging research fronts. See Chapter 25.Citation aging. temporal pattern citations accumulate time publication. citation half-life measures quickly paper accrues half total citations. See Chapter 14.Citation window. time period citations publication counted. Common choices: 3-year, 5-year, variable windows.Co-authorship network. graph nodes represent authors edges connect authors co-authored least one publication. See Chapter 15.Co-citation. Two documents co-cited cited third document. Frequently co-cited documents considered intellectually related. See Chapter 16.Co-word analysis. Network analysis co-occurrence terms (keywords, title words, descriptors) within documents. See Chapter 17.Community detection. Algorithmic identification densely connected subgroups within network. Common methods: Leiden, Louvain, modularity optimisation. See Chapter 18.Disparity filter. network backbone extraction method retains statistically significant edges based local weight distributions. See Chapter 18.DORA. San Francisco Declaration Research Assessment. set recommendations improving research evaluation practices, emphasising journal-based metrics used surrogates individual article quality.Field normalisation. Adjusting citation counts account differences citation practices across research fields publication years. See Chapter 13.Fractional counting. attribution method credit multi-authored publication divided equally among contributors (1/n per author institution), contrast full counting. See Chapter 13.Full counting. attribution method author institution publication receives full credit (count 1). See Chapter 13.h-index. researcher h-index h h papers cited least h times. See Chapter 10.Impact factor (JIF). mean number citations received year t articles published journal years t−1 t−2. See Chapter 11.Leiden Manifesto. Ten principles responsible research metrics, emphasising quantitative evaluation support qualitative, expert assessment rather replace . See Chapter 3.Leiden algorithm. community detection method improves Louvain guaranteeing well-connected communities. See Chapter 18.MNCS (Mean Normalised Citation Score). average field-normalised citation scores across set publications. MNCS 1.0 indicates -world-average impact. See Chapter 13.Network backbone. reduced network retaining statistically significant structurally important edges, obtained filtering methods disparity filter. See Chapter 18.OpenAlex. free, open catalogue global research system, covering works, authors, institutions, sources, concepts, funders, publishers. default data source book. See Chapter 5.Overlay map. visualisation technique subset data (e.g., one institution’s publications) projected onto pre-computed base map science. See Chapter 19.PP(top 10%). proportion entity’s publications rank top 10% cited field year. percentile-based impact indicator. See Chapter 10.Price index. proportion paper’s references older 5 years, measuring current cited literature . See Chapter 14.RAKE. Rapid Automatic Keyword Extraction. unsupervised method extracts multi-word keywords based word co-occurrence within phrases delimited stopwords. See Chapter 23.Research front. cluster recently published, actively cited papers representing current edge research area.Retraction. formal withdrawal published paper authors publisher due errors, misconduct, irreproducibility. See Chapter 29.Scientometrics. quantitative study science innovation, encompassing bibliometrics, patent analysis, measurement research systems.STM (Structural Topic Model). topic model allows document-level covariates (e.g., publication year, journal) affect topic prevalence content. See Chapter 22.TF-IDF. Term Frequency–Inverse Document Frequency. weighting scheme highlights terms distinctive individual documents within corpus. See Chapter 21.Topic modelling. Unsupervised methods discovering latent thematic structure collections text. Common approaches: LDA, STM, BERTopic. See Chapter 22.UMAP. Uniform Manifold Approximation Projection. dimensionality-reduction technique visualising high-dimensional data (e.g., document embeddings) 2D. See Chapter 24.
